title: Graph products of operator algebras
overall_summary: This paper constructs graph products of reduced and maximal C*-algebras, von Neumann algebras, and compact/discrete quantum groups, using a graph-product Fock space indexed by reduced words in the defining graph. Its most useful structural results for von Neumann algebras are the reduced-word normal form, the explicit left and right action formulas on the graph-product Hilbert space, canonical conditional expectations onto subgraph products, and the decomposition of a graph product over a vertex as an amalgamated free product over the link of that vertex. The paper also proves universal properties, subgraph intersection formulas, commutant/relative commutant results in the tracial diffuse case, and permanence of properties such as being a II1 factor and the Haagerup property. For the original problem, the paper supplies the exact reduced-word calculus and conditional expectation/amalgamated-free-product framework likely needed to analyze proper proximality of graph-product von Neumann algebras.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1.3 (normal form for graph words)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a simplicial graph. A word is a finite sequence v=(v1,...,vn) of vertices. Words are equivalent under the equivalence relation generated by two moves: deletion of adjacent equal letters, (v1,...,vi,vi+1,...,vn) ≃ (v1,...,vi,vi+2,...,vn) when vi=vi+1; and swapping adjacent linked letters, (v1,...,vi,vi+1,...,vn) ≃ (v1,...,vi+1,vi,...,vn) when vi is adjacent to vi+1 in Γ. A word v is reduced if whenever vk=vl with k>l, it is not the case that every intervening letter vk+1,...,vl−1 lies in Star(vk). Then every word is equivalent to a reduced word. If two reduced words w=(w1,...,wn) and w'=(w'1,...,w'm) are equivalent, then m=n, and there is a permutation σ of {1,...,n} with w'=(wσ(1),...,wσ(n)). Moreover, among such permutations there is a unique σ satisfying the order-preserving condition for repeated vertices: if k>l and wk=wl, then σ(k)>σ(l). The paper then fixes W_min, a complete set of representatives of reduced words modulo this equivalence, and calls its elements minimal words.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Existence of reduced representatives follows by induction: any word that cannot be shortened by using swaps to bring equal adjacent letters together and then deleting is reduced. For uniqueness of length, the authors invoke Green's normal form theorem for graph products of groups by assigning to every vertex v the group R_+ under multiplication and associating to a reduced word w the graph-product group element 2_{w1}2_{w2}...2_{wn}. Equivalent graph words give the same group element after possibly replacing factors by powers or roots of 2, and Green's normal form theorem implies that two reduced representatives have the same length. To prove the permutation statement, one compares the number of occurrences of each vertex in the two reduced representatives; if a vertex occurred fewer times in one representative, a deletion of two equal letters separated only by vertices in the relevant star would have been possible, contradicting reducedness. The final uniqueness condition is the uniqueness of the increasing bijection between the positions of each repeated vertex in w and its positions in w'.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2.1 (graph product Hilbert space construction)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For each vertex v of Γ, let (H_v,ξ_v) be a pointed Hilbert space with ||ξ_v||=1, and put H_v^∘=H_v⊖Cξ_v. For a reduced word w=(w1,...,wn), set H_w=H_{w1}^∘⊗...⊗H_{wn}^∘. If v and w are equivalent reduced words, Lemma 1.3 gives a unique tensor-factor permutation, and the paper defines a unitary Q_{v,w}:H_v→H_w by permuting tensor factors accordingly. The graph product Hilbert space is H=CΩ ⊕ ⊕_{w∈W_min} H_w. For a vertex v, let W_v be the set of minimal reduced words w such that the concatenation vw is still reduced, and set H(v)=CΩ ⊕ ⊕_{w∈W_v} H_w. The map U_v:H_v⊗H(v)→H is defined by U_v(ξ_v⊗Ω)=Ω, U_v(H_v^∘⊗Ω)=H_v^∘, U_v(ξ_v⊗H_w)=H_w, and U_v(H_v^∘⊗H_w)=Q_{vw}(H_v^∘⊗H_w). This U_v is unitary, and it gives a faithful unital normal representation λ_v:B(H_v)→B(H) by λ_v(x)=U_v(x⊗1)U_v^*.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The only nontrivial point in the construction is surjectivity of U_v. The paper observes that for any reduced word w, the concatenation vw fails to be reduced if and only if w is equivalent, by type-II moves only, to a reduced word beginning with v. Thus every summand in H either belongs to H(v), is obtained by adding a first v-letter, or is represented by the Ω and one-letter cases. The unitaries Q_{v,w} are well defined because Lemma 1.3 supplies a unique permutation once the order of equal vertices is fixed. Faithfulness and normality of λ_v are immediate because U_v is unitary and x↦x⊗1 is faithful and normal.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.1 (left action formulas and edge commutation)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: With the graph product Hilbert space H and λ_v:B(H_v)→B(H) as above, let P_v be the projection H_v→H_v^∘ and let ξ_v^*:H_v→C be η↦⟨η,ξ_v⟩. For x∈B(H_v): (1) λ_v(x)Ω=P_v(xξ_v)+⟨xξ_v,ξ_v⟩Ω. (2) If ξ∈H_v^∘, then λ_v(x)ξ=P_v(xξ)+⟨xξ,ξ_v⟩Ω. (3) If w∈W_v and ξ∈H_w, then λ_v(x)ξ=Q_{vw}(P_v(xξ_v)⊗ξ)+⟨xξ_v,ξ_v⟩ξ. (4) If w∉W_v, then there is a unique w_v∈W_v such that w and v w_v are type-II equivalent, and for ξ∈H_w one has λ_v(x)ξ=Q_{v w_v}(P_v x⊗id)Q_{v w_v}^*ξ + (ξ_v^* x⊗id)Q_{v w_v}^*ξ. Moreover, if v and v' are adjacent vertices in Γ, then the images of λ_v:B(H_v)→B(H) and λ_{v'}:B(H_{v'})→B(H) commute.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The four formulas follow directly by applying U_v(x⊗1)U_v^* to the four possible types of summands: the vacuum, the one-letter v summand, a summand whose minimal word remains reduced after adding v on the left, and a summand whose word can be written after type-II moves as v followed by a reduced word. In the last case, Q_{v w_v}^* identifies H_w with H_v^∘⊗H_{w_v}, so x either keeps the first component in H_v^∘, producing the P_v x term, or collapses it to the vacuum component, producing the ξ_v^* x term. For commutation along an edge, the proof first subtracts scalar parts of x and y so that xξ_v and yξ_{v'} are orthogonal to the vacuum vectors. It then checks equality on Ω and on each reduced-word summand. The required identities are exactly the tensor-factor flip identities allowed by the edge (v,v')∈EΓ, plus a detailed case analysis according to whether a minimal word accepts v and/or v' on the left.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 2.2 and 2.3 (right actions and left-right commutation)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: There is a right-handed analogue of the construction. Let W_v^r be the set of minimal reduced words w such that wv is reduced, and set H'(v)=CΩ ⊕ ⊕_{w∈W_v^r}H_w. The unitary U'_v:H'(v)⊗H_v→H gives a faithful unital normal representation ρ_v:B(H_v)→B(H) by ρ_v(x)=U'_v(1⊗x)(U'_v)^*. For x∈B(H_v), ρ_v satisfies the analogous right formulas: ρ_v(x)Ω=P_v(xξ_v)+⟨xξ_v,ξ_v⟩Ω; for ξ∈H_v^∘, ρ_v(x)ξ=P_v(xξ)+⟨xξ,ξ_v⟩Ω; for w∈W_v^r and ξ∈H_w, ρ_v(x)ξ=Q_{wv}(ξ⊗P_v(xξ_v))+⟨xξ_v,ξ_v⟩ξ; and if w∉W_v^r, there is a unique w'_v∈W_v^r with w≃w'_v v, and ρ_v(x)ξ=Q_{w'_v v}(id⊗P_v x)Q_{w'_v v}^*ξ + (id⊗ξ_v^*x)Q_{w'_v v}^*ξ. The images of ρ_v and ρ_{v'} commute whenever v and v' are adjacent. In addition, Proposition 2.3 states that λ_v(x)ρ_{v'}(y)=ρ_{v'}(y)λ_v(x) whenever v≠v', and also when v=v' provided xy=yx in B(H_v).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof of the right formulas is the mirror image of Proposition 2.1, using concatenation on the right rather than on the left. For the left-right commutation result, the authors again reduce to the case where the scalar vacuum coefficients of x and y vanish. They verify equality on Ω and then on each reduced-word summand. The proof is a careful four-case decomposition according to whether a minimal word can accept v on the left and v' on the right. If adding both letters remains reduced, the conclusion follows from the canonical tensor permutation Q. If adding letters causes cancellation, the only nontrivial same-vertex case is controlled by the assumption xy=yx. All other cases are handled by the same type-II commutation calculus used in Proposition 2.1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 2.19, Proposition 2.20, and Remark 2.21 (graph product von Neumann algebra, modular theory, and commutant)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (M_v,ω_v) be von Neumann algebras with faithful normal states, represented on their GNS Hilbert spaces (H_v,ξ_v). The graph product von Neumann algebra is M_Γ=(∪_{v∈VΓ}λ_v(M_v))''⊂B(H), where H is the graph product Hilbert space. A reduced operator is a product a=a1...an with ai∈M_{vi}^∘:=ker(ω_{vi}) and (v1,...,vn) a reduced word; the linear span of 1 and all reduced operators is weakly dense in M_Γ. The graph product state is ω_Γ(x)=⟨xΩ,Ω⟩; it is faithful, and Ω is cyclic and separating for M_Γ. If J_v, ∇_v, and σ_t^v are the modular objects of (M_v,ω_v), then the modular objects of (M_Γ,ω_Γ) are obtained by taking direct sums over reduced-word tensor products, reversing tensor order by the canonical permutation Σ_w, and applying the vertex modular objects. In particular, for every reduced operator a1...an with ai∈M_{vi} one has σ_t(a1...an)=σ_t^{v1}(a1)...σ_t^{vn}(an). The right graph product algebra M_Γ^r is generated by the right representations ρ_v^Γ(a):=ρ_v(J_v a^* J_v), and Remark 2.21 states that M_Γ'=M_Γ^r; more explicitly, for a reduced operator a=a1...an, J a J=ρ_{v1}^Γ(a1)...ρ_{vn}^Γ(an).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The density and formula aΩ=â1⊗...⊗ân for reduced a follow from the explicit λ_v action formulas. The right representations commute with the left algebra by Proposition 2.3, so M_Γ^r⊂M_Γ'. Since reduced right products send Ω to reversed reduced tensors, Ω is cyclic for M_Γ^r, hence separating for M_Γ; faithfulness of ω_Γ follows. For modular theory, define the Tomita operator on the dense subspace spanned by Ω and reduced vectors. On a reduced vector â1⊗...⊗ân it acts by applying the vertex Tomita operators and reversing tensor order according to the unique reduced representative of the reversed word. The polar decomposition gives the stated J and ∇ formulas, and the modular automorphism formula follows by applying ∇^{it} to each tensor factor. The equality M_Γ'=M_Γ^r follows from J M_Γ J=M_Γ^r together with Tomita-Takesaki theory.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.22 (universal property of graph product von Neumann algebras)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (M_v,ω_v) be von Neumann algebras with faithful normal states and let (M_Γ,ω_Γ) be their graph product. Suppose N is a von Neumann algebra with a GNS-faithful normal state ω, and for every v∈VΓ there is a unital normal faithful *-homomorphism π_v:M_v→N such that: (i) N is generated as a von Neumann algebra by ∪_v π_v(M_v); (ii) the images π_v(M_v) and π_{v'}(M_{v'}) commute whenever v and v' are adjacent in Γ; and (iii) for every reduced word (v1,...,vn) and every ai∈M_{vi}^∘, one has ω(π_{v1}(a1)...π_{vn}(an))=0. Then there exists a unique normal *-isomorphism π:M_Γ→N such that π|_{M_v}=π_v for all v, and π intertwines ω_Γ and ω. In particular, ω is faithful.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is the von Neumann version of the C*-algebraic Proposition 2.12. Put N in the GNS representation of ω with cyclic vector η. Define V:H→L^2(N,ω) by VΩ=η and V(a1...an Ω)=π_{v1}(a1)...π_{vn}(an)η for reduced products with ai centered. The reduced-word vacuum-orthogonality assumption makes V isometric, and generation makes its range dense, so V is unitary. Conjugating by V gives the desired spatial isomorphism. Normality follows because the isomorphism is implemented on GNS Hilbert spaces, and uniqueness follows from generation by the vertex algebras.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remarks 2.23 and 2.24 (standard identifications, subgraph inclusions, and conditional expectations)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The paper records several consequences of the universal property. If M_v=L(G_v) are group von Neumann algebras with canonical traces, then the graph product von Neumann algebra is (M_Γ,ω_Γ)=(L(G_Γ),τ), where G_Γ is the graph product of the groups. If Γ is a complete graph, then (M_Γ,ω_Γ)=⊗_{v∈VΓ}(M_v,ω_v). If Γ has no edges, then (M_Γ,ω_Γ)=∗_{v∈VΓ}(M_v,ω_v). If Γ0⊂Γ is a full subgraph and N_v⊂M_v are unital von Neumann subalgebras for v∈VΓ0, then the graph product N_{Γ0} is canonically identified with (∪_{v∈VΓ0}N_v)''⊂M_Γ. For each vertex v, there is a state-preserving isomorphism M_{Star(v)}≃M_v⊗M_{Link(v)} identifying x⊗y with xy; consequently M_v'∩M_{Star(v)}=N_{Star(v)}, where N_w=M_w for w∈Link(v) and N_v=Z(M_v). Finally, for every full subgraph Γ0⊂Γ, there is a unique normal state-preserving conditional expectation E_{Γ0}:M_Γ→M_{Γ0} such that E_{Γ0}(a)=0 for every reduced operator a whose associated reduced word contains at least one vertex outside Γ0. In particular, for each vertex v there is E_v:M_Γ→M_v killing all reduced operators not contained in M_v.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The group, complete-graph, no-edge, and subalgebra identifications are direct applications of Proposition 2.22: the relevant candidate algebras are generated by the vertex images, satisfy exactly the required commutation relations, and have the same vanishing rule on centered reduced products. The Star(v) formula uses the fact that v is adjacent to every vertex in Link(v), so M_v commutes with M_{Link(v)}, while the graph product over Star(v) is generated by these two commuting subalgebras. The relative commutant inside M_v⊗M_{Link(v)} is Z(M_v)⊗M_{Link(v)}, expressed again as a graph product over Star(v). For E_{Γ0}, the construction is compression by the orthogonal projection from the graph-product Hilbert space H onto the sub-Fock space CΩ⊕⊕_{w∈W_min, vertices(w)⊂Γ0}H_w; the reduced-word formula shows exactly which reduced products survive.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.25 (intersection of subgraph von Neumann algebras)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ0 and Γ1 be full subgraphs of Γ. Inside the graph product von Neumann algebra M_Γ, one has M_{Γ0}∩M_{Γ1}=M_{Γ0∩Γ1}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The inclusion M_{Γ0∩Γ1}⊂M_{Γ0}∩M_{Γ1} is immediate. For the reverse inclusion, first consider the algebraic span M_{Γ0}^{alg} of 1 and reduced operators supported in Γ0. If x∈M_{Γ0}^{alg}∩M_{Γ1}, write x=ω_Γ(x)1+Σ_i x_i with x_i reduced and supported in Γ0. Applying the conditional expectation E_{Γ1} gives x=E_{Γ1}(x)=ω_Γ(x)1+Σ_i E_{Γ1}(x_i), and each E_{Γ1}(x_i) is either zero or the same reduced operator with all vertices in Γ0∩Γ1. Thus x∈M_{Γ0∩Γ1}. Passing to weak closures gives the result for von Neumann algebras.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorems 2.15 and 2.26 (unscrewing as an amalgamated free product over the link)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a vertex v∈VΓ. Let Γ1=Star(v), Γ2=Γ\{v}, and let N=M_{Link(v)}, M1=M_{Γ1}, M2=M_{Γ2} inside M_Γ, with their canonical normal conditional expectations onto N. Then the graph product von Neumann algebra decomposes as M_Γ≃M1 ∗_N M2, the von Neumann algebraic reduced amalgamated free product over N, and the isomorphism restricts to the canonical inclusions on M1 and M2 and preserves the graph product state. Theorem 2.15 gives the analogous statement for reduced graph product C*-algebras A_Γ: with A1=A_{Star(v)}, A2=A_{Γ\{v}}, and B=A_{Link(v)}, one has A_Γ≃A1 ∗_B A2 with respect to the canonical GNS-faithful expectations.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses the universal property of the amalgamated free product. Since M_Γ is generated by M1 and M2, it suffices to prove freeness over N: if a1,...,an are alternating elements with ak∈ker(E_{lk})⊂M_{lk}, lk∈{1,2}, and lk≠lk+1, then E_N(a1...an)=0. By density, each ak can be taken to be a reduced operator whose associated reduced word lies in Γ_lk and contains at least one vertex outside Link(v). Let the concatenated word for a=a1...an have total length l. The proof is by induction on l. If the concatenated word is reduced, then it contains a vertex outside Link(v), so E_N(a)=0. If it is not reduced, then after type-II moves and using that each individual block is reduced, a cancellation must occur across the boundary between two adjacent alternating blocks. Such a repeated vertex must lie in Γ1∩Γ2=Link(v). Writing the product of the two adjacent centered letters as its centered part plus its scalar expectation splits E_N(a) into two terms with smaller total length; both vanish by induction. This proves the amalgamated freeness and hence the claimed isomorphism.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.27 (finite right-dimension bimodules and normalizers in the tracial diffuse case)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume that every vertex state ω_v is a trace. Fix v∈VΓ, and let Q⊂M_v be a diffuse von Neumann subalgebra. Then Q does not intertwine into M_{Link(v)} inside M_{Star(v)} in Popa's sense: Q ⊀_{M_{Star(v)}} M_{Link(v)}. Moreover, every Q-M_{Star(v)} sub-bimodule of L^2(M_Γ) that has finite dimension as a right M_{Star(v)}-module is contained in L^2(M_{Star(v)}). In particular, Q'∩M_Γ⊂N_{M_Γ}(Q)''⊂M_{Star(v)}, where N_{M_Γ}(Q) is the unitary normalizer of Q in M_Γ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Using the unscrewing theorem, M_Γ=M_{Star(v)} ∗_{M_{Link(v)}} M_{Γ\{v}}. Since M_{Star(v)}≃M_v⊗M_{Link(v)}, the diffuseness of Q⊂M_v gives Q ⊀_{M_{Star(v)}} M_{Link(v)}. The authors then apply [IPP08, Theorem 1.1] to this amalgamated free product. That theorem implies that finite right M_{Star(v)}-dimensional Q-M_{Star(v)} sub-bimodules of L^2(M_Γ) must lie in the base part L^2(M_{Star(v)}), and yields the inclusion of the relative commutant and normalizer algebra into M_{Star(v)}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollaries 2.28 and 2.29 (relative commutants of diffuse vertices and factoriality of graph products of II1 factors)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Assume all vertex states are traces. Fix v∈VΓ, and define N_w=M_w for w∈Link(v) and N_v=Z(M_v). If M_v is diffuse, then M_v'∩M_Γ=N_{Star(v)}, the graph product over Star(v) of these algebras N_w. Consequently, Z(M_Γ)=∩_{v∈VΓ}N_{Star(v)} under the same diffuseness hypotheses where applied. In particular, if every M_v is a II1 factor, then M_Γ is a II1 factor.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The inclusion N_{Star(v)}⊂M_v'∩M_Γ is immediate: elements from link vertices commute with M_v, and the v-coordinate contribution must lie in Z(M_v). For the reverse inclusion when M_v is diffuse, Lemma 2.27 with Q=M_v gives M_v'∩M_Γ⊂M_{Star(v)}. Inside M_{Star(v)}≃M_v⊗M_{Link(v)}, the relative commutant is exactly Z(M_v)⊗M_{Link(v)}, i.e. N_{Star(v)}. For factoriality, by an inductive-limit reduction the graph may be assumed finite. If all M_v are II1 factors, then Z(M_v)=C, so the center lies in ∩_v M_{Link(v)}. Proposition 2.25 identifies this intersection with M_{∩_v Link(v)}. Since ∩_v Link(v)⊂∩_v(Γ\{v})=∅, the intersection algebra is C, so M_Γ has trivial center.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.30 (graph product of state-preserving ucp maps)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (M_v,ω_v) and (N_v,μ_v), v∈VΓ, be two families of von Neumann algebras with faithful normal states. For each v, let φ_v:M_v→N_v be a state-preserving normal unital completely positive map. Then there exists a unique normal ucp map φ:M_Γ→N_Γ such that, for every reduced operator a1...an∈M_Γ with ai∈M_{vi}^∘, φ(a1...an)=φ_{v1}(a1)...φ_{vn}(an). The map φ preserves the graph product states. Its L^2-extension T_φ is the direct sum over minimal reduced words w=(v1,...,vn) of the tensor products T_{φ_{v1}}|_{L^2(M_{v1})^∘}⊗...⊗T_{φ_{vn}}|_{L^2(M_{vn})^∘}, together with the identity on the vacuum line CΩ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each φ_v, take its GNS/Stinespring pointed M_v-N_v bimodule (K_v,η_v), where ⟨aη_v b,η_v⟩=μ_v(φ_v(a)b). Form the graph product Hilbert space K of the pointed spaces (K_v,η_v). The left M_v-actions and right N_v-actions embed into B(K) via λ_v and ρ_v, and Proposition 2.3 ensures that the total left and right graph product actions commute. The vector η is cyclic enough for both sides, and the universal property identifies these actions with M_Γ and N_Γ. Define φ(x)=V^* x V via the isometry V:L^2(N_Γ)→K, V(ξ)=η·ξ. Computing on reduced words gives the stated formula. The L^2-extension formula follows because each reduced tensor component is acted on independently by the corresponding vertex Stinespring/L^2 map.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 2.33 (Haagerup property permanence)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M_Γ be the graph product von Neumann algebra of vertex algebras (M_v,ω_v) with faithful normal states. Then M_Γ has the Haagerup property if and only if every M_v has the Haagerup property.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof first reduces to finite Γ by the standard inductive-limit argument used in the paper. If every M_v has the Haagerup property, choose state-preserving ucp maps φ_{v,i}:M_v→M_v with compact L^2-implementations T_{v,i} converging strongly to the identity; using a standard adjustment, assume ||T_{v,i}|| on the orthogonal complement of the vacuum vector is strictly less than 1. Proposition 2.30 forms graph product ucp maps φ_i. Because Γ is finite and all non-vacuum tensor factors are contracted while each vertex implementation is compact, the L^2-implementation of φ_i is compact. It converges strongly to the identity on reduced words and hence on all of L^2(M_Γ). Conversely, if M_Γ has the Haagerup property, the conditional expectation M_Γ→M_v passes the property to each vertex algebra.

proof_techniques.name: Reduced-word Fock-space bookkeeping
proof_techniques.description: The paper realizes the graph product on a Fock-type Hilbert space whose orthogonal summands are indexed by minimal reduced words in the graph. The key bookkeeping device is the unitary Q_{v,w} associated with two equivalent reduced words, which permutes tensor factors according to the unique permutation from the normal form lemma. This lets one treat graph commutation relations as literal tensor permutations and graph cancellations as vacuum contractions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A proof of proper proximality will likely need to estimate or identify matrix coefficients of left and right multiplication by reduced words. The Fock-space model gives an explicit orthogonal decomposition on which trace-zero vertex unitaries act as reduced-word creation, annihilation, or permutation operators.

proof_techniques.name: Creation-annihilation formulas for λ_v and ρ_v
proof_techniques.description: Propositions 2.1 and 2.2 give exact formulas for how a vertex operator acts on a reduced-word summand: it either creates a new tensor factor, preserves a word after applying the scalar part, annihilates a matching first or last vertex to a shorter word, or permutes through linked vertices before doing one of these. These formulas encode all graph-product relations at the Hilbert-space level.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Proper proximality arguments often require showing that certain bimodule coefficients vanish or that certain left-right actions are compact modulo a boundary. These formulas are the basic computational input for such estimates, especially when the chosen vertex elements are trace-zero unitaries.

proof_techniques.name: Subgraph conditional expectations as Fock projections
proof_techniques.description: For every full subgraph Γ0⊂Γ, the paper constructs a canonical normal state-preserving conditional expectation E_{Γ0}:M_Γ→M_{Γ0}. It is simply compression to the closed span of reduced-word summands using only vertices from Γ0, and it kills every reduced operator whose word contains a vertex outside Γ0.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Expectations onto stars, links, complements, and intersections are natural tools for decomposing M and proving mixing relative to subalgebras. They can be used to isolate which reduced words survive under projections to candidate boundary or parabolic subalgebras.

proof_techniques.name: Unscrewing a graph product into an amalgamated free product
proof_techniques.description: Fixing a vertex v, the paper writes M_Γ as M_{Star(v)} ∗_{M_{Link(v)}} M_{Γ\{v}}. The proof is purely reduced-word induction: alternating products centered over M_{Link(v)} either remain reduced and hence have zero expectation, or admit a cancellation across an alternating boundary at a vertex in Link(v), reducing the total word length.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This provides an induction-on-vertices strategy and connects graph products to known structural results for amalgamated free products. If proper proximality is known to be stable under suitable AFP constructions, or if one can prove relative proper proximality over link algebras, this is the paper's main bridge.

proof_techniques.name: Intersection control for subgraph algebras
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 2.25 proves M_{Γ0}∩M_{Γ1}=M_{Γ0∩Γ1} by applying the conditional expectation onto Γ1 to algebraic reduced sums supported in Γ0. This converts algebraic support of reduced words directly into von Neumann algebra intersections.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Irreducibility of the graph is a combinatorial condition, and a proof may need to translate intersections of stars, links, or complements into algebraic intersections of von Neumann subalgebras. This proposition is the exact translation tool.

proof_techniques.name: Left-right graph product and commutant identification
proof_techniques.description: The paper builds a right graph product representation using the modular conjugations of the vertex states and proves that it is the commutant of the left graph product algebra. Thus M_Γ' is explicitly generated by right actions indexed by the same graph, and J sends reduced left products to corresponding reduced right products.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Definitions and criteria for proper proximality of finite von Neumann algebras often involve the left-right action of M⊗M^op or states on C*-algebras generated by these actions. The explicit commutant model and left-right commutation formulas make such constructions concrete.

proof_techniques.name: Trace and modular reduction via vertex modular data
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 2.20 computes the modular group of the graph product state word-by-word from the vertex modular groups. In particular, if all vertex states are traces, the graph product state is a trace because every vertex modular group is trivial.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem is tracial. This result justifies using the graph product state as the canonical trace and allows all L^2-computations to be performed in the tracial setting without further modular complications.

proof_techniques.name: Graph product of ucp maps through Stinespring bimodules
proof_techniques.description: State-preserving ucp maps on the vertices can be combined into a graph product ucp map whose action on reduced products is tensorwise. The proof constructs graph products of the Stinespring bimodules and uses the left-right commutation theorem to define the global correspondence.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a proof of proper proximality uses deformations, approximating maps, or boundary maps constructed vertexwise, Proposition 2.30 supplies a canonical way to extend those maps to the full graph product while preserving reduced-word structure.

proof_techniques.name: Normalizer localization via AFP rigidity
proof_techniques.description: In the tracial case, Lemma 2.27 applies Ioana-Peterson-Popa's theorem to the unscrewed decomposition M_Γ=M_{Star(v)}∗_{M_{Link(v)}}M_{Γ\{v}}. For a diffuse Q⊂M_v, any finite right-dimensional Q-M_{Star(v)} sub-bimodule of L^2(M_Γ) lies in L^2(M_{Star(v)}), forcing Q'∩M_Γ and the normalizer algebra into M_{Star(v)}.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When vertex algebras contain diffuse subalgebras, this technique can localize centralizers or normalizers to star subalgebras. Such localization may help rule out invariant states or amenable pieces in a proper proximality argument.

other_useful_info: The paper itself does not discuss proper proximality. It does prove that graph products with tracial vertex states are tracial via the modular formula, and it identifies the group case as L(G_Γ) when M_v=L(G_v). Remark 2.18 states the C*-level analogue that if Γ has connected components Γ1,...,Γn, the graph product C*-algebra decomposes as the reduced free product of the component graph products; the same philosophy is relevant for von Neumann graph products with no edges, where the product is a free product. Corollary 2.17 proves exactness of reduced graph product C*-algebras is equivalent to exactness of all vertex C*-algebras, though this is not used elsewhere in the paper's von Neumann arguments. Lemma 2.27 relies on [IPP08, Theorem 1.1], which may be a useful external reference for rigidity or intertwining arguments after applying the unscrewing decomposition.
why_relevant: Introduces graph products of C*-algebras and von Neumann algebras, including the Fock-space/reduced-word construction, conditional expectations, and basic permanence properties. These definitions and reduced-word calculations are indispensable for any proof about graph-product von Neumann algebras.

title: Properly Proximal Groups and Their Von Neumann Algebras
overall_summary: The paper introduces properly proximal groups, a class defined through finitely many compact Γ-actions whose proximal directions cover the Stone-Cech boundary ∂Γ and whose actions admit no invariant probability measures. The core technical framework is the use of boundary pieces X⊂∂Γ, equivalently the absence of left Γ-invariant states on the right-invariant boundary algebra C(X)^{Γ_r}, together with u.c.p. maps from compact dynamical systems or representation-theoretic arrays into these boundary algebras. The main von Neumann algebra application is a central-state obstruction: weak compactness of an inclusion P⊂Q⋊Γ plus P not intertwining into Q produces a left invariant state on C(∂Γ)^{Γ_r}, contradicting proper proximality. For the original graph-product von Neumann algebra problem, the most relevant contributions are the boundary-piece/proximality formalism, the equivalence between proper proximality and nonexistence of invariant boundary states, and the way trace/central states are converted into forbidden invariant states at infinity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 2.2 (weak compactness)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (Q,τ) be a tracial von Neumann algebra and let σ:Γ↷(Q,τ) be a trace-preserving action of a discrete group Γ. The action is weakly compact if there is a state φ on B(L²(Q)) such that φ|_Q=τ and φ∘Ad(u)=φ for every u in U(Q)∪σ(Γ). If Q⊂M is a regular inclusion of tracial von Neumann algebras, then Q⊂M is weakly compact if the action of the normalizer N_M(Q)={u∈U(M):uQu*=Q} on Q is weakly compact.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a definition. Its role in the paper is that weak compactness supplies a state on a large operator algebra which is tracial on the relevant von Neumann algebra and invariant under both the subalgebra and its normalizer; this state is the input for the central-state obstruction in Theorem 4.16.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 2.3 and Lemma 2.4 (Popa intertwining and central-state criterion)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a tracial von Neumann algebra and let P,Q⊂M be von Neumann subalgebras. One writes P≺_M Q, meaning that a corner of P embeds into Q inside M, if equivalently: (1) there are nonzero projections p∈P and q∈Q, a ∗-homomorphism φ:pPp→qQq, and a nonzero v∈qMp such that φ(x)v=vx for all x∈pPp; or (2) there is no net of unitaries u_i∈U(P) such that ∥E_Q(xu_i y)∥_2→0 for all x,y∈M. Lemma 2.4 states that P≺_M Q if and only if there is a P-central state Φ on the basic construction ⟨M,e_Q⟩ which is normal on M and does not vanish on Me_QM.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equivalence in Definition 2.3 is Popa's intertwining-by-bimodules theorem. Lemma 2.4 is the state formulation used in the paper: a nonzero P-central normal-on-M state on the basic construction detects an embedding of P into Q, while if P does not embed, any P-central state normal on M must vanish on the ideal Me_QM. In Theorem 4.16 this is applied with M=Q⋊Γ, where the basic construction over Q identifies with an algebra containing Q⊗K(ℓ²Γ); non-intertwining forces the weak-compactness state to vanish on this compact ideal.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 3.1 and Notation 3.2 (boundary pieces and their ideals)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a discrete group Γ, write ΔΓ for the Stone-Cech compactification and ∂Γ=ΔΓ\Γ. A boundary piece is a closed subset X⊂∂Γ invariant under both the left and right Γ-actions. For such X, I_0(X)⊂ℓ∞(Γ)≅C(ΔΓ) is the ideal of functions vanishing on X. The projection p_X∈ℓ∞(Γ)^{∗∗} is the support projection of I_0(X), and q_X=1-p_X is the indicator projection 1_X. Since X is left and right Γ-invariant, q_X is invariant under both λ_g and ρ_g in B(ℓ²Γ)^{∗∗}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is definitional. The key point is that a boundary piece packages a collection of directions at infinity stable under left and right multiplication, so that the corresponding quotient C(X)=ℓ∞(Γ)/I_0(X) carries commuting left and right Γ-actions. The projection q_X is later used to localize states to the piece X, especially in the relative bi-exactness/solidity argument.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definitions 3.4-3.5 and Lemma 3.6 (compact operators toward a boundary piece)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a discrete group, X⊂∂Γ closed, and let P_U be the projection ℓ²Γ→ℓ²(Γ∩U) for U⊂ΔΓ. A bounded net ξ_i∈ℓ²Γ has positive mass on X if there is ε>0 such that for every neighborhood U of X in ΔΓ, ∥P_Uξ_i∥>ε eventually. It has full mass on X if for every neighborhood U of X, limsup_i ∥ξ_i-P_Uξ_i∥=0. An operator T∈B(ℓ²Γ) is compact toward X if Tξ_i→0 for every bounded net with full mass on X; the set is denoted K(Γ;X). Let A_Γ=C∗(ℓ∞(Γ),λ(Γ),ρ(Γ)) and let I(X)⊂A_Γ be the ideal generated by I_0(X). Lemma 3.6 states that every approximate unit of I_0(X) is an approximate unit for I(X), and I(X)=A_Γ∩K(Γ;X).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Because X is invariant under both Γ-actions, λ_g I_0(X) λ_g* and ρ_h I_0(X) ρ_h* remain in I_0(X). Thus I(X) is the norm closure of the span of elements fλ_gρ_h, equivalently λ_gρ_h f, with f∈I_0(X). Hence an approximate unit for I_0(X) approximates all generators of I(X). For the equality with compact-toward-X operators, one inclusion is immediate since functions vanishing near X kill nets with full mass on X. Conversely, for T∈A_Γ∩K(Γ;X), take neighborhoods U of X and functions e_U∈C(ΔΓ) with e_U=0 on X and e_U=1 on ΔΓ\U. Then (1-e_U)ξ_U has full mass on X uniformly over unit vectors ξ_U, so compactness toward X gives ∥T(1-e_U)∥→0 and hence Te_U→T; since e_U∈I_0(X), T∈I(X).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 3.7 and Lemmas 3.8-3.10 (proximal pieces from compact actions)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ act continuously on a compact space K and let η∈Prob(K). A point ω∈ΔΓ is η-proximal if for every h∈Γ one has lim_{g→ω}((gh)η-gη)=0 in the weak-* topology on Prob(K). The set of η-proximal elements is denoted ∂_ηΓ. Lemma 3.8 states that ∂_ηΓ is closed and left/right Γ-invariant, and that ∂_ηΓ=ΔΓ if η is Γ-invariant while ∂_ηΓ⊂∂Γ if η is not Γ-invariant. Lemma 3.9 states that if η is not Γ-invariant and X=∂_ηΓ, then there is a unital completely positive map θ:C(K)→C(X) satisfying θ(g·f)=λ_g(θ(f)) and ρ_g(θ(f))=θ(f) for all g∈Γ and f∈C(K). Lemma 3.10 states that if K has no Γ-invariant probability measure, then C(X)^{Γ_r} has no left Γ-invariant state; and if Γ↷K is topologically amenable, then the left Γ-action on C(X)^{Γ_r} is amenable.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 3.8, extend the orbit map g↦gη from Γ to a continuous map σ:ΔΓ→Prob(K). Then ∂_ηΓ=⋂_{h∈Γ}{ω:σ(ωh)=σ(ω)}, proving closedness and right invariance; left invariance follows from σ(gω)=gσ(ω). If η is invariant, all points are proximal; if not, no finite point of Γ can satisfy the proximality identities. For Lemma 3.9, define θ_0(f)(g)=∫_K f d(gη), a u.c.p. map C(K)→ℓ∞Γ, and let θ be its restriction to X. The identities follow from θ(h·f)(ω)=lim_{g→ω}θ_0(f)(h^{-1}g) and θ(f)(ωh)=lim_{g→ω}∫f d((gh)η)=lim_{g→ω}∫f d(gη), the latter being exactly proximality. For Lemma 3.10, θ lands in C(X)^{Γ_r}; a left invariant state on this algebra would compose with θ to give a Γ-invariant state on C(K), i.e. an invariant probability measure on K. For amenability, θ induces a Γ-equivariant continuous map from the spectrum of C(X)^{Γ_r} to Prob(K), and amenability of Γ↷K passes through this map.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.11 (every boundary piece is a proximal piece)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every boundary piece X⊂∂Γ there exists a continuous action Γ↷K on a compact space K and a measure η∈Prob(K) such that X=∂_ηΓ. Moreover the action can be chosen so that: if there is no left Γ-invariant state on C(X)^{Γ_r}, then K has no Γ-invariant probability measure; and if the left Γ-action on C(X)^{Γ_r} is amenable, then Γ↷K is topologically amenable.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let φ:ℓ∞Γ≅C(ΔΓ)→C(X) be restriction, let A=C(X)^{Γ_r}, and set B=φ^{-1}(A)⊂ℓ∞Γ. The algebra B is Γ×Γ-invariant; by Gelfand duality B=C(K) for a compact Γ×Γ-space K and the inclusion B⊂ℓ∞Γ corresponds to a Γ×Γ-equivariant map π:ΔΓ→K. This map is a homeomorphism on ΔΓ\X and satisfies π(ωh)=π(ω) for all ω∈X and h∈Γ. Take η=δ_{π(e)} and consider the left Γ-action. If ω∈X, then lim_{g→ω}(gh)η=δ_{π(ωh)}=δ_{π(ω)}=lim_{g→ω}gη, so X⊂∂_ηΓ. If ω∈∂Γ\X, choose h with ωh≠ω; since π is injective off X, π(ωh)≠π(ω), so ω is not proximal. The additional properties are proved by analyzing invariant measures on K through their mass on π(ΔΓ\X), and by using the exact sequence 0→I_0(X)→B→A→0 together with amenability/nuclearity of the reduced crossed products.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 3.12 and Lemma 3.13 (boundary pieces from two-sided arrays)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let π:Γ→U(H) be a unitary representation. A two-sided array into π is a map b:Γ→H such that sup_{g∈Γ} ∥b(sgt)-π(s)b(g)∥<∞ for every s,t∈Γ. If b is unbounded, then X={ω∈ΔΓ:lim_{g→ω}∥b(g)∥=+∞} is a nonempty boundary piece. Moreover there is a u.c.p. map θ:B(H)→C(X) such that θ∘Ad(π(g))=λ_g∘θ and ρ_g∘θ=θ for every g∈Γ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The set X is closed, nonempty by unboundedness, contained in ∂Γ, and left/right invariant because the array relation changes b(g) by a uniformly bounded amount under left/right translation, up to π(s). For T∈B(H), set ξ_g=b(g)/∥b(g)∥ when ∥b(g)∥≠0 and define θ_0(T)(g)=⟨Tξ_g,ξ_g⟩; restricting θ_0 to X gives θ. Along ω∈X, the bounded-error array relation divided by ∥b(g)∥ gives ∥π(s)ξ_g-ξ_{sgt}∥→0, which yields both the left equivariance and right invariance formulas for θ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.16 (weak mixing/compact dichotomy through a mixing boundary piece)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let π:Γ→U(H) be a representation and let X(π)⊂∂Γ be the mixing piece, defined as the inverse image of 0 under the continuous extension ΔΓ→(B(H))_1 of π with the weak operator topology. Let B⊂LΓ be a von Neumann subalgebra and let G⊂U(B) be a group generating B. Then the following are equivalent: (i) some net of unitaries u_i∈G has full mass on X(π), viewed as vectors in ℓ²Γ; (ii) the LΓ-bimodule H⊗overline H⊗ℓ²Γ associated with π⊗overlineπ has no nonzero B-central vector.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses Lemma 3.15, which characterizes full mass on X(π) for uniformly bounded nets c_i∈LΓ by vanishing of matrix coefficients ⟨c_i ξ d_i,η⟩ in all bimodules built from π, and equivalently by vanishing of special positive coefficients in H⊗overline H. If u_i has full mass and ξ is B-central in H⊗overline H⊗ℓ²Γ, applying the coefficient-vanishing criterion with c_i=u_i contradicts centrality unless ξ=0. Conversely, if no net in G has full mass, the negation of Lemma 3.15 gives ε>0 and finitely many positive test vectors ξ_j⊗overline ξ_j, η_j⊗overline η_j such that a uniform positive coefficient lower bound holds for every u∈G. Taking the unique minimal-norm vector in the closed convex hull of {u(ξ⊗δ_e)u*:u∈G} produces a nonzero vector fixed by conjugation by G, hence B-central.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 4.1 and Remark 4.2 (properly proximal groups and noncommutative reformulation)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A discrete group Γ is properly proximal if there are finitely many continuous actions Γ↷K_i on compact spaces and measures η_i∈Prob(K_i) such that no K_i admits a Γ-invariant Borel probability measure and ∂Γ=⋃_i ∂_{η_i}Γ. Remark 4.2 states that the same notion results if one replaces compact spaces by unital C∗-algebras: if Γ acts on C∗-algebras A_i with states φ_i, no A_i has a Γ-invariant state, and ⋃_i ∂_{φ_i}Γ=∂Γ, where ∂_{φ_i}Γ={ω∈∂Γ:lim_{g→ω}(ghφ_i-gφ_i)=0 weak-* for every h∈Γ}, then Γ is properly proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The definition is direct. For the noncommutative reformulation, take K_i to be the state space S(A_i) with the weak-* topology and η_i=δ_{φ_i}. Then ∂_{η_i}Γ=∂_{φ_i}Γ. If K_i had an invariant probability measure η, its barycenter ∫_{S(A_i)}ψ dη(ψ) would be a Γ-invariant state on A_i, contradicting the hypothesis. Thus the compact-space definition applies.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.3 (equivalent characterizations of proper proximality on a boundary piece)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a countable discrete group and let X⊂∂Γ be a boundary piece. The following are equivalent: (i) there are finitely many compact Γ-spaces K_1,...,K_k and measures η_i∈Prob(K_i) such that no K_i admits a Γ-invariant probability measure and X⊂⋃_{i=1}^k ∂_{η_i}Γ; (ii) there is one compact Γ-space K and η∈Prob(K) such that K has no Γ-invariant probability measure and X=∂_ηΓ; (iii) C(X)^{Γ_r}, the C∗-algebra of right Γ-invariant continuous functions on X, admits no left Γ-invariant state; (iv) (C(X)^{∗∗})^{Γ_r} admits no left Γ-invariant state. In particular, for X=∂Γ these conditions are equivalent to Γ being properly proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (i)⇒(iv), put X_i=∂_{η_i}Γ. Lemma 3.10 gives no left invariant state on C(X_i)^{Γ_r}. Let q_i∈C(X)^{∗∗} be the support projection corresponding to X_i∩X; since X⊂⋃X_i, ∨_i q_i=1, and q_i(C(X)^{∗∗})^{Γ_r} identifies with (C(X_i)^{∗∗})^{Γ_r}. A left invariant state on (C(X)^{∗∗})^{Γ_r} would be nonzero on some q_i and would restrict to a forbidden invariant state. The implication (iv)⇒(iii) is the technical heart due to Ozawa: if A=(C(X)^{∗∗})^{Γ_r} has no invariant state, Hahn-Banach gives g_1,...,g_d∈Γ and x_1,...,x_d∈A with ∥1-Σ_k(x_k-g_k x_k)∥<1/2. Approximate x_k weak-* by elements of C(X), use convex combinations to get norm asymptotic right invariance and preserve the inequality, lift to ℓ∞Γ, and then paste the lifts with an increasing quasicentral approximate unit α_n∈I_0(X) satisfying ∥(1-α_n)a∥→0 and ∥α_n-gα_n∥→0. The pasted elements y_k=Σ_n(α_{n+1}-α_n)y_k^{(n)} project to z_k∈C(X)^{Γ_r} and still satisfy ∥1-Σ_k(z_k-g_k z_k)∥≤1/2, which rules out a left invariant state on C(X)^{Γ_r}. The implication (iii)⇒(ii) is Lemma 3.11, and (ii)⇒(i) is immediate.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Example 4.6 (non-elementary convergence groups)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If Γ is a non-elementary convergence group, then Γ is properly proximal. Here a convergence group means that Γ acts continuously on a compact space K with at least three points and the induced action on the locally compact space of distinct triples in K is proper; non-elementary means Γ is infinite and does not preserve a subset of K of cardinality at most two.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a convergence action, Bowditch's result quoted in the paper implies that for every diffuse probability measure η on K, ∂Γ=∂_ηΓ. A non-elementary convergence action has no Γ-invariant probability measure. The compact space K contains a perfect set, hence by Lemma 2.1 carries a diffuse probability measure. Thus the single action Γ↷K with this η satisfies the definition of proper proximality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.7 and Examples 4.8-4.9 (proper arrays, cocycles, and nonamenable bi-exact groups)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If Γ admits a proper two-sided array b:Γ→H into a nonamenable unitary representation π:Γ→U(H), then Γ is properly proximal. In particular, any group admitting a proper cocycle into a nonamenable representation is properly proximal. Also every nonamenable bi-exact group is properly proximal; the paper recalls that bi-exactness is equivalent to exactness plus a proper two-sided array into the left regular representation, and also to amenability of the left Γ-action on C(∂Γ)^{Γ_r}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If b is proper, then the boundary piece from Lemma 3.13 is all of ∂Γ. Lemma 3.13 gives an equivariant u.c.p. map θ:B(H)→C(∂Γ)^{Γ_r}. If C(∂Γ)^{Γ_r} had a left Γ-invariant state m, then m∘θ would be an Ad(π(Γ))-invariant state on B(H), meaning π is amenable in Bekka's sense. Since π is assumed nonamenable, no such state exists; Theorem 4.3 then gives proper proximality. A proper cocycle is a special case of a proper two-sided array. For a nonamenable bi-exact group, either use the proper array into the left regular representation, which is nonamenable exactly when Γ is nonamenable, or use amenability of Γ↷C(∂Γ)^{Γ_r}, since an invariant state would imply amenability of Γ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.10 (stability properties)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The class of properly proximal groups is stable under finite direct products, under passing to co-amenable subgroups, and under commensurability up to finite kernels. More explicitly: (1) a direct product of finitely many properly proximal groups is properly proximal; (2) if Λ<Γ is co-amenable and Γ is properly proximal, then Λ is properly proximal; (3) finite-index changes and quotients or extensions by finite kernels preserve proper proximality.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a product Γ=Γ_1×Γ_2, the coordinate maps extend to π_i:ΔΓ→ΔΓ_i and ∂Γ=π_1^{-1}(∂Γ_1)∪π_2^{-1}(∂Γ_2). Pulling back the compact actions witnessing proper proximality of Γ_i gives a finite family covering ∂Γ, and invariant measures are preserved exactly under the quotient to Γ_i. For a co-amenable subgroup Λ<Γ, restriction C(∂Γ)→C(∂Λ) is Λ×Λ-equivariant; a left Λ-invariant state on C(∂Λ)^{Λ_r} would produce a left Λ-invariant state on C(∂Γ)^{Γ_r}, and co-amenability averages it to a left Γ-invariant state, contradicting Theorem 4.3. For finite-index inclusions, one reduces to a normal finite-index subgroup and uses averaging over Γ/Λ to build a Γ-equivariant conditional expectation C(∂Γ)^{Λ_r}→C(∂Γ)^{Γ_r}, together with a decomposition of C(∂Γ)^{Λ_r} into finitely many right translates of C(∂Λ)^{Λ_r}. For a surjection Γ→Γ′ with finite kernel, the induced map identifies C(∂Γ′)^{Γ′_r} with C(∂Γ)^{Γ_r}, so invariant boundary states exist for one group exactly when they exist for the other.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.11 (properly proximal groups are not inner amenable)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If Γ is properly proximal, then Γ is not inner amenable. Equivalently, every inner amenable group fails to be properly proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For an inner amenable group Γ, take a conjugation-invariant state m on ℓ∞Γ that vanishes on c_0(Γ). It descends to a state on C(∂Γ). On C(∂Γ)^{Γ_r}, left translation agrees with conjugation after right translation, and right translation acts trivially; therefore the restriction of m to C(∂Γ)^{Γ_r} is left Γ-invariant. This contradicts Theorem 4.3 if Γ were properly proximal.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.13 (parabolic proximal pieces for simple algebraic groups over local fields)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be an almost k-simple, connected, simply connected algebraic group over a local field k, and write G=G(k). Then there are finitely many proper parabolic k-subgroups P_i<G and measures η_i∈Prob(G(k)/P_i(k)) such that ∂G=⋃_i ∂_{η_i}G, where ∂G is the Stone-Cech boundary for the locally compact group G.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the Cartan decomposition G=MS^+M, where M is compact, S is a maximal k-split torus, and S^+ is a positive Weyl chamber. Let α_1,...,α_d be the simple roots. For each i, take the maximal parabolic P_i corresponding to deleting α_i, and let V_i^- be the unipotent radical of the opposite parabolic. The projection p_i:G→G/P_i is open on V_i^-, and the push-forward η_i of a Haar-equivalent probability measure on V_i^- is quasi-invariant. The key contraction estimate is: for every compact C⊂V_i^- and neighborhood O of the identity in V_i^-, there is A>0 such that sCs^{-1}⊂O whenever s∈S^+ and |α_i(s)|_k≥A. This follows from the root-space decomposition of the Lie algebra of V_i^-: all roots occurring have negative α_i coefficient, so Ad(s) contracts them when α_i(s) grows. Consequently, if s_n∈S^+ has |α_i(s_n)|→∞ and h_n,k_n converge in G, then h_n s_n k_n η_i converges weak-* to δ_{hP_i}. Finally any unbounded net in S^+ has |α_i(s_n)|→∞ for some i, so every boundary direction lies in some ∂_{η_i}G.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.14 and Corollary 4.15 (linear examples)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let k_i be finitely many local fields, let G_i be semisimple connected k_i-groups, set G_i=G_i(k_i), and let Γ be a discrete subgroup of Π_i G_i whose projection to each G_i is Zariski dense. Then Γ is properly proximal. In particular, lattices in semisimple algebraic groups over local fields are properly proximal. Corollary 4.15 adds that if Γ<GL_d(Q) is finitely generated and has trivial solvable radical, then Γ is properly proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Proposition 4.14, first reduce to the case where each algebraic group is simply connected by passing to simply connected covers; finite kernels do not affect proper proximality by Proposition 4.10. Then decompose into almost simple factors and discard compact projection factors, again using finite-kernel stability. Lemma 4.13 gives finitely many compact homogeneous spaces G_i/P_{i,j} with measures η_{i,j} whose proximal pieces cover each ∂G_i. Pulling these actions back to Γ, the boundary of Γ is covered because a sequence escaping in the product escapes in some coordinate. Furstenberg's lemma, as cited in the paper, says that a Zariski dense subgroup with noncompact closure has no invariant probability measure on G_i/P_{i,j}; hence the pulled-back actions witness proper proximality. For Corollary 4.15, embed the finitely generated group over a number field into a finite product of local fields so that it is discrete, replace its Zariski closure by the quotient modulo its solvable radical, and apply Proposition 4.14; the trivial solvable radical ensures injectivity on Γ up to the required finite-kernel considerations.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.16 (weakly compact subalgebras in crossed products by properly proximal groups)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a properly proximal group, let σ:Γ↷(Q,τ) be a trace-preserving action on a tracial von Neumann algebra, and set M=Q⋊Γ. If P⊂M is a weakly compact von Neumann subalgebra such that N_M(P)'' contains LΓ, then P≺_M Q; that is, a corner of P embeds into Q inside M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Identify L²(M)=L²(Q)⊗ℓ²Γ, with M generated by Q⊗1 and u_g=σ_g⊗λ_g, while JMJ is generated by JQJ and 1⊗ρ(Γ). Weak compactness gives a state φ on B(L²M) which is tracial on M and JMJ, P-central, and invariant under uJuJ for u∈N_M(P). Assume for contradiction that P⊀_M Q. By Lemma 2.4, the P-central state φ must vanish on Q⊗K(ℓ²Γ), the compact ideal corresponding to Me_QM. Let π:ℓ∞Γ→C(∂Γ) be the quotient and let B=π^{-1}(C(∂Γ)^{Γ_r}). Put C=C∗(1⊗B,{u_g:g∈Γ}) and D=C∗(JQJ,1⊗ρ(Γ))⊂JMJ. For x∈C and y∈D, the commutator [x,y] lies in Q⊗K(ℓ²Γ), because functions in B are right invariant modulo c_0 and the left/right regular representations commute modulo the same compact ideal. The main claim is that φ(ux)=φ(xu) for every x∈C and u∈N_M(P). To prove it, apply the uJuJ-invariance of φ to T=xJu*J to get φ(uJuJ x Ju*J)=φ(xu), approximate Ju*J strongly by y_i∈D, use normality of φ on JMJ and Cauchy-Schwarz for the seminorm ∥a∥_φ=φ(a*a)^{1/2}, and commute x past y_i modulo Q⊗K(ℓ²Γ), where φ vanishes. Since φ is tracial on M, the claim extends to u∈N_M(P)'', in particular to LΓ. Hence φ(1⊗λ_g(f))=φ(1⊗f) for all g∈Γ and f∈B whose image is in C(∂Γ)^{Γ_r}. Because φ vanishes on 1⊗c_0(Γ), its restriction factors through a left Γ-invariant state on C(∂Γ)^{Γ_r}, contradicting Theorem 4.3. Therefore P≺_M Q.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.17 and Proposition 4.18 (weak amenability upgrades weak compactness to amenable subalgebras)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Theorem 4.17: If Γ is properly proximal and weakly amenable, σ:Γ↷(Q,τ) is trace-preserving, and M=Q⋊Γ, then every amenable von Neumann subalgebra P⊂M with N_M(P)'' containing LΓ satisfies P≺_M Q. In particular Γ is Cartan-rigid: for every free ergodic probability-measure-preserving action, the group-measure-space factor has the canonical Cartan subalgebra as its unique Cartan up to unitary conjugacy. Proposition 4.18, used to prove this, states: if Γ is properly proximal and weakly amenable, B is tracial, M=B⊗LΓ, A⊂M is amenable, and for every g∈Γ there is w_g∈U(B) such that w_g⊗u_g∈N_M(A)'', then A≺_M B.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Proposition 4.18, weak amenability and amenability of A allow the use of Popa-Vaes [PV14a, Theorem 5.1]. In the notation of the paper, this produces normal states ω_i on a von Neumann algebra N built from B, P=N_M(A)'', and LΓ, whose implementing vectors ξ_i yield a state Ω on B(H⊗ℓ²Γ) satisfying: Ω is trace-like on π(M) and Jπ(M)J; Ω is A-central on B⊗B(ℓ²Γ); and Ω is invariant under π(u)Jπ(u)J for u∈N_M(A). If A⊀_M B, Lemma 2.4 forces Ω to vanish on B⊗K(ℓ²Γ). Then the proof repeats the boundary-state argument of Theorem 4.16 with B⊗B(ℓ²Γ) in place of B(L²M): define the preimage of C(∂Γ)^{Γ_r} in ℓ∞Γ, commute it past a strongly dense algebra modulo B⊗K(ℓ²Γ), and show Ω(xπ(u))=Ω(π(u)x) for all normalizers. Applying this to the assumed normalizing unitaries w_g⊗u_g gives a left Γ-invariant state on C(∂Γ)^{Γ_r}, contradicting proper proximality. For Theorem 4.17, use the dual coaction Δ:M→M⊗LΓ given by Δ(a u_g)=a u_g⊗u_g. Put B=M and A=Δ(P). Since Δ(u_g)=u_g⊗u_g lies in N_{M⊗LΓ}(Δ(P))'', Proposition 4.18 gives Δ(P)≺_{M⊗LΓ}M; a routine intertwining argument then implies P≺_M Q.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.4 (equivalent formulations of bi-exactness toward a boundary piece)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a countable discrete group and let X⊂∂Γ be a nonempty boundary piece. The following are equivalent: (i) Γ is bi-exact toward X, meaning that the left Γ-action on the Gelfand spectrum of C(X)^{Γ_r} is amenable; (ii) there is an amenable action Γ↷K on a compact space and η∈Prob(K) such that X⊂∂_ηΓ; (iii) Γ is exact and there is a map μ:Γ→Prob(Γ) such that lim_{g→ω} ∥μ(sgt)-s·μ(g)∥_1=0 for every s,t∈Γ and every ω∈X.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (i)⇒(ii), apply Lemma 3.11 to realize X as ∂_ηΓ for an action whose amenability follows from amenability of Γ↷C(X)^{Γ_r}. For (ii)⇒(iii), take the amenability maps P_n:K→Prob(Γ) and define μ_n(g)=∫_K P_n(gx)dη(x). Uniform equivariance of P_n gives ∥μ_n(sg)-sμ_n(g)∥_1 small on prescribed finite sets. For right translation, fix t and approximate P_n uniformly by its restriction to a finite subset F⊂Γ. If the set of g where ∥μ_n(gt)-μ_n(g)∥_1 is large had closure meeting X, then the finite-dimensional continuous function x↦P_n(x)|_F would contradict the proximality relation lim_{g→ω}(gtη-gη)=0. A diagonal argument over finite sets and tolerances gives μ satisfying (iii); exactness follows from existence of an amenable compact action. For (iii)⇒(i), define μ∗:ℓ∞Γ→ℓ∞Γ by μ∗(f)(g)=∫ f dμ(g), and compose with restriction to C(X). The limit identities imply that the resulting u.c.p. map is left Γ-equivariant and lands in C(X)^{Γ_r}. Since Γ is exact, Γ↷ℓ∞Γ is amenable, and amenability passes through this equivariant u.c.p. image.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.5 (relative property AO / multiplication modulo compact-toward-X operators)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be an exact group and let X⊂∂Γ be a boundary piece. Then Γ is bi-exact toward X if and only if there exists a u.c.p. map θ:C∗_λ(Γ)⊗_min C∗_ρ(Γ)→B(ℓ²Γ) such that θ(x⊗y)-xy∈K(Γ;X) for all x∈C∗_λ(Γ) and y∈C∗_ρ(Γ), where K(Γ;X) denotes operators compact toward X.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is the relative version of Brown-Ozawa's characterization of bi-exactness. Amenability of the action on C(X)^{Γ_r} gives a completely positive lift of the multiplication representation of the left and right reduced group C∗-algebras modulo the ideal I(X)=A_Γ∩K(Γ;X). Conversely, such a u.c.p. lift implies the amenability condition for the boundary action. Lemma 3.6 is what identifies the relevant quotient of A_Γ=C∗(ℓ∞Γ,λΓ,ρΓ) with multiplication modulo compact-toward-X operators.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.6 (solidity-type consequence of bi-exactness toward X)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a group and X⊂∂Γ a boundary piece. Assume Γ is bi-exact toward X. If (u_i) is a net of unitaries in U(LΓ) which has positive mass on X when viewed as a bounded net in ℓ²Γ, then the relative commutant Q=(u_i)'∩LΓ has an amenable direct summand.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Define a state φ on B(ℓ²Γ) by φ(T)=lim_i⟨T û_i,û_i⟩ along a subnet/ultrafilter. Then φ is tracial on LΓ and RΓ, and φ(vJvJ)=1 for all v∈U(Q). Extend φ to B(ℓ²Γ)^{∗∗}; positive mass on X gives φ(q_X)>0. Normalize ψ(a)=φ(q_X a)/φ(q_X) on A_Γ. By Lemma 3.6, ψ vanishes on I(X). Lemma 5.5 then makes the map x⊗y↦ψ(xy) a state on C∗_λ(Γ)⊗_min C∗_ρ(Γ). Exactness gives property C, allowing this state to extend to LΓ⊗_min RΓ, normal on each factor. The relation φ(vJvJ)=1 yields ψ(v⊗overline v)=1 for v∈U(Q). Approximate the extended state by vector states on ℓ²Γ⊗ℓ²Γ; the vectors are asymptotically Q-central. The resulting state on B(ℓ²Γ), obtained by testing T⊗1, is normal on LΓ and Q-central, which is the standard criterion that Q has an amenable direct summand.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 5.7 and 5.8 (arrays and patching for directional bi-exactness)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Proposition 5.7: For a discrete group Γ and boundary piece X⊂∂Γ, the following are equivalent: (i) there is a map μ:Γ→Prob(Γ) satisfying the condition of Theorem 5.4(iii); (ii) there is a two-sided array b:Γ→ℓ²Γ into the left regular representation which is proper toward X, meaning that the boundary piece {ω:lim_{g→ω}∥b(g)∥=∞} contains X; (iii) there is a unitary representation weakly contained in the regular representation and an array into it proper toward X. Proposition 5.8: if Γ is bi-exact toward each of finitely many boundary pieces X_1,...,X_n, then Γ is bi-exact toward ⋃_i X_i.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Proposition 5.7 is the relative form of the Popa-Vaes array characterization of bi-exactness: probability kernels μ with asymptotic left-equivariance/right-invariance along X can be converted into regular-representation arrays by standard square-root or cocycle-type constructions, and conversely arrays weakly contained in the regular representation yield such kernels. For Proposition 5.8, choose for each X_i an array q_i into ℓ²Γ proper toward X_i. The direct sum q=⊕_i q_i is again a two-sided array into a multiple of the regular representation, and it is proper toward ⋃_i X_i because divergence occurs in at least one summand along every point of the union. Proposition 5.7 and Theorem 5.4 then give bi-exactness toward the union.

proof_techniques.name: Boundary pieces as two-sided directions at infinity
proof_techniques.description: The paper systematically replaces the whole Stone-Cech boundary ∂Γ by closed subsets X⊂∂Γ invariant under both left and right multiplication. Such a piece has an ideal I_0(X)⊂ℓ∞Γ and a quotient C(X) with commuting left and right Γ-actions; the right-invariant algebra C(X)^{Γ_r} is the object on which one tests for left invariant states. This localizes boundary arguments to selected directions, while keeping enough two-sided invariance to commute left and right regular representations modulo compact-type ideals.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For graph product von Neumann algebras, one can try to build analogues of these boundary pieces from reduced words, cliques, or graph-product normal forms, with left/right module actions replacing group translations. Irreducibility of the graph may be used to ensure finitely many such pieces cover all noncompact directions without admitting a central/invariant state.

proof_techniques.name: Proper proximality as absence of invariant boundary states
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 4.3 turns the dynamical definition of proper proximality into the state-theoretic criterion that C(∂Γ)^{Γ_r} has no left Γ-invariant state. This is crucial because von Neumann algebraic hypotheses naturally produce states, not compact actions. The proof also shows that finite families of proximal actions, a single proximal action, and bidual boundary state obstructions are all equivalent.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A von Neumann algebra version of proper proximality is likely best attacked by proving that the relevant boundary algebra admits no central state extending the trace. The paper's equivalence suggests that it is enough to rule out invariant states on a carefully chosen right-invariant boundary quotient.

proof_techniques.name: Constructing boundary maps from compact actions
proof_techniques.description: Given Γ↷K and η∈Prob(K), the map θ_0(f)(g)=∫f d(gη) from C(K) to ℓ∞Γ descends on the proximal piece X=∂_ηΓ to a u.c.p. map θ:C(K)→C(X) which is left equivariant and right invariant. Thus absence of invariant measures on K transfers directly to absence of invariant states on C(X)^{Γ_r}.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the graph product algebra admits a natural compact or noncommutative boundary action, an analogous orbit-state map may push states on the boundary into a quotient algebra at infinity. The trace-zero unitaries at vertices could help construct nontrivial orbit states whose proximal directions are controlled by graph-product word combinatorics.

proof_techniques.name: Constructing boundary maps from arrays
proof_techniques.description: A two-sided array b:Γ→H into a representation π gives a boundary piece where ∥b(g)∥→∞. Normalized vectors b(g)/∥b(g)∥ define a u.c.p. map B(H)→C(X) which is left equivariant for Ad π and right invariant. If π is nonamenable and b is proper, any invariant state on C(∂Γ)^{Γ_r} would yield an Ad π-invariant state on B(H), contradiction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For graph products, a length or syllable-count function may produce an array into an appropriate Hilbert bimodule/correspondence. Properness of such an array in irreducible graph-product directions could be a route to a nonamenability or no-central-state argument.

proof_techniques.name: Central-state obstruction via weak compactness and non-intertwining
proof_techniques.description: The proof of Theorem 4.16 begins with a weak-compactness state φ on B(L²(Q⋊Γ)). If P does not intertwine into Q, Popa's criterion forces φ to vanish on Q⊗K(ℓ²Γ). This vanishing lets one commute a boundary algebra past a dense subalgebra of JMJ modulo compacts, proving that φ is tracial on an algebra large enough to make its restriction a left invariant state on C(∂Γ)^{Γ_r}. Proper proximality forbids that state.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is the closest template for proving proper proximality of graph product von Neumann algebras: assume a forbidden central state exists, use compact-at-infinity ideals coming from graph-product word length to commute modulo compacts, and extract an invariant/central boundary state contradicting irreducibility.

proof_techniques.name: Finite patching of boundary directions
proof_techniques.description: Proper proximality is defined using finitely many actions whose proximal pieces cover ∂Γ. Theorem 4.3 shows finite patching is equivalent to a single boundary-state obstruction, while Proposition 5.8 shows that for the stronger directional bi-exactness property, finite patches can be combined by direct sums of arrays. This lets the paper handle higher-rank examples where no single negatively curved boundary captures all directions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Finite graph products naturally have finitely many vertex or clique directions. One can try to prove proper proximality by assigning a boundary piece to each relevant graph direction and using irreducibility to show the pieces collectively rule out central states.

proof_techniques.name: Right-invariance modulo c_0 or compact ideals
proof_techniques.description: In the crossed-product proof, the algebra B=π^{-1}(C(∂Γ)^{Γ_r}) consists of bounded functions that are right invariant modulo c_0(Γ). Consequently elements from B commute with right regular operators modulo compact operators. This is the operator-algebraic bridge between boundary dynamics and commutation estimates in B(L²M).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A graph-product proof may need an analogue of right invariance modulo compact operators for projections/functions depending on reduced-word tails. Trace-zero vertex unitaries could be used to test that the error terms vanish in the appropriate compact or mixing ideal.

proof_techniques.name: Dual coaction amplification
proof_techniques.description: To pass from weak compactness to arbitrary amenable subalgebras under weak amenability, the paper uses the dual coaction Δ(a u_g)=a u_g⊗u_g. This embeds a crossed product into a tensor product where Popa-Vaes weak amenability technology supplies states with the same invariance properties as weak compactness. Proper proximality is then applied in the tensor product setting.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a graph product von Neumann algebra has a usable grading or coaction by a graph product group or word-length structure, an amplification into a tensor product may help convert amenability assumptions into the type of central states that can be attacked by boundary methods.

proof_techniques.name: Directional bi-exactness and positive-mass nets
proof_techniques.description: For a boundary piece X, bi-exactness toward X is equivalent to amenability of the left action on C(X)^{Γ_r}, to probability kernels μ(sgt)≈sμ(g) along X, and to arrays proper toward X. If a net of unitaries in LΓ has positive mass on such an X, then its relative commutant has an amenable direct summand. This is a localized version of Ozawa solidity.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Even if full proper proximality is the target, localized bi-exactness-style estimates may help control relative commutants associated with vertex or link subalgebras in a graph product von Neumann algebra.

other_useful_info: The introduction states the main consequences in broad form: every properly proximal group satisfies the weakly compact Cartan obstruction of Theorem 1.4, and if it is also weakly amenable then its group-measure-space factors are Cartan-rigid as in Theorem 1.5. The paper explicitly lists properly proximal classes: nonamenable bi-exact groups, non-elementary convergence groups, lattices in noncompact semisimple Lie groups of arbitrary rank, and groups admitting a proper cocycle into a nonamenable representation; it also proves closure under finite products and commensurability up to finite kernels. Properly proximal groups are never inner amenable, so inner amenability is a fundamental obstruction. The paper raises questions about mapping class groups, Out(F_n), measure-equivalence invariance, and whether finitely generated linear groups are properly proximal exactly when they are not inner amenable. Key cited external tools that may matter for related arguments are Popa's intertwining-by-bimodules, Popa-Vaes weak amenability theorem [PV14a, Theorem 5.1], Brown-Ozawa bi-exactness machinery, Bowditch's convergence group proximality result, and Furstenberg's lemma on invariant measures for Zariski dense subgroups.
why_relevant: Introduces proper proximality for groups and the small-at-infinity boundary viewpoint. The group analogue for irreducible graph products is a central model for the desired von Neumann algebra statement, and the paper supplies the original central-state obstruction philosophy.

title: Proper proximality in non-positive curvature
overall_summary: The paper proves proper proximality for several classes of countable groups acting on nonpositively curved spaces: groups with proper nonelementary rank-one CAT(0) actions, nonelementary CAT(0) cubical groups, groups acting on locally finite thick affine buildings, and many hierarchically hyperbolic groups including mapping class groups. The core method is dynamical: construct compact boundary G-spaces carrying diffuse or quasi-invariant measures and prove that every sequence escaping compact sets has a subsequence with north-south or almost-everywhere convergence on the boundary; this verifies Boutonnet-Ioana-Peterson proper proximality. For CAT(0) cube complexes, the paper combines this boundary criterion with Caprace-Sageev rank rigidity to reduce to rank-one factors. For the original graph-product von Neumann algebra problem, the most useful content is the collection of abstract proximality criteria, the product/finite-family mechanisms, and the CAT(0) cube-complex proof template, since irreducible graph products often have non-product, rank-one-like boundary dynamics.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 1.1 (Boutonnet-Ioana-Peterson proper proximality for countable groups)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a countable group. An ultrafilter ω on G is nonprincipal if every finite subset of G has ω-measure 0. If K is a compact G-space and η is a diffuse probability measure on K, meaning η has no atoms, then a nonprincipal ultrafilter ω on G is η-proximal if for every h in G one has lim_{g→ω}((gh)·η - g·η) = 0 in the weak-star topology on signed measures on K. The group G is properly proximal if there exist finitely many compact G-spaces K_1,...,K_l and diffuse probability measures η_i on K_i such that: first, no K_i carries a G-invariant probability measure; second, for every nonprincipal ultrafilter ω on G, there exists i such that ω is η_i-proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a definition recalled from BIP21. The paper emphasizes that it is often easier to prove this definition through sequential dynamical criteria on compact boundaries, developed in the subsequent lemmas.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1.3 (properly proximal proper action implies properly proximal group)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a countable group and let X be a proper metric space equipped with a proper isometric G-action. Assume the G-action on X is properly proximal in the following sequential sense: there are finitely many compact metrizable G-spaces K_1,...,K_l, none carrying a G-invariant probability measure, and diffuse probability measures η_i on K_i, such that for every sequence (g_n) in G escaping every compact subset of X, there exist an index i and a subsequence (g_{σ(n)}) such that for every h in G, lim_{n→∞}(g_{σ(n)}hη_i - g_{σ(n)}η_i) = 0 weak-star. Then G is properly proximal in the ultrafilter sense of Definition 1.1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix a nonprincipal ultrafilter ω on G. For every i and h, compactness of the probability-measure space on K_i gives a weak-star ultralimit η_{i,h} = lim_{g→ω} ghη_i. Given a finite subset F of G containing the identity and small neighborhoods V_{i,h} of η_{i,h}, the set of g such that ghη_i lies in V_{i,h} for all i and h in F belongs to ω, hence is infinite. Since the measure spaces are metrizable, one chooses a sequence of pairwise distinct elements g_n such that g_nhη_i converges to η_{i,h} simultaneously for all i and h in F. Properness of the G-action on X implies that a subsequence of (g_n) escapes every compact subset of X. By sequential proper proximality of the action, after passing to a further subsequence there is an i such that g_{σ(n)}hη_i - g_{σ(n)}η_i tends to 0 for every h in F. Therefore η_{i,h} = η_{i,id} for all h in F. Since F was arbitrary and there are only finitely many i, a finite-intersection or pigeonhole argument over an exhaustion of G by finite sets gives one i for which η_{i,h} = η_{i,id} for all h in G. Thus ω is η_i-proximal. As the same K_i have no invariant probability measures and η_i are diffuse, the BIP definition is satisfied.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1.4 (products of properly proximal actions)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let k be a positive integer. For each i in {1,...,k}, let G_i be a countable group acting by isometries on a proper metric space X_i, and assume the G_i-action on X_i is properly proximal in the sequential action sense. Let G be a subgroup of G_1 × ... × G_k whose projection to each factor G_i is surjective. Then the induced G-action on X_1 × ... × X_k is properly proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use, for the G-action, the finite union of the compact spaces that witness proper proximality of each factor action, with G acting through the corresponding coordinate projection. Surjectivity of G → G_i ensures that if one of these compact G_i-spaces had a G-invariant probability measure, then it would have a G_i-invariant probability measure, which is excluded. If a sequence g_n = (g_{n,1},...,g_{n,k}) in G escapes every compact subset of the product X_1 × ... × X_k, then after passing to a subsequence at least one coordinate sequence g_{n,i} escapes every compact subset of X_i. Proper proximality of the G_i-action gives a compact space K and diffuse measure η in the i-th family, plus a further subsequence such that g_{n,i}aη - g_{n,i}η tends to 0 for every a in G_i. For h in G, take a to be the i-th coordinate of h; this gives g_nhη - g_nη → 0 for the G-action.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1.5 (measure accumulation under pointwise accumulation control)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a countable group acting on a compact metrizable space K. Let (g_n) be a sequence in G, let λ be a probability measure on K, and let A be a Borel subset of K. Suppose that for λ-almost every x in K, every accumulation point of the sequence (g_nx) belongs to A. Then every weak-star accumulation point ν of the sequence of measures (g_nλ) satisfies ν(A) = 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites FLM18, Lemma 8.3. The standard argument uses compact metrizability and regularity. If L is a compact subset of K \ A, then for almost every x the compact cluster set of (g_nx) is disjoint from L, so for that x there is some positive neighborhood radius, depending on x, such that eventually g_nx avoids that neighborhood of L. By decomposing the full-measure set according to a countable list of radii and using dominated convergence, one obtains arbitrarily small upper bounds on g_nλ of some open neighborhood of L along the sequence. Portmanteau then gives ν(L) = 0. Inner regularity of ν on K \ A gives ν(K \ A) = 0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1.6 (north-south-type dynamical criterion with one compact space)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a countable group and X a proper metric space with an isometric G-action. Assume there is a compact metrizable G-space K with no G-invariant probability measure, and a G-invariant Borel subset K* of K containing a Borel subset homeomorphic to a Cantor set. Assume further that for every sequence (g_n) in G escaping every compact subset of X, there are a subsequence (g_{σ(n)}) and points ξ_- and ξ_+ in K such that for every ξ in K* \ {ξ_-}, the sequence g_{σ(n)}ξ converges to ξ_+. Then the G-action on X is properly proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Since K* contains a Borel Cantor subset, choose a diffuse probability measure η on K with η(K*) = 1. Given an escaping sequence, pass to the subsequence and points ξ_-, ξ_+ provided by the hypothesis. For every h in G, the measure hη is still diffuse and supported on K*, because K* is G-invariant; hence hη gives zero mass to the exceptional singleton {ξ_-}. For hη-almost every ξ, g_{σ(n)}ξ → ξ_+. Applying Lemma 1.5 with A = {ξ_+} yields g_{σ(n)}hη → δ_{ξ_+}. The same holds for h = id, so g_{σ(n)}hη - g_{σ(n)}η → 0 for every h. This is exactly sequential proper proximality of the action.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1.8 (almost-everywhere convergence criterion with finitely many compact spaces)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a countable group acting by isometries on a proper metric space X. Let K_1,...,K_l be compact metrizable G-spaces, none carrying a G-invariant probability measure. For each i let η_i be a quasi-invariant probability measure on K_i. Assume that for every sequence (g_n) in G escaping every compact subset of X, there exist an index i, a subsequence (g_{σ(n)}), and a point ξ_+ in K_i such that for η_i-almost every ξ in K_i, g_{σ(n)}ξ converges to ξ_+. Then the G-action on X is properly proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix an escaping sequence and choose i, a subsequence, and ξ_+ as in the hypothesis. For every h in G, quasi-invariance of η_i implies that hη_i has the same null sets as η_i, so g_{σ(n)}ξ → ξ_+ for hη_i-almost every ξ. Lemma 1.5 gives g_{σ(n)}hη_i → δ_{ξ_+}. Taking h = id gives g_{σ(n)}η_i → δ_{ξ_+}, hence g_{σ(n)}hη_i - g_{σ(n)}η_i → 0 for every h. This verifies the sequential definition of a properly proximal action.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.3 (free rank-one subgroup from a nonelementary rank-one CAT(0) action)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a proper CAT(0) space and let G act nonelementarily by isometries on X, where nonelementary means that G fixes no point of X and has no finite orbit in the visual boundary ∂∞X. Assume G contains a rank-one isometry, meaning an isometry with an invariant axis that bounds no half-plane. Then G contains a nonabelian free subgroup whose action on X is proper, nonelementary, and contains a rank-one element.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Hamenstädt, G contains two rank-one elements g and h with disjoint fixed point sets {g^{-∞},g^{+∞}} and {h^{-∞},h^{+∞}} in ∂∞X. Choose pairwise disjoint neighborhoods U_g^+, U_g^-, U_h^+, U_h^- in the visual compactification X ∪ ∂∞X, with a point x outside their union. Rank-one elements act with north-south dynamics, so for sufficiently large n the powers g^{±n} and h^{±n} send the complement of the corresponding repelling neighborhood into the attracting neighborhood. The ping-pong lemma gives a nonabelian free subgroup H generated by large powers of g and h. The same ping-pong setup shows that nontrivial elements move x into the chosen neighborhoods and that the H-orbit of x is free and discrete. If the H-action were not proper, infinitely many distinct h_n would move some y a uniformly bounded distance; by the triangle inequality they would move x a uniformly bounded distance, contradicting discreteness in the proper space X. The subgroup is nonelementary and contains rank-one powers of g and h.
