key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.5 (Cantor set of uniformly Morse rays in the CAT(0) visual boundary)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a proper CAT(0) space admitting a nonelementary isometric action of a group G with a rank-one element, and fix a basepoint x_0 in X. Then there exist a Morse gauge N and a topological embedding of a Cantor set into the visual boundary ∂∞X such that for every image point ξ, the geodesic ray [x_0,ξ) is N-Morse. Here N-Morse means that every (K,C)-quasigeodesic segment with endpoints on the ray stays in the N(K,C)-neighborhood of the ray.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Lemma 2.3, pass to a subgroup H acting properly and nonelementarily with a rank-one element. Yang's theorem supplies constants K,C,L, an infinitely ended rooted simplicial tree T, and a quasi-isometric embedding f:T→X with f(root)=x_0 such that the image of every infinite ray based at the root is an L-strongly contracting (K,C)-quasigeodesic ray. Sultan's result implies these images are uniformly Morse for some gauge N'. Cordes's Morse-boundary functoriality then gives a Morse gauge N such that the geodesic ray in X with the same endpoint as f(r) is N-Morse for every ray r in T, and the induced map on Morse boundaries ∂_M T → ∂_M X is a topological embedding into the subspace of N-Morse rays. Since T is a tree, its Morse boundary equals its visual boundary, a Cantor set. On a fixed Morse-gauge stratum, the topology agrees with the induced visual-boundary topology in the CAT(0) space, giving an embedded Cantor subset of ∂∞X.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.6 (no invariant probability on the visual boundary in the rank-one nonelementary case)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a proper CAT(0) space and let G act by isometries on X. Assume the G-action is nonelementary and G contains a rank-one element. Then the visual boundary ∂∞X carries no G-invariant probability measure.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let g be a rank-one element. Rank-one isometries act on X ∪ ∂∞X with north-south dynamics, so for any probability measure ν on ∂∞X, every weak-star accumulation point of g^nν is supported on the two fixed points {g^{-∞},g^{+∞}}. Thus any G-invariant probability measure must be supported on this two-point set. Hamenstädt's theorem gives another rank-one element with disjoint fixed point set. Applying the same argument to it forces the invariant measure to be supported on a disjoint two-point set, impossible for a probability measure.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.7 (visibility-boundary Borel set containing a Cantor set)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a proper CAT(0) space and let G be a countable group acting nonelementarily by isometries on X with a rank-one element. Let (∂∞X)_vis be the subset of the visual boundary consisting of visibility points, namely points ξ such that every η in ∂∞X can be joined to ξ by a geodesic in X. Then (∂∞X)_vis contains a G-invariant Borel subset K* which contains a Borel subset homeomorphic to a Cantor set.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Charney-Sultan prove that every Morse geodesic ray in a proper CAT(0) space has a visibility endpoint. Proposition 2.5 gives a Cantor set of endpoints of uniformly Morse rays, hence a Cantor subset of (∂∞X)_vis. Taking the union of its G-translates gives a G-invariant subset K*. Since G is countable and the original Cantor set is Borel, this union is Borel, and it remains inside the visibility boundary because visibility is G-invariant.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.8 (Papasoglu-Swenson dynamics on visibility points)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a proper CAT(0) space and let G act by isometries on X. If (g_n) is a sequence in G escaping every compact subset of X, then there exist a subsequence (g_{σ(n)}) and points ξ_- and ξ_+ in ∂∞X such that for every visibility point ξ in (∂∞X)_vis \ {ξ_-}, the sequence g_{σ(n)}ξ converges to ξ_+ in ∂∞X.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If ξ is a visibility point and ξ' is any point of ∂∞X, then ξ and ξ' are joined by a geodesic. This geodesic cannot bound a half-plane, otherwise the boundary circle of that half-plane would contain points not joinable to ξ. By Bridson-Haefliger, this implies the Tits distance d_T(ξ,ξ') is greater than π. Hence a Tits ball of radius π contains at most one visibility point. Papasoglu-Swenson's theorem for sequences escaping compact subsets of a proper CAT(0) space gives, after passing to a subsequence, attracting and repelling boundary points with convergence outside a Tits ball of radius π. Since that ball contains at most one visibility point, convergence holds for every visibility point except possibly one.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.1 (proper proximality of rank-one CAT(0) actions)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a countable group and X a proper CAT(0) metric space with an isometric G-action. Assume the G-action is nonelementary, meaning G fixes no point of X and has no finite orbit in ∂∞X, and assume G contains a rank-one isometry. Then the G-action on X is properly proximal. In particular, if the G-action on X is proper, then G is a properly proximal group.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply the abstract criterion Lemma 1.6 with K = ∂∞X. Lemma 2.6 shows K has no G-invariant probability measure. Lemma 2.7 provides a G-invariant Borel subset K* of the visibility boundary containing a Cantor set, so K supports a diffuse probability measure concentrated on K*. Lemma 2.8 supplies the required one-exception north-south convergence for every sequence escaping compact subsets of X. Lemma 1.6 therefore gives proper proximality of the action. If the action is proper, Lemma 1.3 converts proper proximality of the action into proper proximality of the countable group G.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.2 (product version for rank-one CAT(0) actions)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let k be a positive integer. For each i in {1,...,k}, let X_i be a proper CAT(0) space and let G_i act by isometries on X_i. Let G be a countable subgroup of G_1 × ... × G_k which acts properly on X_1 × ... × X_k. Assume that for every i, the projection π_i(G) acts nonelementarily on X_i and contains a rank-one element. Then G is properly proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each i, apply Theorem 2.1 to the countable group π_i(G) acting on X_i; this gives a properly proximal action of π_i(G) on X_i. Regard G as a subgroup of the product of its images π_i(G), so its projections are surjective. Lemma 1.4 gives proper proximality of the G-action on the product X_1 × ... × X_k. Since the G-action on this product is assumed proper, Lemma 1.3 implies that G is properly proximal.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.9 (proper proximality of nonelementary CAT(0) cubical groups)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a countable group acting properly and nonelementarily by cubical automorphisms on a proper finite-dimensional CAT(0) cube complex X. Then there exist a G-invariant subcomplex X' of X and a finite-index subgroup G_0 of G such that the G_0-action on X' is properly proximal. Consequently G is properly proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Caprace-Sageev first gives a G-invariant subcomplex X' on which the action is essential and remains nonelementary. Their product decomposition theorem gives, after passing to a finite-index subgroup G_0, a product decomposition X' = X'_1 × ... × X'_p into irreducible CAT(0) cube complexes preserved by G_0. The G_0-action on each factor remains nonelementary. Caprace-Sageev rank rigidity then says each irreducible factor action contains a rank-one isometry. By Theorem 2.1, each factor action is properly proximal. Lemma 1.4 implies the product action of G_0 on X' is properly proximal. Since the action is proper, Lemma 1.3 gives proper proximality of G_0; proper proximality passes to finite-index overgroups by BIP21, Proposition 1.6, so G is properly proximal.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.13 (almost every Weyl chamber is opposite a fixed chamber for the building harmonic measure)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be a locally finite thick affine building with no tree factor in the setup of Section 2.3. Let Δ be the compact space of Weyl chambers in the spherical building at infinity of X. For a special vertex o in X, let μ_o be the standard probability measure on Δ characterized by μ_o(Ω_o(z)) = 1/N_{σ(o,z)}, where Ω_o(z) is the set of Weyl chambers whose sector based at o contains z, σ(o,z) is the vectorial distance, and N_{σ(o,z)} is the cardinality of the corresponding vectorial sphere. Then for every Weyl chamber C_0 in Δ, μ_o-almost every Weyl chamber C is opposite to C_0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a special vertex x and Weyl group element w, define Δ_x^w(C_0) as the set of chambers C such that the sectors Q(x,C) and Q(x,C_0) lie in a common apartment and have Weyl distance w. Also define Y_λ^w(x,C_0) as the set of vertices y at vectorial distance λ from x for which Ω_x(y) meets Δ_x^w(C_0). A vectorial horofunction computation shows Y_λ^w(x,C_0) = {y in V_λ(x) : h_{x,C_0}(y) = wλ}. Parkinson's counting results imply that the cardinality of this set, and therefore μ_x(Δ_x^w(C_0)), is independent of C_0. If w is not the longest Weyl group element w_0, choose a nonmaximal simplex u such that for distinct chambers C,C' in the infinite residue Res(u), the sets Δ_x^w(C) and Δ_x^w(C') are disjoint. Since all these sets have the same μ_x-measure and Res(u) is infinite by thickness, that common measure must be 0. The union over special vertices x of the non-opposite sets is countable, so μ_o gives measure 0 to chambers at Weyl distance w≠w_0 from C_0. Thus almost every chamber is opposite C_0.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.16 (proper proximality for groups acting on affine buildings)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a countable group admitting a proper minimal isometric action on a locally finite thick affine building X, and assume G has no finite orbit in the visual boundary ∂∞X. Then G is properly proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First assume X has no tree factor. The compact spaces used are the finitely many spaces Δ_I of simplices of fixed type I in the spherical building at infinity. They carry projected measures μ_I from the harmonic measure μ_o on Weyl chambers. Lemma 2.18, using Adams-Ballmann's theorem and Lemma 2.10, shows none of the Δ_I carries a G-invariant probability measure: an invariant measure on Δ_I would give one on ∂∞X, forcing a fixed boundary point or a Euclidean factor, contrary to the hypotheses in this building setting. For a sequence g_n escaping compact sets, Papasoglu-Swenson's CAT(0) boundary theorem gives, after subsequence, points m,p in ∂∞X such that points outside a Tits ball near m have limit points near p. Choose a simplex type I and σ in Δ_I so that p lies in the interior position corresponding to σ. Let C_0 be a chamber containing m. By Proposition 2.13, μ_o-almost every chamber C is opposite C_0; such a chamber contains points at Tits distance π from m. Perturbing inside C and using the Papasoglu-Swenson convergence shows that the type-I face τ of C satisfies g_nτ → σ. Hence μ_I-almost every τ in Δ_I converges to σ, and Lemma 1.8 gives proper proximality of the action; properness gives proper proximality of G via Lemma 1.3. In the general case, decompose X = Y × Z where Z is a product of tree factors and Y has no tree factor. Minimality and the no-finite-boundary-orbit hypothesis pass to the factors. The above argument treats Y, while Z is a proper CAT(0) cube complex handled by Theorem 2.9 after passing to a finite-index subgroup. Lemma 1.4 combines the factor actions, Lemma 1.3 gives proper proximality of the finite-index subgroup, and BIP finite-index stability gives proper proximality of G.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.6 (compact metrizability of the HHS compactification)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (X,S) be a hierarchically hyperbolic space, assume X is proper and S is countable. Let ∂X be the Durham-Hagen-Sisto HHS boundary. Then X ∪ ∂X is compact and metrizable.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The compactness is exactly Durham-Hagen-Sisto, Theorem 3.4. Metrizability is attributed to Hagen, Proposition 2.1. The lemma is used so that the HHS compactification can serve as a compact metrizable G-space in Lemma 1.6.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Facts 3.3 and 3.5 (HHS boundary convergence criteria)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (X,S) be a hierarchically hyperbolic space with principal domain S_0 and principal hyperbolic space C S_0. A boundary point ξ in ∂X is represented as a finite sum ξ = Σ_S a_S^ξ ξ_S over a nonempty finite set Supp(ξ) of pairwise orthogonal domains S, with ξ_S in ∂∞C S, positive coefficients a_S^ξ, and total coefficient 1. Fact 3.3 says that a sequence x_n in X converges to ξ if and only if: for every S in Supp(ξ), π_S(x_n) converges to ξ_S in C S ∪ ∂∞C S; and for every S equal to an element of Supp(ξ) or orthogonal to every element of Supp(ξ), and every S' in Supp(ξ), the ratio d_{C S}(π_S(x_0),π_S(x_n)) / d_{C S'}(π_{S'}(x_0),π_{S'}(x_n)) tends to a_S^ξ/a_{S'}^ξ. Fact 3.5 gives the analogous criterion for a sequence ξ_n in ∂∞C S_0 converging to a point ξ in ∂X \ ∂∞C S_0: replace π_S(x_n) by the boundary projection ∂π_S(ξ_n), and require the same convergence and ratio conditions.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: These facts are unpacked directly from Durham-Hagen-Sisto's definition of the HHS boundary topology. In the paper they are used to prove convergence of images g_nξ of principal-boundary points by comparing their nonprincipal projections with projections of actual points g_n y in X.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.7 (north-south dynamics on the HHS boundary from escaping sequences)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (X,S) be a hierarchically hyperbolic space with X proper. Let G be a countable group acting nonelementarily by HHS automorphisms on (X,S), where nonelementary means that the induced action on the principal hyperbolic space C S_0 contains two independent loxodromic isometries. Assume the G-action on X is by uniform quasi-isometries and has an almost equivariant principal projection, meaning there is C≥0 such that d_{C S_0}(π_{S_0}(gx), gπ_{S_0}(x))≤C for all g in G and x in X. If x is in X and (g_n) is a sequence in G escaping every compact subset of X, then there exist ξ_- and ξ_+ in ∂X and a subsequence (g_{σ(n)}) such that for every ξ in ∂∞C S_0 \ {ξ_-}, one has g_{σ(n)}ξ → ξ_+ in ∂X.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Since X is proper and the action is by uniform quasi-isometries, d(x,g_nx) and d(x,g_n^{-1}x) diverge. Compactness of X ∪ ∂X gives a subsequence, still denoted g_n, such that g_nx → ξ_+ and g_n^{-1}x → ξ_-; the same limits hold if x is replaced by any fixed y in X. Fix ξ in ∂∞C S_0 \ {ξ_-}. The first key claim is that for every p in C S_0, the sequence g_n^{-1}p does not converge to ξ in C S_0 ∪ ∂∞C S_0; otherwise almost equivariance of π_{S_0} and Fact 3.4 would force g_n^{-1}x → ξ in X ∪ ∂X, contradicting ξ≠ξ_-. Hyperbolicity of C S_0 then gives a point z on a quasigeodesic ray to ξ, uniformly far along the ray, such that the Gromov products ⟨g_n^{-1}p,ξ⟩_z are uniformly bounded. Applying g_n gives uniformly bounded ⟨p,g_nξ⟩_{g_nz}. If ξ_+ lies in ∂∞C S_0, then g_np → ξ_+ in the principal hyperbolic compactification, and the bounded Gromov product forces g_nξ → ξ_+ in ∂∞C S_0, hence in ∂X. If ξ_+ is not in ∂∞C S_0, write ξ_+ = Σ_S a_S ξ_S over pairwise orthogonal domains. Choose z, and then a point ~z in X with π_{S_0}(~z) close to z, sufficiently far so that geodesics from π_{S_0}(g_n~z) to g_nξ avoid uniform neighborhoods of the bounded subsets X_S associated to every S in Supp(ξ_+) or orthogonal to all of Supp(ξ_+). The bounded geodesic image axiom then makes ∂π_S(g_nξ) uniformly close to ρ_{S}^{S_0}(π_{S_0}(g_n~z)), and the consistency axiom makes this uniformly close to π_S(g_n~z). Since g_n~z → ξ_+, the HHS convergence criteria Facts 3.3 and 3.5 imply g_nξ → ξ_+.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.1 (proper proximality of nonelementary HHS actions)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (X,S) be a hierarchically hyperbolic space with X proper. Let G be a countable group acting nonelementarily by HHS automorphisms on (X,S), assume the G-action on X is by uniform quasi-isometries, and assume the action has an almost equivariant principal projection to the principal hyperbolic space C S_0. Then the G-action on X is properly proximal. If in addition the G-action on X is proper, then G is properly proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use Lemma 1.6 with K = X ∪ ∂X, which is compact metrizable by Lemma 3.6, and with K* = ∂∞C S_0 embedded in ∂X. Since the action on C S_0 is nonelementary, ∂∞C S_0 contains a Cantor set, for instance the limit set of a Schottky subgroup generated by independent loxodromics. The proof notes that nonelementarity also prevents K from carrying a G-invariant probability measure. Proposition 3.7 gives the required one-exception convergence for every sequence escaping compact subsets of X. Lemma 1.6 gives proper proximality of the action, and if the action on X is proper, Lemma 1.3 gives proper proximality of G.

proof_techniques.name: Sequential action criterion as a bridge to ultrafilters
proof_techniques.description: Instead of checking the BIP ultrafilter definition directly, the paper introduces a sequential notion of a properly proximal action on a proper metric space. One proves that every sequence escaping compact sets has a subsequence along which a boundary measure becomes asymptotically insensitive to right multiplication by any fixed group element. Lemma 1.3 then converts this sequential statement into ultrafilter proper proximality of the group, using compactness of probability-measure spaces and properness of the action.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a graph product von Neumann algebra, this suggests proving a sequential escape criterion for words or bimodule vectors first, and only afterward converting it into the formal proper proximality condition. Properness of a geometric action is replaced in the von Neumann setting by whatever notion forces sequences outside finite or relatively compact pieces.

proof_techniques.name: Diffuse measure on a boundary subset with one exceptional repelling point
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 1.6 isolates a reusable north-south principle. If a compact G-boundary K contains a G-invariant Borel subset K* with a Cantor subset, and every escaping sequence admits a subsequence that sends every point of K* except possibly one to a common attracting point, then any diffuse measure supported on K* witnesses proper proximality. The exceptional point is harmless because diffuse measures have no atoms, and Lemma 1.5 turns pointwise almost-everywhere convergence into weak-star convergence of pushed measures.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In a graph product setting, one can look for a compact boundary of infinite reduced words, rays, or states where multiplication has convergence away from one forbidden prefix or direction. The trace-zero unitary hypothesis is a natural source of non-atomic or diffuse boundary data in an operator-algebraic analogue.

proof_techniques.name: Finite family of boundary pieces with quasi-invariant measures
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 1.8 allows proper proximality to be witnessed by finitely many compact spaces rather than a single boundary. The hypothesis is weaker than one-exception convergence: for each escaping sequence, it is enough that on one of the finitely many compact spaces, almost every point with respect to a quasi-invariant measure is attracted to a single limit. This is essential in the affine-building proof, where different simplex types at infinity are used as separate compact spaces.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the graph product von Neumann algebra has several natural boundary strata indexed by cliques, syllable supports, or graph types, this finite-family criterion suggests not forcing all dynamics onto one compactification. Irreducibility may be used to rule out invariant measures on each stratum.

proof_techniques.name: Coordinate escape in products
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 1.4 proves that proper proximality of actions is stable under finite products when a sequence escaping the product must escape in at least one coordinate. The compact spaces for the product action are just the compact spaces from the factor actions, viewed through coordinate projections. Surjectivity of the subgroup projections ensures that absence of invariant measures is inherited from the factors.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Graph joins correspond to product or tensor decompositions in many graph-product contexts. The lemma gives a clean model for handling decomposable cases and clarifies why irreducibility, i.e. absence of a nontrivial join decomposition, is the case where one expects a single nonelementary direction.

proof_techniques.name: Rank-one CAT(0) boundary package: Morse Cantor set plus Papasoglu-Swenson convergence
proof_techniques.description: For rank-one CAT(0) actions, the paper constructs the boundary subset K* from Morse or visibility rays. A free subgroup generated by independent rank-one elements gives a quasi-isometrically embedded tree whose boundary is a Cantor set of uniformly Morse rays. Papasoglu-Swenson's theorem then supplies convergence of escaping sequences on all visibility points except possibly one. Together with absence of invariant measures on the visual boundary, this proves proper proximality.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a geometric blueprint for graph-product groups acting on CAT(0) cube complexes, and it suggests an operator-algebraic analogue: identify rank-one-like reduced words whose powers have north-south behavior on a boundary and use a Cantor family of independent directions to build diffuse measures.

proof_techniques.name: CAT(0) cube rank-rigidity reduction
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 2.9 combines Caprace-Sageev rank rigidity with the rank-one CAT(0) criterion. A nonelementary proper cubical action is first restricted to an essential invariant subcomplex; after finite index, the complex splits as a product of irreducible factors; nonelementarity on each factor yields rank-one elements; proper proximality follows factorwise and then by the product lemma.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The irreducibility assumption in the original graph problem resembles the non-product hypothesis in cubical rank rigidity. This proof suggests reducing any graph-product argument to showing that the irreducible graph forces rank-one or nonelementary behavior, while join decompositions should be treated by product stability.

proof_techniques.name: No invariant boundary measure from independent north-south dynamics
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 2.6 shows a common way to verify the no invariant probability condition. A rank-one element forces every invariant measure to be supported on its two fixed points because its iterates have north-south dynamics. A second rank-one element with disjoint fixed points gives a contradiction. The same philosophy appears in the HHS proof, where nonelementary action on the principal hyperbolic space rules out invariant measures.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For graph product von Neumann algebras, one should look for two incompatible proximal directions generated by trace-zero unitaries in different noncommuting parts. Such directions can rule out invariant states or means on a proposed boundary object.

proof_techniques.name: Building opposition and harmonic measure
proof_techniques.description: In affine buildings, ordinary pointwise north-south dynamics on the full boundary is replaced by a finite collection of simplex-type compacta Δ_I. The harmonic measure μ_o on chambers has the key property that almost every chamber is opposite any fixed chamber. Papasoglu-Swenson convergence in the Tits boundary then implies that for a suitable type I, almost every simplex of that type is attracted to one limit simplex.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This technique is useful if a graph-product boundary naturally has transversality or opposition rather than global one-exception convergence. A measure saying that a random boundary point is transverse to any fixed finite obstruction could replace the building harmonic measure.

proof_techniques.name: HHS principal hyperbolic projection controls boundary dynamics
proof_techniques.description: For hierarchically hyperbolic spaces, the paper proves north-south-type convergence on the HHS compactification by using the principal hyperbolic space C S_0. If an escaping sequence has forward and backward limits ξ_+ and ξ_-, then any principal-boundary point ξ≠ξ_- is sent to ξ_+. The proof uses hyperbolic Gromov products in C S_0, the bounded geodesic image axiom, consistency of projections, and the HHS boundary convergence criteria.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Many graph products of groups admit hierarchical structures, and reduced-word combinatorics often has a principal hyperbolic or contact-graph-like projection. Even for von Neumann graph products, this suggests proving convergence by first controlling a main coarse projection and then using consistency to control subordinate components.

proof_techniques.name: Diffuse Cantor sets from Schottky subgroups
proof_techniques.description: Both the CAT(0) and HHS arguments use nonelementarity to produce a Cantor set in the boundary: in CAT(0), via a quasi-isometrically embedded tree coming from independent rank-one elements; in HHS, via the limit set of a Schottky subgroup generated by independent loxodromics in the principal hyperbolic space. This Cantor set is used only to support a diffuse probability measure, not to carry the whole dynamics.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The trace-zero unitary condition at each vertex may be used to produce nontrivial independent directions or free-product-like substructures. A Cantor family of boundary states or paths could provide the diffuse measure required for proper proximality.

other_useful_info: The introduction notes that BIP21, Theorem 4.3, makes the finite-family definition of proper proximality equivalent to a formulation with a single compact G-space, although finite families are convenient in proofs. The paper repeatedly uses BIP21, Proposition 1.6, for stability of proper proximality under passing to finite-index overgroups, and in the mapping-class-group section also uses the obstruction that groups with infinite FC-center are inner amenable and not properly proximal. The paper itself proves proper proximality for countable groups and discusses consequences for group von Neumann algebras and group-measure-space factors; it does not treat graph product von Neumann algebras directly. The CAT(0) cubical result, Theorem 2.9, is the closest group-theoretic analogue to graph-product situations where a graph product group acts on a Davis or quasi-median CAT(0)-cubical object. Theorem 4.1, quoted from BIP21, says that if G is properly proximal then LG has no weakly compact Cartan subalgebra, and for essentially free ergodic pmp actions G↷(X,μ), the crossed product L∞(X)⋊G has the expected uniqueness or nonexistence of weakly compact Cartan subalgebras depending on whether the action is weakly compact.
why_relevant: Provides geometric criteria for proper proximality for groups acting on CAT(0) spaces and CAT(0) cube complexes. It is useful for understanding the group-theoretic graph-product analogue and for importing boundary/proximality ideas from non-positive curvature.

title: Proper Proximality Among Various Families of Groups
overall_summary: The paper proves new proper proximality results for groups acting on trees, wreath products, and especially a complete classification of proper proximality for finite graph products of non-trivial groups. The graph-product proof is built from three ingredients: decompose a graph product at each vertex as an amalgamated free product over the link subgroup, prove relative proper proximality for such amalgamated free products, and show that the Stone-Cech boundary of the graph product is covered by the boundary pieces associated with all link subgroups. The key combinatorial input is a normal-form intersection lemma for double cosets of full subgraph products, which implies that the link boundary pieces cover the whole boundary. For the original von Neumann algebra problem, the most relevant group-theoretic template is Proposition 3.7 and its immediate consequence that arbitrary graph products over irreducible finite graphs are properly proximal; the proof suggests replacing non-trivial vertex groups by vertex algebras containing trace-zero unitaries, and replacing link subgroups by link subalgebras in an analogous relative-to-full upgrade.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 2.5 and BIP18 relative proper proximality criterion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be a countable group and let S = {H_i}_{i∈I} be a countable family of subgroups of G. A sequence g_n in G is said to go to infinity relative to S, written g_n → ∞/S, if for every t_1,t_2∈G and every i∈I, eventually g_n is not in t_1 H_i t_2. Let c0(G,S) be the C*-subalgebra of ℓ∞(G) consisting of functions f such that f(g_n) → 0 for every sequence g_n → ∞/S. Then c0(G,S) contains c0(G) and is invariant under the left and right actions of G. The quotient ℓ∞(G)/c0(G,S) is C(X_S), where X_S is a closed left- and right-G-invariant subset of the Stone-Cech boundary βG \ G. The paper records the following equivalent formulations: G is properly proximal relative to S iff there is no left-G-invariant state on (ℓ∞(G)/c0(G,S))^{G_r}, equivalently no left-G-invariant state on ((ℓ∞(G)/c0(G,S))** )^{G_r}; equivalently, there are one or finitely many compact G-spaces with no invariant probability measure and probability measures whose associated small-at-infinity boundary pieces contain X_S. The group G is properly proximal, absolutely, when S is the trivial subgroup. The paper also notes the finite-cover consequence: if H_1,...,H_n are subgroups such that G is properly proximal relative to each H_i and the associated boundary pieces X_{H_i} cover the whole Stone-Cech boundary βG \ G, then G is properly proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equivalence itself is cited from Boutonnet-Ioana-Peterson, Theorem 4.3, and is used as a black box. The important mechanism for the paper is the state formulation: relative proper proximality eliminates left-invariant states on the right-invariant part of the quotient by c0(G,S). The finite-cover consequence follows most directly from the compact-action formulation: for each H_i choose compact G-spaces with no invariant measures and measures whose small-at-infinity boundary pieces contain X_{H_i}; if the finitely many X_{H_i} cover the full boundary, the union of these finitely many compact actions witnesses absolute proper proximality by condition (2) in the criterion. Equivalently, a hypothetical left-invariant state on the absolute proximal quotient would have to be supported on at least one of the relative boundary pieces, contradicting relative proper proximality there.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.3 and Lemma 2.4: normal forms and vertex-link amalgam decomposition of graph products
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a finite simple undirected graph with vertex set V(Γ), and let {G_v}_{v∈V(Γ)} be countable groups. The graph product Γ(G) is the quotient of the free product ∗_{v∈V(Γ)} G_v by the commutation relations [g,h]=1 whenever g∈G_u, h∈G_v, and (u,v) is an edge of Γ. A word g_1...g_n with g_i∈G_{v_i}\{1} is reduced in Green's sense if syllables from the same vertex group cannot be brought together by commuting intervening syllables along graph edges. Theorem 2.3 says that every non-trivial element of Γ(G) has a reduced expression, unique up to commuting adjacent syllables from adjacent vertex groups. For v∈V(Γ), let S_v = {w∈V(Γ) : (v,w)∈E(Γ)} be the link of v, and for T⊂V(Γ) write Γ_T(G) for the full subgraph product over T. Lemma 2.4 says that Γ(G) is canonically isomorphic to the amalgamated free product G_1 ∗_H G_2, where G_1 = Γ_{S_v∪{v}}(G), G_2 = Γ_{V(Γ)\{v}}(G), and H = Γ_{S_v}(G).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The normal-form theorem is quoted from Green's thesis. Lemma 2.4 follows directly from the graph-product presentation. The subgroup Γ_{S_v∪{v}}(G) contains the vertex group G_v and all groups in its link, so G_v commutes with H = Γ_{S_v}(G). The subgroup Γ_{V\{v}}(G) contains all vertex groups except G_v. These two full subgraph products generate Γ(G), and their intersection is exactly the link subproduct H by the normal-form theorem. The only relations between the two generated subgroups that are not already internal to them are precisely the relations inside H, so the universal property of amalgamated free products gives the canonical isomorphism.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.1: relative north-south dynamics for tree actions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G act by isometries on a tree T with infinitely many vertices. Let ΔT be Bowditch's compactification V(T) ⊔ ∂T. Let S = {Stab(e) : e∈E(T)} be the family of edge stabilizers. If g_n → ∞/S, then there is a subsequence g_{n_k} and points a,b∈ΔT such that for every x∈ΔT\{b}, one has g_{n_k}x → a. Furthermore, if the action G ↷ ΔT is non-elementary, meaning that it does not preserve any subset of cardinality at most 2, then G is properly proximal relative to S.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First choose a vertex o∈V(T) such that both sets {g_n o} and {g_n^{-1} o} are infinite, after passing to a subsequence. This uses the hypothesis g_n → ∞/S: for two vertices u,v, the image paths g_n[u,v] cannot take any fixed edge value infinitely often, since that would put infinitely many g_n into a fixed double coset of an edge stabilizer. Compactness of ΔT gives a subsequence with g_{n_k}o → a and g_{n_k}^{-1}o → b. If a lies in the Gromov boundary ∂T, then b also lies in ∂T. For basic neighborhoods U(a,{e}) and U(b,{f}), the proof uses Gromov products based at o. Points outside U(b,{f}) have uniformly bounded Gromov product with b. Since g_{n_k}o tends to a and g_{n_k}^{-1}o tends to b, the Gromov product estimates imply that g_{n_k} sends the complement of a sufficiently small neighborhood of b into an arbitrarily small neighborhood of a. This gives north-south convergence. If a is a vertex of T, then for x∈V(T) and any edge e, the set of n for which e lies in g_n[o,x] is finite; otherwise some edge of the finite segment [o,x] would equal g_n^{-1}e infinitely often, contradicting escape relative to edge stabilizers. Since g_n o → a, this forces g_n x → a. Boundary points x∈∂T are handled by approximating x along the geodesic ray from o and using boundedness of g_n o near a. For relative proper proximality, choose a diffuse probability measure η on the perfect limit set of the non-elementary action. The north-south convergence and a lemma of Fernos-Lecureux-Matheus imply that for every h∈G, g_{n_k}hη and g_{n_k}η both converge weak-* to δ_a, so the relative boundary piece X_S is contained in the small-at-infinity set ∂_ηG. A G-invariant probability measure on ΔT would be forced by north-south dynamics to give full mass to a two-point set of poles, contradicting non-elementarity. Hence the compact-action criterion for relative proper proximality applies.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.2: amalgamated free products are properly proximal relative to the amalgam
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G_1 and G_2 be countable groups with a common subgroup H≤G_i. Let G = G_1 ∗_H G_2. If for {i,j}={1,2} one has [G_i:H]≥3 and [G_j:H]≥2, then G is properly proximal relative to H.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The short proof uses Bass-Serre theory. The group G acts on the Bass-Serre tree of the amalgamated free product; edge stabilizers are precisely conjugates of H. Under the index assumptions the action on the Bowditch compactification of the Bass-Serre tree is non-elementary: the boundary is infinite and boundary orbits are infinite. Proposition 3.1 then gives proper proximality relative to the family of conjugates of H, which is the same as proper proximality relative to H. The paper also gives a direct normal-form proof that is useful as a template. Choose transversals T={e}∪{t_i} for H\G_1 and S={e}∪{s_j} for H\G_2. In the quotient ℓ∞(G)/c0(G,H), let p_1 be the characteristic projection of elements whose normal form begins with a non-trivial G_1-coset syllable h t_i, and p_2 the analogous projection for a non-trivial G_2-coset syllable h s_j. The key claim is that p_1 and p_2 are right-G-invariant modulo c0(G,H): if right multiplication by a fixed g changed p_1 along a sequence escaping all H-double cosets, then normal-form cancellation would force infinitely many escaping words to have one of finitely many fixed tails, contradicting escape relative to H. Modulo c0(G,H), p_1+p_2=1 because the omitted H part is H-small. Pick two elements g_1,g_2 in G_i\H from distinct H-cosets and one element g_3 in G_j\H. Normal-form ping-pong gives g_1p_1 + g_2p_1 ≤ p_2 and g_3p_2 ≤ p_1. A left-G-invariant state φ on the relative proximal quotient would therefore satisfy 2φ(p_1)≤φ(p_2)≤φ(p_1), hence φ(p_1)=φ(p_2)=0, contradicting φ(p_1+p_2)=1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2: tree action criterion for absolute proper proximality
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be an infinite countable group acting by isometries on a countably infinite tree T. Assume: (1) the induced action G ↷ ΔT on the Bowditch compactification is non-elementary; and (2) for any two distinct edges e,f∈E(T), the intersection Stab(e)∩Stab(f) is finite. Then G is properly proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Proposition 3.1 first gives proper proximality relative to the family S of edge stabilizers. Suppose toward a contradiction that G is not properly proximal. By the double-dual criterion, there is a left-G-invariant state φ on ((ℓ∞(G)/c0(G))** )^{G_r}. Choose representatives H_i for the conjugacy classes of edge stabilizers. In the double dual, form projections p_i = ∨_{t,s∈G} 1_{tH_i s}, and p=∨_i p_i. The quotient by the support p is the relative quotient for the family S, so relative proper proximality forces φ(1-p)=0, hence φ(p)=1. For each left coset position tH_i define p_{t,i}=∨_{s∈G}1_{tH_i s}. Approximate p_{t,i} by projections 1_{tH_i F_n} for an increasing exhaustion F_n of G. The finite-intersection hypothesis on distinct edge stabilizers implies that if tH_i t^{-1} and sH_j s^{-1} are distinct edge stabilizers, then the corresponding approximants have finite intersections modulo c0(G), and hence p_{t,i}p_{s,j}=0 in the double dual. Thus the projections p_{t,i} behave as mutually orthogonal boundary supports indexed by edge stabilizers. For each edge e choose the unique projection p(e) corresponding to Stab(e); this gives a G-equivariant embedding of ℓ∞(E(T)) into the right-invariant double-dual proximal algebra, with unit equal to the edge-support projection p. There is also a G-equivariant unital map ψ:ℓ∞(V(T))→ℓ∞(E(T)) given on functions by averaging over the two endpoints of an edge, equivalently ψ(f)(e)= (f(e_-) + f(e_+))/2. Since C(ΔT) embeds into ℓ∞(V(T)), the composition φ∘ι∘ψ gives a G-invariant probability measure on ΔT, using φ(p)=1. This contradicts non-elementarity of G ↷ ΔT, as in Proposition 3.1. Therefore G must be properly proximal.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3: relative proper proximality plus almost malnormality upgrades to absolute proper proximality
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H be a subgroup of a countable group G. Assume that G is properly proximal relative to H. Assume also that H is almost malnormal in G, meaning |gHg^{-1}∩H|<∞ for every g∈G\H, and that H is not co-amenable in G, meaning there is no G-invariant mean on ℓ∞(G/H). Then G is properly proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume G is not properly proximal, and let φ be a left-G-invariant state on ((ℓ∞(G)/c0(G))** )^{G_r}. Let p=∨_{t,s∈G}1_{tHs} be the right-invariant support projection of all H-double-coset boundary pieces. Since G is properly proximal relative to H, the complement (1-p)((ℓ∞(G)/c0(G))** )^{G_r} identifies with the relative proximal algebra ((ℓ∞(G)/c0(G,H))** )^{G_r}; hence φ(1-p)=0 and φ(p)=1. For t∈G put p_t=∨_{s∈G}1_{tHs}. Almost malnormality implies that p_g is orthogonal to p_e whenever g∉H: finite intersections of the corresponding double cosets vanish in the quotient by c0(G), and the conclusion follows after taking strong limits in the double dual. Thus δ_{gH}↦p_g gives a G-equivariant unital embedding of ℓ∞(G/H) into the p-corner of the double-dual proximal algebra. Composing with φ yields a G-invariant state on ℓ∞(G/H), contradicting non-co-amenability of H. Therefore no such φ exists and G is properly proximal.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.5: finite-union control of intersections of full subgraph double cosets
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a finite graph and Γ(G) a graph product over Γ. Let Γ_1 and Γ_2 be full subgraphs of Γ, and write Γ_i(G) for the corresponding full subgraph product subgroups. For any g,h∈Γ(G), there exists a finite set F={c_i,d_i : 1≤i≤n}⊂Γ(G) such that Γ_1(G) ∩ g Γ_2(G) h is contained in the finite union ∪_{i=1}^n c_i (Γ_1∩Γ_2)(G) d_i, where Γ_1∩Γ_2 is the full subgraph on V(Γ_1)∩V(Γ_2).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write reduced words g=g_1...g_k and h=h_1...h_m. Let F consist of all products obtained by taking subsequences of the syllables of g and of h, in their original order; this is finite. It suffices to prove that any element of Γ_1(G)∩c Γ_2(G)d, with c,d of this form, lies in one of the claimed c_i(Γ_1∩Γ_2)(G)d_i. Take t=t_1...t_l a reduced word in Γ_2(G) such that ctd∈Γ_1(G). Induct on n(t), the number of syllables of t whose vertices are not in Γ_1∩Γ_2. If n(t)=0, then t∈(Γ_1∩Γ_2)(G) and there is nothing to prove. If n(t)>0, choose a vertex v_0 outside Γ_1∩Γ_2 appearing in t. Since t∈Γ_2(G), this v_0 lies in Γ_2 but not Γ_1. The product ctd lies in Γ_1(G), so every syllable from G_{v_0} must disappear after commuting and cancellation. The proof formalizes this by deleting all G_{v_0}-letters from the word ctd and showing that the resulting ordered product represents the same group element. If not, after reducing the subwords between consecutive G_{v_0}-letters one obtains a non-cancelling configuration whose image under the canonical retraction onto G_{v_0}, together with a non-commuting middle block, prevents the element from belonging to Γ_1(G), a contradiction. Deleting the G_{v_0}-letters reduces n(t), while the surviving pieces of c and d are still subsequence-products from the original finite set F. The induction then gives membership in a finite union of double cosets over (Γ_1∩Γ_2)(G).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.6: link boundary pieces cover the Stone-Cech boundary of a graph product
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a finite graph with vertex set V(Γ), and let Γ(G) be a graph product. For v∈V(Γ), let S_v be the link of v and let Γ_{S_v}(G) be the link subproduct. Let X_{Γ_{S_v}(G)} be the boundary piece associated to the subgroup Γ_{S_v}(G) in the sense of Definition 2.5. Then ∪_{v∈V(Γ)} X_{Γ_{S_v}(G)} equals the whole Stone-Cech boundary of the group Γ(G). Equivalently, if V(Γ)={v_1,...,v_n} and Γ_i=Γ_{S_{v_i}}, then ∩_{i=1}^n c0(Γ(G), Γ_i(G)) = c0(Γ(G)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equality of boundary pieces is reduced to the ideal statement. To prove the ideal statement, it is enough to show that for arbitrary s_i,t_i∈Γ(G), the intersection ∩_{i=1}^n s_i Γ_i(G) t_i is finite. Apply Lemma 3.5 repeatedly to this intersection. It is contained in a finite union of sets g_j (∩_{i=1}^n Γ_i)(G) h_j. But ∩_{i=1}^n S_{v_i}=∅: no vertex is adjacent to itself, so for the index i corresponding to that vertex, it is not in its own link. Therefore (∩_i Γ_i)(G) is the trivial group. The original intersection is consequently finite. This means that any bounded function vanishing relative to each link subgroup differs from a c0-function only, so the link boundary pieces cover the full Stone-Cech boundary.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.7 and immediate irreducible-graph consequence
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a finite graph. The following are equivalent in the paper: (1) the radius r(Γ) is at least 2 and there is no pair of vertices v_1,v_2∈V(Γ) such that every u∈V(Γ)\{v_1,v_2} is adjacent to both v_1 and v_2; (2) for every choice of non-trivial countable groups G_v, the graph product Γ(G) is properly proximal. The paper then notes that, in particular, for an arbitrary choice of non-trivial groups, Γ(G) is properly proximal whenever Γ is irreducible, i.e. whenever the complement graph Γ^c is connected, and also whenever r(Γ)≥3.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the positive implication, fix a vertex v and use Lemma 2.4 to write Γ(G)=G_1∗_H G_2 with G_1=Γ_{S_v∪{v}}(G), G_2=Γ_{V\{v}}(G), and H=Γ_{S_v}(G). Under condition (1), H is proper in both G_1 and G_2, and the graph condition ensures that at least one of the two indices [G_1:H] and [G_2:H] is infinite; in particular the hypotheses of Corollary 3.2 are satisfied for this decomposition. Thus Γ(G) is properly proximal relative to Γ_{S_v}(G) for every vertex v. Lemma 3.6 says that the boundary pieces associated to all these link subgroups cover the whole Stone-Cech boundary. The finite-cover consequence of the relative proper proximality criterion then gives absolute proper proximality of Γ(G). For the obstruction direction, the paper explicitly treats the radius-2 exceptional configuration: if there are vertices v_1,v_2 adjacent to every other vertex, choose G_{v_1}=G_{v_2}=Z/2Z. Since in the radius-2 case v_1 and v_2 are not adjacent, the graph product contains a direct factor (Z/2Z)∗(Z/2Z), the infinite dihedral group, so the resulting graph product is inner amenable and not properly proximal. Radius-1 obstructions are handled by the same product-decomposition philosophy used later in the classification: a universal vertex gives a direct product factor, and choosing that vertex group to be an infinite amenable group obstructs proper proximality. For the irreducible consequence, if Γ^c is connected and |V(Γ)|≥2, Γ has no universal vertex, and the exceptional pair would disconnect Γ^c by isolating {v_1,v_2} from the remaining vertices; hence condition (1) applies.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.8: radius at least 2 gives either proper proximality or an infinite amenable direct summand
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a finite graph with r(Γ)≥2 and let Γ(G) be a graph product of non-trivial groups. Then Γ(G) is either properly proximal or contains an infinite amenable direct summand.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Proposition 3.7 the only case not already properly proximal is the radius-2 exceptional case: there exist vertices v_1,v_2 such that all vertices in S=V(Γ)\{v_1,v_2} are adjacent to both v_1 and v_2. Since r(Γ)=2, v_1 and v_2 are not adjacent. Hence Γ(G) decomposes as (G_{v_1}∗G_{v_2}) × Γ_S(G). The free product G_{v_1}∗G_{v_2} is the infinite amenable group Z/2Z∗Z/2Z if both vertex groups have order 2; otherwise it is properly proximal by the amalgamated-free-product criterion with trivial amalgam. One then iterates the same analysis on the induced subgraph Γ_S. The conclusion uses the known fact, cited from BIP18 Proposition 4.11, that properly proximal groups cannot have infinite amenable direct summands.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.9: proper proximality of a direct product forces proper proximality of each factor
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If G=G_1×G_2 is a countable direct product and G is properly proximal, then both G_1 and G_2 are properly proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: It is enough to prove the assertion for G_1. In (ℓ∞(G)/c0(G))** define p=∨_{g∈G_2}1_{G_1×{g}}, the boundary support of the vertical slices. The paper constructs a G-equivariant isomorphism p(ℓ∞G/c0(G))** ≅ ⊕_{G_2}^{∞}(ℓ∞G_1/c0(G_1))**, where the direct sum is an ℓ∞-sum indexed by G_2. For a finite F⊂G_2, there is an isomorphism Θ_F from ⊕_F ℓ∞G_1 to 1_{G_1×F}ℓ∞G, sending a tuple (f_h)_{h∈F} to the function with value f_h(g) at (g,h). Passing to quotients and then to biduals gives weak-* continuous isomorphisms on finite corners. Taking strong operator limits over finite F gives the displayed isomorphism, and conversely finite cutdowns recover the inverse. Passing to right-invariant subspaces yields ((ℓ∞G_1/c0(G_1))** )^{G_{1,r}} ≅ p((ℓ∞G/c0(G))** )^{G_r}. If G_1 were not properly proximal, there would be a left-G_1-invariant state φ on the left-hand algebra. Composing φ with the inverse isomorphism and then cutting by p gives a left-G-invariant state on the full right-invariant proximal algebra of G: ψ(f)=φ(Θ^{-1}(pf)). This contradicts proper proximality of G. Thus G_1 is properly proximal, and the same argument applies to G_2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.8: classification of proper proximality for finite graph products of non-trivial groups
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For finite graph products of non-trivial countable groups, the paper defines a recursive class G_pp and proves that Γ(G) is properly proximal if and only if Γ(G) belongs to G_pp. The recursive description is: base case, if r(Γ)≥2 and Γ has no exceptional pair v_1,v_2 that are both adjacent to every vertex outside {v_1,v_2}, then Γ(G) is in G_pp for every choice of non-trivial vertex groups; radius-2 reduction, if r(Γ)=2 and such an exceptional pair v_1,v_2 exists, put S=V(Γ)\{v_1,v_2}, and membership of Γ(G) reduces to requiring that at least one of G_{v_1},G_{v_2} has order at least 3 and that the induced subproduct Γ_S(G) lies in G_pp; radius-1 reduction, if Γ has a universal vertex v, so v is adjacent to every other vertex, put T=V(Γ)\{v}, and membership of Γ(G) reduces to requiring that G_v itself is properly proximal and that Γ_T(G) lies in G_pp. With this recursive definition, Γ(G) is properly proximal exactly for Γ(G)∈G_pp.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The base case is Proposition 3.7. If r(Γ)=2 and the base condition fails, there are vertices v_1,v_2 adjacent to all vertices in S=V\{v_1,v_2}; they are not adjacent to each other, otherwise the radius would be 1. Therefore Γ(G) decomposes as Γ_{ {v_1,v_2} }(G) × Γ_S(G) = (G_{v_1}∗G_{v_2}) × Γ_S(G). The free product factor is properly proximal exactly when at least one of the two vertex groups has order at least 3; if both are order 2, it is the infinite dihedral amenable group. The product criterion, namely Proposition 3.9 together with the converse product stability from BIP18, shows that the direct product is properly proximal exactly when both factors are properly proximal, giving the stated radius-2 recursion. If r(Γ)=1, choose a universal vertex v. Then Γ(G)=G_v × Γ_T(G), where T=V\{v}. Again the direct-product criterion gives proper proximality exactly when both factors are properly proximal, giving the radius-1 recursion. Since each recursive step removes one or two vertices, the procedure terminates and yields the classification.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 1.9: graph products of properly proximal groups
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Arbitrary finite graph products of properly proximal groups are properly proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is immediate from Theorem 1.8. In the base case the result holds for arbitrary non-trivial vertex groups. In the radius-1 case, the universal vertex group is properly proximal by hypothesis and the induced subgraph product is handled recursively. In radius-2 exceptional cases, a properly proximal vertex group cannot be the order-2 obstruction in both exceptional vertices, and the remaining induced subgraph product is again handled recursively. The proof also uses stability of proper proximality under finite direct products in the direction cited from BIP18.

proof_techniques.name: Vertex-link amalgam decomposition
proof_techniques.description: For every vertex v of a graph product Γ(G), split the group as Γ_{S_v∪{v}}(G) ∗_{Γ_{S_v}(G)} Γ_{V\{v}}(G). This isolates the link subproduct Γ_{S_v}(G) as an amalgam and converts questions about the whole graph product into relative questions over links. The proof relies only on the graph-product presentation and Green normal forms, and it is applied simultaneously for all vertices.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A von Neumann graph product should have an analogous decomposition over the link algebra. The trace-zero unitary in each vertex algebra can play the role of a non-trivial syllable ensuring that the two sides of the amalgam are genuinely larger than the link.

proof_techniques.name: Relative proper proximality from amalgamated-free-product ping-pong
proof_techniques.description: Corollary 3.2 proves that G_1∗_H G_2 is properly proximal relative to H when one index is at least 3 and the other at least 2. Besides the Bass-Serre proof, the paper gives a direct quotient-algebra proof: define projections p_1 and p_2 according to whether an amalgam normal form begins on the G_1 side or G_2 side. These projections are right-invariant modulo c0(G,H), and left multiplication by carefully chosen coset representatives gives inequalities g_1p_1+g_2p_1≤p_2 and g_3p_2≤p_1, forcing any invariant state to vanish on p_1+p_2=1.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a strong template for proving relative proper proximality of a graph-product von Neumann algebra over a link subalgebra: replace coset-normal-form projections by reduced-word subspaces or support projections, and use trace-zero unitaries to create the ping-pong shifts.

proof_techniques.name: Boundary cover by link subgroups
proof_techniques.description: The graph-product proof upgrades relative proper proximality to absolute proper proximality by proving ∪_v X_{Γ_{S_v}(G)} is the full Stone-Cech boundary. The key is Lemma 3.5: intersections of double cosets of full subgraph products are controlled by finite unions of double cosets of the intersection subgraph product. Applying this to all links gives finite intersections because the intersection of all vertex links is empty.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the von Neumann algebra problem, this suggests proving a link-cover analogue at the level of relative compact ideals, correspondences, or central states: if a state survives all link-relative quotients, then it should be supported only on the scalar/compact part, because the intersection of all links is empty.

proof_techniques.name: Finite-cover relative-to-absolute upgrade
proof_techniques.description: Once a group is properly proximal relative to each subgroup in a finite family and the associated boundary pieces cover the entire Stone-Cech boundary, absolute proper proximality follows immediately from the BIP18 criterion. In the graph-product proof, the finite family is the set of link subgroups Γ_{S_v}(G), one for each vertex.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is likely the main proof skeleton to port: establish proper proximality of the graph product von Neumann algebra relative to every link subalgebra, then prove a finite cover or intersection-triviality statement showing that these relative obstructions exhaust the full boundary/proximal algebra.

proof_techniques.name: Double-dual support projections as a filler space
proof_techniques.description: In Theorem 1.2 and Lemma 3.3, the paper works inside ((ℓ∞(G)/c0(G))** )^{G_r}. For a subgroup H, projections of the form p_t=∨_{s∈G}1_{tHs} record the right-invariant boundary support of a left coset tH. Relative proper proximality forces an invariant state to live on the join of these supports, while almost malnormality or finite intersections of stabilizers make different supports orthogonal. This allows one to G-equivariantly embed ℓ∞(G/H) or ℓ∞(E(T)) into the proximal algebra and derive a contradiction from non-coamenability or non-elementarity.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A von Neumann analogue may use support projections in a bidual or basic construction to encode relative subalgebras and their translates. Orthogonality should come from reduced-word intersection properties; the resulting embedded commutative algebra can turn a hypothetical central/invariant state into an amenability-type contradiction.

proof_techniques.name: Normal-form deletion induction
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 3.5 uses a deletion induction on the number of syllables of a word lying outside the intersection of two full subgraphs. If an element represented by ctd lies in Γ_1(G) while t lies in Γ_2(G), syllables from vertices in Γ_2\Γ_1 must cancel after commuting; deleting all syllables of one such vertex reduces the complexity while keeping track of only finitely many possible prefixes and suffixes from the fixed words c and d. This yields finite-union containment of intersections of double cosets.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is the combinatorial heart of the graph argument. In a von Neumann graph product, an analogous reduction would likely be formulated in terms of reduced-word Hilbert space decompositions: words supported in two different full subgraphs can overlap only through words supported in the intersection subgraph, up to finitely many fixed prefix/suffix patterns.

proof_techniques.name: Direct-product factor detection
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 3.9 proves that if G_1×G_2 is properly proximal then each G_i is properly proximal, using an explicit identification of a slice-support corner of the proximal double dual with an ℓ∞-sum of copies of the proximal double dual of a factor. This lets the classification reduce join decompositions and universal vertices to proper proximality of direct factors.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original hypothesis assumes irreducibility, so direct-product decomposition is excluded at the graph level. Still, this technique identifies the obstruction one avoids: if the graph were a join, proper proximality would reduce to the factors rather than follow from the link-cover base case.

proof_techniques.name: Radius and exceptional-pair classification
proof_techniques.description: The graph classification separates graphs into: a base case with radius at least 2 and no exceptional pair, where all non-trivial vertex labels give proper proximality; a radius-2 exceptional case where the graph product splits as (G_{v_1}∗G_{v_2})×Γ_S(G); and a radius-1 case where a universal vertex gives G_v×Γ_T(G). The base case is exactly where link-relative proper proximality plus boundary cover applies without requiring vertex groups themselves to be properly proximal.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For an irreducible finite graph with at least three vertices, the complement graph is connected, which rules out universal vertices and the radius-2 exceptional pair. Thus the original problem should correspond to the base-case proof, not the recursive product cases.

other_useful_info: The paper explicitly notes after Proposition 3.7 that arbitrary graph products over irreducible finite graphs are properly proximal, for any non-trivial vertex groups. It also notes in Remark 3.10 that graph products admit natural CAT(0) cube complex actions, but these actions are generally not proper, so the proper proximality result is not a direct consequence of known CAT(0) proper-action criteria. The proof relies on external results from BIP18: the equivalent definitions of relative proper proximality, stability of proper proximality under direct products, and the obstruction that properly proximal groups cannot have infinite amenable direct summands. The graph-product classification generalizes the Duchesne-Tucker-Drob-Wesolek classification of inner amenability for graph products, using the fact that proper proximality implies non-inner amenability.
why_relevant: Contains the complete classification of proper proximality for graph products of nontrivial groups. Its graph-product and relative-to-full proper-proximality upgrade arguments are likely the closest group-theoretic template for proving the stated von Neumann algebra result.

title: Cartan Subalgebras in von Neumann Algebras Associated with Graph Product Groups
overall_summary: The paper classifies Cartan subalgebras for von Neumann algebras arising from graph product groups and their free ergodic probability-measure-preserving actions. The main inputs are Popa intertwining-by-bimodules, Ioana--Vaes normalizer dichotomy/trichotomy results for amalgamated free product von Neumann algebras, and graph-product combinatorics of full or parabolic subgroups, links, stars, joins, and de Rham join decompositions. A central technical result says that a masa with finite-index normalizer inside a crossed product by a graph product group must intertwine into a crossed product by a full subgroup whose de Rham factors are only a clique plus possible exceptional two-vertex edgeless Z_2-pair factors. For the original problem, the most useful content is the systematic use of vertex-removal amalgamated free product decompositions over link subalgebras, the way irreducibility/join decompositions remove tensor-product obstructions, and the lemma showing that co-amenability relative to the amalgam in a nondegenerate group AFP forces an index-two exceptional case.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2.1: full subgroups, links, stars, and vertex-removal amalgamated free product decomposition
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G = (V,E) be a finite simple graph and let {Γ_v : v in V} be groups. The graph product group Γ = G{Γ_v} is generated by the vertex groups Γ_v, subject only to the internal relations in each Γ_v and to commutation relations [Γ_u,Γ_v] = 1 whenever (u,v) is an edge of G. For U subset V, the full subgroup Γ_U is the subgroup generated by {Γ_u : u in U}; it is naturally identified with the graph product over the induced subgraph on U. For v in V, lk(v) is the set of vertices w ≠ v adjacent to v, and st(v) = {v} union lk(v). For U subset V, lk(U) = intersection over u in U of lk(u), with convention lk(empty set) = V. For every w in V there is a graph-product splitting Γ = Γ_{V minus {w}} *_Γ_{lk(w)} Γ_{st(w)}. This splitting is degenerate precisely in the case V = st(w), when Γ_{lk(w)} = Γ_{V minus {w}}. If the graph is a join G = H o K with vertex partition V = W disjoint union U and all cross edges between W and U present, then the corresponding graph product decomposes as a direct product Γ_V = Γ_W × Γ_U.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper recalls this from Green's normal form theory for graph products, specifically citing Green's Lemma 3.20 for the amalgamated free product decomposition. The two full subgroups Γ_{V minus {w}} and Γ_{st(w)} generate Γ, and their intersection is exactly Γ_{lk(w)}; the normal form for graph products shows that no additional relations are introduced beyond the common full subgroup, giving the AFP. The degeneracy condition follows because Γ_{lk(w)} equals Γ_{V minus {w}} exactly when every vertex other than w lies in lk(w), i.e. V = st(w). For a join, every generator in Γ_W commutes with every generator in Γ_U and the full subgroups have trivial intersection, so the universal graph-product presentation identifies Γ_V with Γ_W × Γ_U.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2.1: irreducibility and de Rham join decomposition of a finite graph
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A finite graph G is called irreducible if it does not admit a nontrivial join decomposition G = H o K, where H and K are proper induced subgraphs whose vertex sets form a nonempty partition of V and every vertex of H is adjacent to every vertex of K. The paper recalls the standard de Rham join decomposition: every finite graph G admits a join decomposition G = G_0 o G_1 o ... o G_k, where G_0 is a clique, i.e. a complete graph, and each G_i for i ≥ 1 is an irreducible subgraph with at least two vertices.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is recalled as a well-known graph-theoretic fact and is not proved in the paper. The decomposition is obtained by splitting off maximal join factors: complete-graph factors are grouped into the clique part G_0, while the remaining factors are irreducible for the join operation and have at least two vertices. The paper uses this decomposition to isolate exactly which join factors of a graph product can support finite-index-normalizer masas.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.1: Popa intertwining-by-bimodules criterion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (M,τ) be a tracial von Neumann algebra, let p be a projection in M, let P be a von Neumann subalgebra of pMp, and let Q be a von Neumann subalgebra of M. Let e_Q be the orthogonal projection from L^2(M) onto L^2(Q), and let <M,e_Q> be the basic construction, equipped with its canonical semifinite trace Tr. The following are equivalent. (1) There exist projections p_0 in P and q_0 in Q, a unital normal *-homomorphism θ: p_0 P p_0 → q_0 Q q_0, and a nonzero partial isometry v in q_0 M p_0 such that θ(x)v = vx for every x in p_0 P p_0. (2) There exists a nonzero positive element a in P' ∩ p<M,e_Q>p with Tr(a) < ∞. (3) There is no sequence of unitaries u_n in U(P) such that ||E_Q(x* u_n y)||_2 → 0 for all x,y in pM. When these equivalent conditions hold, the notation is P <_M Q, meaning that a corner of P embeds into Q inside M. If P p_1 <_M Q for every nonzero projection p_1 in P' ∩ pMp, the notation is P <^s_M Q.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this theorem from Popa and does not reprove it. The conceptual proof is that finite-trace positive elements in the basic construction correspond to nonzero P-Q subbimodules of L^2(pM) with finite right Q-dimension; a finite-dimensional right-Q module can be represented by an intertwining partial isometry, giving condition (1). Conversely, an intertwining partial isometry yields a finite-trace projection or positive element in the basic construction. The unitary criterion is the contrapositive formulation: if no finite right-Q-dimensional P-Q subbimodule exists, one can find unitaries in P whose Q-Fourier coefficients vanish against all fixed x,y; if such a sequence exists, it kills all possible finite-trace P-central elements in the basic construction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.2: group-theoretic criterion for intertwining crossed products by subgroups
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Λ and Σ be subgroups of a countable group Γ. Suppose Γ acts trace-preservingly on a tracial von Neumann algebra P, and set M = P ⋊ Γ. Then P ⋊ Λ <_M P ⋊ Σ if and only if there exists g in Γ such that the index [Λ : Λ ∩ gΣg^{-1}] is finite.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper calls this a straightforward generalization of Chifan--Ioana and leaves the proof to the reader. In one direction, if Λ_0 = Λ ∩ gΣg^{-1} has finite index in Λ, then finitely many Λ-coset representatives together with the group unitary u_g produce a nonzero P⋊Λ to P⋊Σ bimodule inside L^2(M) of finite right P⋊Σ-dimension, yielding Popa intertwining. Conversely, if no conjugate of Σ intersects Λ in finite index, one chooses a sequence λ_n in Λ escaping every finite union of sets of the form F_1 Σ F_2, with F_1,F_2 finite subsets of Γ. For x,y with finite Fourier support, E_{P⋊Σ}(x* u_{λ_n} y) is then eventually zero; approximation in L^2 gives the vanishing for all x,y in M. Popa's criterion then gives P⋊Λ not <_M P⋊Σ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.3: Popa conjugacy criterion for Cartan subalgebras
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a II_1 factor and let P,Q be Cartan subalgebras of M. If P <_M Q, then there exists a unitary u in U(M) such that uPu* = Q.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this from Popa. The idea is that an intertwiner between Cartan subalgebras can be amplified, using the fact that both normalizers generate the whole factor, until its support projections become full. Maximal abelianness and regularity then upgrade the partial isometry implementing a corner embedding into a unitary conjugating the two Cartan subalgebras.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2.3 and Lemma 2.4: relative amenability and co-amenability for nondegenerate AFP groups
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a tracial von Neumann algebra (M,τ), a projection p in M, and subalgebras P ⊂ pMp and Q ⊂ M, the paper says P is amenable relative to Q inside M if there is a sequence ξ_n in L^2(<M,e_Q>) such that <xξ_n,ξ_n> → τ(x) for every x in pMp and ||yξ_n - ξ_n y||_2 → 0 for every y in P. When P = M, M is called co-amenable relative to Q. On the group side, a subgroup H of G is co-amenable if there is a left G-invariant state on ℓ∞(G/H). Lemma 2.4 states: if Γ = Γ_1 *_Σ Γ_2 is a nondegenerate amalgamated free product and Γ is co-amenable relative to Σ, then [Γ_i : Σ] = 2 for i = 1,2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume, for contradiction, that one of the indices is at least 3, say [Γ_1:Σ] ≥ 3, and let φ be a left Γ-invariant state on ℓ∞(Γ/Σ). Let S_i be the subset of cosets in Γ/Σ whose normal form begins with a letter from Γ_i minus Σ. Up to the base coset, the sets S_1 and S_2 partition Γ/Σ in the sense used by the argument, and φ(χ_{S_1}+χ_{S_2}) = 1. Choose two distinct nontrivial coset representatives in Γ_1/Σ and one nontrivial representative h in Γ_2/Σ. Normal form multiplication shows that suitable translates of S_2 are disjoint and contained in a translate of S_1, while a translate of S_1 is contained in S_2. By Γ-invariance of φ this forces an inequality of the form 2φ(χ_{S_2}) ≤ φ(χ_{S_1}) ≤ φ(χ_{S_2}), hence φ(χ_{S_2}) = 0. By symmetry φ(χ_{S_1}) = 0, contradicting φ(χ_{S_1}+χ_{S_2}) = 1. Therefore each index is at most 2; nondegeneracy gives each index at least 2, so both are exactly 2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.1: finite-index-normalizer masas intertwine into restricted parabolic crossed products
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ = G{Γ_v} be a graph product group over a finite graph G, and suppose Γ acts trace-preservingly on a tracial von Neumann algebra P. Let M = P ⋊ Γ, let p be a projection in M, and let A ⊂ pMp be a masa such that its normalizer N_{pMp}(A)'' has finite index in pMp. Then there exists an induced subgraph G_0 of G such that A <_M P ⋊ Γ_{G_0}, and such that the de Rham join product decomposition of G_0 has the following restricted form: one distinguished factor may be a clique, while every other de Rham factor is an edgeless two-vertex graph {v_i,w_i} with Γ_{v_i} ≅ Γ_{w_i} ≅ Z_2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose an induced subgraph G_0 = (V_0,E_0) with |V_0| minimal among those satisfying A <_M N, where N = P ⋊ Γ_{G_0}. Let G_0 = G_1 o ... o G_k be its de Rham join decomposition, with G_1 the clique factor. The goal is to show that every non-clique factor G_i, i ≥ 2, is an edgeless two-vertex Z_2-pair. Since A is a masa and its normalizer has finite index, the paper invokes CIK13, Proposition 3.6, to obtain nonzero projections a in A and e in N, a masa B ⊂ eNe, a nonzero projection e_1 in B' ∩ eMe, and a unitary u in M such that Q := N_{eNe}(B)'' has finite index in eNe, the support of E_N(e_1) is e, and u A a u* = B e_1. Now fix a non-clique de Rham factor and a vertex v in it for which the full subgroup Γ_{G_0} has the noncanonical vertex-removal splitting Γ_{G_0} = Γ_{G_0 minus {v}} *_Γ_{lk(v)} Γ_{st(v)}; in particular |lk(v)| ≤ |V_0|-2. Applying the Ioana--Vaes normalizer theorem for amalgamated free product von Neumann algebras to the AFP decomposition of N gives three alternatives: (i) B <_N P⋊Γ_{lk(v)}; (ii) Q <_N P⋊Γ_{G_0 minus {v}} or Q <_N P⋊Γ_{st(v)}; or (iii) Q is amenable relative to P⋊Γ_{lk(v)} inside N. In case (i), an intertwining partial isometry w in N can be chosen with w*w in B and wB ⊂ (P⋊Γ_{lk(v)})w. The support condition sp(E_N(e_1)) = e implies we_1 ≠ 0: otherwise w*w E_N(e_1)=0 and hence w*w e=0, contradiction. Thus w u a A ⊂ (P⋊Γ_{lk(v)}) w e_1 u, so A <_M P⋊Γ_{lk(v)}, contradicting minimality of |V_0|. In case (ii), after cutting by a nonzero projection, one obtains strong intertwining of Q into one AFP leg. Since Q has finite index in eNe, standard finite-index intertwining lemmas imply N <_N that same leg. Lemma 2.2 then forces Γ_{G_0 minus {v}} or Γ_{st(v)} to have finite index in Γ_{G_0}, which is impossible by graph-product normal forms: choosing a vertex t nonadjacent to v and nontrivial g in Γ_t, h in Γ_v gives infinitely many distinct cosets represented by powers of gh. Thus (ii) is impossible. In case (iii), finite index gives eNe amenable relative to Q, and transitivity of relative amenability gives eNe amenable relative to the amalgam; this implies the group Γ_{G_0} is co-amenable relative to Γ_{lk(v)}. Lemma 2.4 applied to the vertex-removal AFP then forces both indices to be 2. Translating the index-two condition back into graph-product combinatorics yields G_0 minus st(v) = {t}, Γ_t ≅ Γ_v ≅ Z_2, and lk_{G_0}(t) = lk_{G_0}(v). Hence the de Rham factor containing v is exactly the edgeless graph on {v,t} with both vertex groups Z_2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.2: Cartan subalgebras force an exceptional join factor
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ = G{Γ_v} be a graph product group over a finite graph G, let Γ act trace-preservingly on a tracial von Neumann algebra P, and put M = P ⋊ Γ. Assume M is a II_1 factor. Let p be a projection in M and let A ⊂ pMp be a Cartan subalgebra. Then there exists a join decomposition G = G_0 o K such that A <_M P ⋊ Γ_{G_0}, and the de Rham join decomposition of G_0 has the restricted form: one distinguished factor may be a clique, while every other de Rham factor is an edgeless two-vertex graph {v_i,w_i} with Γ_{v_i} ≅ Γ_{w_i} ≅ Z_2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Start from Theorem 3.1, obtaining a subgraph G_0 with the restricted de Rham form and A <_M P⋊Γ_{G_0}. By a standard strengthening lemma, invoked as DHI16, Lemma 2.4(3), this can be arranged as A <^s_M P⋊Γ_{G_0}. Choose such G_0 with |V_0| minimal. Compare the de Rham join decomposition of G_0 with the de Rham join decomposition G = H_0 o H_1 o ... o H_l of the whole graph, where H_0 is the clique factor and the H_i for i ≥ 1 are irreducible. The proof groups the de Rham factors of G_0 according to which H_i they lie in. Suppose some non-clique irreducible factor H_i of G contains a vertex v not belonging to the corresponding part of G_0 but whose link inside H_i does not contain all of that part; irreducibility of H_i supplies such a v whenever the inclusion is proper. Then Γ has the noncanonical splitting Γ = Γ_{G minus {v}} *_Γ_{lk(v)} Γ_{st(v)}. Applying the Ioana--Vaes AFP normalizer theorem to M and the Cartan A yields three alternatives: (i) A <^s_M P⋊Γ_{lk(v)}; (ii) pMp <^s_M P⋊Γ_{G minus {v}} or pMp <^s_M P⋊Γ_{st(v)}; or (iii) pMp is co-amenable relative to P⋊Γ_{lk(v)}. In case (i), combine the strong intertwining A <^s_M P⋊Γ_{lk(v)} with A <^s_M P⋊Γ_{G_0}. A transitivity/intersection lemma for intertwinings gives an h in Γ such that A strongly embeds into P⋊(Γ_{lk(v)} ∩ hΓ_{G_0}h^{-1}). The paper then uses AM10, Proposition 3.4, to identify this intersection as a conjugate of Γ_S for some induced subgraph S contained in lk(v) ∩ G_0, and this S is a proper subgraph of G_0. Hence A embeds into P⋊Γ_S, contradicting the minimality of G_0 because S inherits the same restricted de Rham form. In case (ii), Lemma 2.2 would imply that Γ_{G minus {v}} or Γ_{st(v)} has finite index in Γ, which is impossible by graph-product normal forms for a noncanonical splitting. In case (iii), group co-amenability relative to Γ_{lk(v)} and Lemma 2.4 force G minus st(v) to be a single vertex t, with Γ_t ≅ Γ_v ≅ Z_2 and G = {v,t} o lk({v,t}). Since v and t lie in an irreducible de Rham factor H_i, that factor would be precisely the exceptional two-vertex edgeless Z_2-pair, again contradicting the chosen minimal placement of G_0. Thus no such extra vertex v exists, so the subgraph G_0 is a union of whole de Rham join factors of G. Therefore G splits as a join G = G_0 o K, as claimed.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.1: absence of Cartan subalgebras in many graph product group factors
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ = G{Γ_v} be an icc graph product group over a finite graph G. Assume G does not admit a de Rham join decomposition G = G_1 o G_2 o ... o G_k of the exceptional form in which, for every i with 2 ≤ i ≤ k, the factor G_i is the edgeless graph on two vertices {v_i,w_i} and Γ_{v_i} ≅ Γ_{w_i} ≅ Z_2. Then the group von Neumann algebra L(Γ) has no Cartan subalgebra.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume A ⊂ M = L(Γ) is Cartan. Apply Theorem 3.2 with P = C to get a join decomposition G = G_0 o lk(G_0), where G_0 has the restricted exceptional de Rham form, and A <_M L(Γ_{G_0}). Because G is a join of G_0 and its link, the algebra L(Γ_{lk(G_0)}) lies in L(Γ_{G_0})' ∩ M. Passing to relative commutants in the intertwining relation gives L(Γ_{lk(G_0)}) <_M A' ∩ M = A. Since A is abelian, this forces a nonzero corner of L(Γ_{lk(G_0)}) to be amenable, and hence Γ_{lk(G_0)} is amenable. The paper concludes that this contradicts the assumed exclusion of the exceptional de Rham form for G, so no Cartan subalgebra can exist.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 1.2: non-Coxeter-type graph products with a nonedge have no Cartan
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G = (V,E) be a finite simple graph with at least one pair of nonadjacent vertices. Let Γ = G{Γ_v} be an icc graph product group such that |Γ_v| ≥ 3 for every v in V. Then L(Γ) has no Cartan subalgebra.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Because G is not complete and no vertex group has order two, G cannot have a de Rham join decomposition whose non-clique irreducible factors are edgeless two-vertex factors with both vertex groups Z_2. The conclusion is therefore an immediate application of Theorem 1.1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.3: uniqueness of Cartan subalgebra for free ergodic pmp actions under optimal graph restrictions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ = G{Γ_v} be a graph product group over a finite graph G. Assume G does not admit a nontrivial join decomposition G = H o K where either H is a single vertex graph {v}, or H is an edgeless two-vertex graph {v,w} with Γ_v ≅ Γ_w ≅ Z_2. Let Γ act freely and ergodically by probability-measure-preserving transformations on a probability space X, and set M = L∞(X) ⋊ Γ. Then every Cartan subalgebra A of M is unitarily conjugate to L∞(X): there exists u in U(M) such that A = u L∞(X) u*.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Apply Theorem 3.2 with P = L∞(X). Under the stated graph condition, the subgraph G_0 appearing in Theorem 3.2 cannot contain a clique vertex, because that would give a singleton join factor, and cannot contain any non-clique exceptional factor, because that would give an edgeless Z_2-pair join factor. Thus the only possible conclusion is A <_M L∞(X). Since both A and L∞(X) are Cartan subalgebras of the II_1 factor M, Popa's conjugacy criterion, Theorem 2.3, upgrades the intertwining to unitary conjugacy.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.3: graph products of nonamenable C-superrigid groups are C-superrigid
