key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A group Λ is called C-superrigid in the sense used by the paper if, for every trace-preserving action Λ on a tracial von Neumann algebra P, every projection p in M = P ⋊ Λ, and every masa A ⊂ pMp whose normalizer has finite index in pMp, one has A <_M P. Corollary 3.3 states: if Γ = G{Γ_v} is a graph product group such that every vertex group Γ_v is nonamenable and C-superrigid, then Γ is C-superrigid.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by induction on the number of vertices. For one vertex this is the definition. Suppose the result is known for smaller graphs. If G is not star-shaped, Theorem 3.2 gives the conclusion directly because the nonamenability and infiniteness of the vertex groups rule out the Z_2-pair exceptions and the absence of a singleton join factor prevents any nontrivial parabolic target other than the base P. The remaining case is a star-shaped join G = {v} o lk(v), so Γ = Γ_v × Γ_{lk(v)}. Let Γ act trace-preservingly on P, let M = P⋊Γ, and let A ⊂ pMp be a masa with finite-index normalizer. View M as (P⋊Γ_{lk(v)}) ⋊ Γ_v. Since Γ_v is C-superrigid, A <_M N := P⋊Γ_{lk(v)}. As in Theorem 3.1, use CIK13, Proposition 3.6, to transfer a corner of A to a masa B ⊂ eNe with finite-index normalizer and with uAa u* = Be_1 for suitable projections and a unitary. By induction Γ_{lk(v)} is C-superrigid, so B <_N P. A partial isometry implementing B <_N P, together with the support condition sp(E_N(e_1)) = e, gives we_1 ≠ 0 and hence wua A ⊂ P we_1u. Therefore A <_M P, proving the inductive step.

proof_techniques.name: Vertex-removal AFP decomposition over links
proof_techniques.description: For a graph product group, removing a vertex v gives the splitting Γ = Γ_{V minus {v}} *_Γ_{lk(v)} Γ_{st(v)}. The paper repeatedly applies von Neumann algebraic AFP technology to the corresponding crossed product decomposition P⋊Γ = (P⋊Γ_{V minus {v}}) *_{P⋊Γ_{lk(v)}} (P⋊Γ_{st(v)}). The splitting is useful only when it is nondegenerate, which is detected graph-theoretically by the existence of a vertex not adjacent to v.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Graph product von Neumann algebras have analogous full-subalgebra decompositions over link subalgebras. In the original problem, irreducibility and |V| ≥ 3 should help ensure that suitable vertex-removal decompositions are nontrivial, while trace-zero unitaries can play the role of nontrivial reduced letters in normal-form arguments.

proof_techniques.name: Minimal parabolic support for an intertwining obstruction
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 3.1 chooses a full subgraph G_0 of minimal size such that a masa A with finite-index normalizer embeds into P⋊Γ_{G_0}. If an AFP normalizer theorem later forces A or a conjugate corner of A to embed into P⋊Γ_{lk(v)} for a proper link inside G_0, minimality gives an immediate contradiction. This converts analytic intertwining information into combinatorial restrictions on the possible support subgraph.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For proving proper proximality, one can try to assume the existence of a non-proximality obstruction and choose a minimal full subgraph supporting it. Any argument that pushes the obstruction into a link or a proper intersection of full subalgebras would then contradict minimality.

proof_techniques.name: Ioana--Vaes normalizer trichotomy for AFP von Neumann algebras
proof_techniques.description: In the AFP decomposition coming from vertex removal, the paper applies results of Ioana and Vaes to an amenable subalgebra B, typically a masa. The outcome is a trichotomy: B embeds into the amalgam, the normalizer of B embeds into one of the AFP legs, or the normalizer is amenable relative to the amalgam. The proof then eliminates the first two alternatives using minimality and infinite-index arguments, and analyzes the third using group co-amenability.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the original proper proximality statement is attacked through relative amenability or central-state obstructions, the same AFP trichotomy suggests a route: either the obstruction descends to a link subalgebra, is trapped in a leg, or yields relative amenability over the link, all of which may be ruled out by irreducibility and trace-zero reduced-word constructions.

proof_techniques.name: Co-amenability over the amalgam forces index-two exceptional cases
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 2.4 is used as a rigidity statement for nondegenerate AFP groups: if Γ_1 *_Σ Γ_2 is co-amenable relative to Σ, both inclusions Σ < Γ_i have index exactly two. In graph-product terms, applying this to Γ_{V minus {v}} *_Γ_{lk(v)} Γ_{st(v)} forces the complement of st(v) to be a single vertex t and forces Γ_v and Γ_t to be Z_2 with the same link. Thus relative amenability over a link is converted into the specific infinite-dihedral-type exception.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original hypotheses exclude small join decompositions by assuming irreducibility with at least three vertices, and trace-zero unitaries should rule out degenerate one-dimensional behavior. An analogue of Lemma 2.4 for graph product von Neumann algebras could be a key way to show that relative amenability over a link cannot occur.

proof_techniques.name: Infinite-index verification by reduced words
proof_techniques.description: When the proof needs to rule out an embedding P⋊Γ_{G_0} < P⋊Γ_{G_0 minus {v}} or P⋊Γ_{st(v)}, Lemma 2.2 reduces this to showing that the relevant full subgroup has infinite index. The paper uses graph-product normal forms: if t is nonadjacent to v and g in Γ_t, h in Γ_v are nontrivial, then the cosets represented by (gh)^k are distinct modulo the subgroup omitting v, giving infinite index.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In graph product von Neumann algebras, trace-zero unitaries in nonadjacent vertex algebras can generate reduced alternating words with orthogonality properties analogous to distinct cosets. This is likely useful for proving non-containment, non-amenability, or proper proximality relative to link subalgebras.

proof_techniques.name: Strong intertwining plus parabolic subgroup intersections
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 3.2 combines two strong intertwining relations, A <^s_M P⋊Γ_{lk(v)} and A <^s_M P⋊Γ_{G_0}, to force A to intertwine into a crossed product by the intersection Γ_{lk(v)} ∩ hΓ_{G_0}h^{-1}. The paper invokes Antolin--Minasyan's parabolic intersection result to identify this intersection as a conjugate of Γ_S for some induced subgraph S contained in lk(v) ∩ G_0. If S is proper, minimality of G_0 gives a contradiction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A von Neumann algebraic analogue would identify intersections of full graph-product subalgebras, or at least their bimodule supports, with smaller full subalgebras. Such an intersection principle would let one reduce any hypothetical obstruction to a proper link intersection.

proof_techniques.name: Passing to relative commutants in a join decomposition
proof_techniques.description: In the proof of Theorem 1.1, Theorem 3.2 gives a join G = G_0 o lk(G_0) and an embedding A < L(Γ_{G_0}). Since the link subgroup commutes with Γ_{G_0}, L(Γ_{lk(G_0)}) lies in the relative commutant of L(Γ_{G_0}) inside L(Γ). A standard relative-commutant intertwining argument then gives L(Γ_{lk(G_0)}) < A' ∩ M = A, forcing amenability because A is abelian.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Proper proximality is sensitive to direct-product or tensor-product decompositions. This technique emphasizes that irreducibility should prevent a nontrivial commuting link component from surviving as an obstruction, and suggests checking relative commutants of full graph-product subalgebras.

proof_techniques.name: Induction through singleton join factors
proof_techniques.description: Corollary 3.3 handles star-shaped graphs G = {v} o lk(v) by viewing the crossed product as an iterated crossed product by Γ_v and Γ_{lk(v)}. C-superrigidity of the singleton vertex group first pushes a finite-index-normalizer masa into P⋊Γ_{lk(v)}, and the induction hypothesis for the link then pushes it further into P. The same support-projection trick from Theorem 3.1 transfers intertwiners through the corner equivalence.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a proof of proper proximality proceeds by induction on vertices, the star or singleton-join case is the natural tensor-product obstruction to separate. The original irreducibility assumption is designed to avoid this case, but the inductive pattern may still be useful when reducing to links.

proof_techniques.name: Exceptional Z_2-pair as the only coamenable non-clique obstruction
proof_techniques.description: Across Theorems 3.1 and 3.2, the only non-clique de Rham factor not eliminated is an edgeless two-vertex graph whose two vertex groups are both Z_2. This corresponds to the infinite dihedral group Z_2 * Z_2, which is amenable and appears exactly when the AFP over the link has both indices equal to two.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem assumes at least three vertices and irreducibility; this should rule out a graph being only the two-vertex Z_2 exception. If trace-zero unitaries are used to build alternating reduced words, the key is to show that no analogue of this index-two amenable exception remains.

other_useful_info: The paper explicitly cites [CF14] Caspers--Fima for graph products of operator algebras, [AM10] Antolin--Minasyan for structural facts about parabolic subgroups of graph product groups, [Io12] and [Va13] for normalizers in amalgamated free product von Neumann algebras, and [DK-E21] Ding--Kunnawalkam Elayavalli for proper proximality of groups acting on trees. The proof of Theorem 3.2 uses [AM10, Proposition 3.4] in the following form: intersections of full graph-product subgroups, after conjugacy, are again full subgroups over induced subgraphs; specifically Γ_{lk(v)} ∩ hΓ_{G_0}h^{-1} = kΓ_Sk^{-1} for some S contained in lk(v) ∩ G_0. The remarks at the end show optimality of the graph restrictions in Theorem 1.3: for a singleton join factor one can use Γ_w = ℤ^2 ⋊ SL_2(ℤ) and a profinite action to get two non-unitarily-conjugate Cartan subalgebras, and for an edgeless Z_2-pair join factor one uses the infinite dihedral group Z_2 * Z_2 = ℤ ⋊ Z_2 and a profinite action to produce another non-unique Cartan example. No result in this paper directly proves proper proximality for graph product von Neumann algebras of arbitrary tracial vertex algebras; its main value for that problem is the graph-product AFP and link/join technology.
why_relevant: Contains operator-algebraic techniques specialized to von Neumann algebras of graph-product groups, including parabolic-subgroup, relative-amenability, and intertwining arguments. These are relevant background for translating graph-product combinatorics into von Neumann algebraic structural statements.

title: Properly Proximal von Neumann Algebras
overall_summary: This paper defines proper proximality for finite von Neumann algebras using an operator-system compactification S(M), built from the ∥·∥∞,1 closure of compact operators on L2(M). Its core technical apparatus is the use of Magajna strong operator bimodules, boundary pieces, convolution-algebra topologies, and normal-bidual compactifications to make the group notion of proper proximality purely von Neumann algebraic. The paper proves permanence and recognition results, including equivalence between proper proximality of a group Γ and of LΓ, proper proximality of free products of diffuse tracial von Neumann algebras, tensor-product and crossed-product criteria, and deformation/derivation criteria. For the graph-product problem, the most relevant tools are the definition and generator criterion for S(M), the strong-bimodule closure properties of K∞,1, the free-product paradoxical projection argument, the tensor-product theorem for join-type decompositions, and the deformation/boundary-piece criteria that can be adapted to reduced-word decompositions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definitions in Sections 3 and 6: boundary pieces, S_X(M), and proper proximality
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (M, τ) be a finite von Neumann algebra with L2-space L2(M) and Tomita conjugation J. For T in B(L2(M)), define ∥T∥∞,1 as the operator norm of T viewed from the unit ball of M, with operator norm ∥·∥∞, into L1(M). An M-boundary piece is a nonzero hereditary C*-subalgebra X of B(L2(M)) such that M(X) ∩ M and M(X) ∩ JMJ are ultraweakly dense in M and JMJ, where M(X) is the multiplier algebra of X inside B(L2(M)). Define K_X^L(M) as the ∥·∥∞,2 closure of B(L2(M))X; define K_X(M) = (K_X^L(M))* ∩ K_X^L(M); define K_X^{∞,1}(M) as the ∥·∥∞,1 closure of X. The relative operator-system compactification is S_X(M) = {T in B(L2(M)) : [T, JxJ] belongs to K_X^{∞,1}(M) for every x in M}. For X = K(L2(M)), write K^{∞,1}(M) and S(M). If B is a von Neumann subalgebra of M, the inclusion B ⊂ M is properly proximal relative to X if there is no B-central state φ on S_X(M) whose restriction to M is normal. The algebra M is properly proximal if M ⊂ M is properly proximal for X = K(L2(M)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: These are definitions, but several features are proved around them. The hereditary condition and density of multipliers ensure that X behaves like a boundary ideal relative to both the left M-action and the right JMJ-action. The commutator condition defining S_X(M) is designed so that S_X(M) is an operator system containing M. Proper proximality is then expressed as nonexistence of a central state on this compactification, exactly paralleling nonexistence of invariant states on group compactifications.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.1, Corollary 3.2, and Proposition 3.6: ∥·∥∞,1 closure and strong bimodules
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (M, τ) be finite. For x in B(L2(M)), the norm ∥x∥∞,1 satisfies the two-sided estimate ∥x∥∞,1 ≤ inf (∥a∥2^2 + ∥b∥2^2)^{1/2} ∥z∥ (∥c∥2^2 + ∥d∥2^2)^{1/2} ≤ 4∥x∥∞,1, where the infimum is over all z in M2(B(L2(M))) and a,b,c,d in M such that x = (JaJ, b)^* z (JcJ, d)^T. More generally, if M and N are finite von Neumann algebras, X is a subspace of B(L2(N), L2(M)), T_i is a uniformly bounded net in X, and ∥T_i − T∥∞,1 tends to 0, then T lies in the smallest subspace of B(L2(N), L2(M)) containing X that is both a strong M-N bimodule and a strong JMJ-JNJ bimodule. For any M-boundary piece X, K_X^{∞,1}(M) equals the ∥·∥∞,1 closure of K_X(M), and it is a self-adjoint closed strong M-M and JMJ-JMJ bimodule.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The first inequality is a direct Cauchy-Schwarz estimate after writing x as a two-sided factorization through L2(M) ⊕ L2(M). For the reverse inequality, the paper associates to x the normal bilinear form φ(a,b)=⟨xâ, b̂⟩ and applies the noncommutative Grothendieck theorem. Spectral truncations of the Grothendieck vectors produce bounded elements a,b,c,d in M with controlled L2-norms, and the resulting bounded sesquilinear form gives the matrix z. Corollary 3.2 combines this factorization with Magajna duality: functionals continuous for both module topologies separate the strong bimodule closure, so ∥·∥∞,1 limits of uniformly bounded nets are forced into the smallest strong two-sided bimodule. Proposition 3.6 first shows K_X^{∞,1} is also the ∥·∥∞,1 closure of K_X by approximating products S*R with S,R in K_X^L by products from B(L2(M))X; then Corollary 3.2 gives strong M-M and JMJ-JMJ closure.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.8 and Proposition 5.9: square criteria for relative compactness
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be finite, X an M-boundary piece, and H a Hilbert space. For T in B(L2(M), H), the following are equivalent: T belongs to K_X^L(M,H), meaning the ∥·∥∞,2 closure of B(L2(M),H)X; T*T belongs to K_X(M); and T*T belongs to K_X^{∞,1}(M). More generally, if M and N are finite, X and Y are boundary pieces for M and N, and T belongs to B(L2(N),L2(M)), then T belongs to K_{X,Y}(N,M) = K_X(M) B(L2(N),L2(M)) K_Y(N) if and only if T*T belongs to K_Y(N) and TT* belongs to K_X(M), equivalently if T*T belongs to K_Y^{∞,1}(N) and TT* belongs to K_X^{∞,1}(M).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The easy implications follow from definitions and hereditary properties. For the hard implication in Proposition 3.8, if T*T is in K_X^{∞,1}, Proposition 3.6 identifies it with the strong M-M and JMJ-JMJ closure of K_X. Magajna's projection-cutdown characterization then gives projections p_n in M and q_n in JMJ increasing strongly to 1 with p_n q_n T*T q_n p_n in K_X. Hence |T| q_n p_n lies in K_X^L, and another strong-module closure argument yields |T|, hence T, in K_X^L. Proposition 5.9 applies the same idea to both T*T and TT*, using the polar decomposition T = V|T| and writing T as |T*|^{1/2} V |T|^{1/2} in the two-sided ideal K_X B K_Y.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.6: characterization of K_X,Y^{∞,1} by mixing and module-continuous functionals
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M and N be finite von Neumann algebras, and let X ⊂ B(L2(M)) and Y ⊂ B(L2(N)) be boundary pieces. Define K_{X,Y}^{∞,1}(N,M) as the ∥·∥∞,1 closure of X B(L2(N),L2(M)) Y. For T in B(L2(N),L2(M)), the following are equivalent: (1) T belongs to K_{X,Y}^{∞,1}(N,M); (2) T is mixing relative to X×Y, meaning that for the convolution algebra M ⊗Con N^op the map μ ↦ μ(T) is continuous from the X-Y topology to the ultraweak topology on bounded sets; (3) T belongs to the strong M-N and JMJ-JNJ bimodule generated by K_X(M) B(L2(N),L2(M)) K_Y(N); (4) φ(T)=0 for every bounded linear functional φ on B(L2(N),L2(M)) that is continuous for both the M-N and JMJ-JNJ module topologies and vanishes on X B(L2(N),L2(M))Y. In the special case M=N and X=Y with M and JMJ contained in M(X), and for T in M(X), these are also equivalent to vanishing of T under every state in the same module-continuous class that vanishes on X, and to a net criterion using states μ_i in K(L2(M))_J^♮ converging to zero on X.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Equivalence of (1) and (3) is Proposition 3.1 plus Corollary 3.2: ∥·∥∞,1 closure is exactly the relevant strong bimodule closure. Equivalence of (3) and (4) is Hahn-Banach separation in the locally convex topology generated by module-continuous functionals. The implication (1) to (2) uses that the mixing vectors form a strong M-N bimodule and contain X B Y. For (2) to (4), Lemma 5.5 approximates a module-continuous functional φ by a uniformly bounded net in the convolution algebra K(L2(N),L2(M))_J^♮. If φ vanishes on X B Y, the approximating net tends to zero in the X-Y topology, so mixing gives φ(T)=0. The state versions use Jordan decomposition of module-continuous functionals and the fact that positive extensions preserve normality on M and JMJ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.10: mixing correspondences and compact coefficient operators
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M and N be finite von Neumann algebras, X and Y boundary pieces with M, JMJ ⊂ M(X) and N, JNJ ⊂ M(Y), and let H be an M-N correspondence. For a bounded vector ξ in H, let T_ξ: L2(M) → L2(N) be the bounded operator associated to the completely positive coefficient map. The following are equivalent: (1) the bounded vectors ξ such that T_ξ belongs to Y B(L2(M),L2(N)) X are M-N cyclic for H; (2) the dual operator M-N bimodule B_N(L2(N),H) is mixing relative to X×Y; (3) every bounded vector ξ in H satisfies T_ξ belongs to K_{Y,X}(M,N). When X=K(L2(M)) and Y=K(L2(N)), these are also equivalent to the following classical mixing condition: for every sequence u_n in U(N) with u_n tending ultraweakly to 0 and all ξ,η in H, lim_n sup_{x in M, ∥x∥≤1} |⟨x ξ u_n, η⟩|=0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a bounded vector ξ, the paper compares the coefficient of μ(T_ξ*) with the coefficient of μ(L_ξ), where L_ξ: L2(N)→H is right N-modular. This shows T_ξ* is X-Y mixing exactly when L_ξ is. If the cyclic set in (1) is dense, a cutdown-by-projections argument and strong-module closure show every left-bounded vector gives a mixing L_ξ, proving (2). Conversely, (2) implies after projection cutdowns that bounded vectors with T_ξ in Y B X are dense. To obtain (3), apply the already proved equivalence to Connes fusions H⊗_N H and H⊗_M H, so T_ξ*T_ξ and T_ξ T_ξ* lie in the appropriate K^{∞,1} spaces; Proposition 5.9 gives T_ξ in K_{Y,X}. In the compact case, the sequence condition is converted into compactness of T_ξ as a map from N to L1(M), and Lemma 3.9 upgrades this to compactness into L2(M).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.1: generator criterion for proper proximal points
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a finite von Neumann algebra, X an M-boundary piece, V a dual normal M-bimodule, and ξ in V. Then the set M_0 = {x in M : [x,ξ] and [x*,ξ] belong to V_{X-mix}} is a von Neumann subalgebra of M. Consequently, ξ is properly proximal relative to X, meaning [x,ξ] belongs to V_{X-mix} for every x in M, if and only if M_0 is ultraweakly dense in M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: It is straightforward that M_0 is a unital *-subalgebra because V_{X-mix} is an M-bimodule. If x is an ultraweak limit of elements x_i in M_0, take the x_i uniformly bounded by Kaplansky density. Then [x_i,ξ]−[x,ξ]=(x_i−x)ξ−ξ(x_i−x) tends to zero in the M-M module topology. Since V_{X-mix} is a strong M-M bimodule by Proposition 5.1, it is closed for this topology, so [x,ξ] lies in V_{X-mix}; the same argument applies to x*. This proves ultraweak closure.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6.2: equivalent formulations of non-proper-proximality for inclusions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a finite von Neumann algebra, X an M-boundary piece, and B a von Neumann subalgebra of M. The following are equivalent: (1) there exists a B-central state φ on S_X(M) such that φ restricted to M is normal; (2) there exists a nonzero projection p in Z(B) and a B-central state φ on S_X(M) such that φ restricted to pMp is the normalized trace τ(·)/τ(p); (3) there exists a nonzero projection p in Z(B) and an Mp-bimodular unital completely positive map Φ: S_X(M) → ⟨pMp,e_{Bp}⟩, where ⟨pMp,e_{Bp}⟩ is the basic construction; (4) for every normal operator M-system E such that there exists a state φ_0 in the properly proximal part of E^♮ with φ_0 restricted to M equal to τ, there exists a B-central state φ on E whose restriction to M is normal. The inclusion B⊂M is defined to be properly proximal relative to X precisely when these equivalent conditions fail.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equivalence of (1), (2), and (3) is standard: a normal B-central state can be cut by central support in Z(B), and an Mp-bimodular u.c.p. map into the basic construction is obtained by the Stinespring/basic-construction correspondence associated to a B-central state. For (4) implies (1), take E=S_X(M) and φ_0(T)=⟨T1̂,1̂⟩; this vector state is properly proximal because commutators defining S_X(M) are in K_X^{∞,1}. For (1) implies (4), a state φ_0 in E^♮ gives an M-bimodular u.c.p. map Φ_0:E→B(L2(M)). Proper proximality of φ_0 implies the range of Φ_0 lies in S_X(M): testing against convolution algebra elements converts [x,φ_0] being mixing into [Φ_0(T),JxJ] lying in K_X^{∞,1}. Composing Φ_0 with the B-central state on S_X(M) gives the required B-central state on E.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 6.3: monotonicity and intermediate subalgebras
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X and Y be M-boundary pieces with X⊂Y. If B⊂M is properly proximal relative to X, then B⊂M is properly proximal relative to Y. If B⊂Q⊂M and B⊂M is properly proximal relative to X, then Q⊂M is properly proximal relative to X. Also, identifying L2(Q) with e_Q L2(M), the algebra e_Q K_X(M) e_Q is a Q-boundary piece, and if B⊂M is properly proximal relative to X then B⊂Q is properly proximal relative to e_Q K_X(M)e_Q. In particular, if B⊂M is properly proximal in the absolute sense, then B⊂Q is properly proximal and B itself is properly proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The first assertions follow from inclusions K_X^{∞,1}⊂K_Y^{∞,1} and S_X(M)⊂S_Y(M), and from the fact that a Q-central state is in particular B-central or that a B-central obstruction for Q would compose with compression from S_X(M). For the compression statement, e_Q K_X(M)e_Q is hereditary in B(L2(Q)) and contains Q and JQJ in its multiplier algebra because K_X(M) contains M and JMJ in its multiplier algebra. Moreover e_Q K_X^{∞,1}(M)e_Q⊂K_{e_QK_Xe_Q}^{∞,1}(Q), so e_Q S_X(M)e_Q⊂S_{e_QK_Xe_Q}(Q), allowing any obstructing state for B⊂Q to induce one for B⊂M.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6.4: equivalence with group proper proximality
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a discrete group. Let I be a Γ×Γ-invariant proper closed ideal of ℓ∞(Γ) containing c0(Γ), and define S_I(Γ) = {f in ℓ∞(Γ): f−R_t f belongs to I for every t in Γ}. Let X be a boundary piece for LΓ, let I_X be the closed ideal of ℓ∞(Γ) generated by E_{ℓ∞Γ}(X), and let X_I be the LΓ-boundary piece I B(ℓ2Γ) I. Then: (1) if LΓ is properly proximal relative to X, Γ is properly proximal relative to I_X, meaning S_{I_X}(Γ) has no left Γ-invariant state; (2) Γ is properly proximal relative to I if and only if LΓ is properly proximal relative to X_I. In particular, with I=c0(Γ), Γ is properly proximal if and only if LΓ is properly proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (1), if Γ has a left-invariant state on S_{I_X}(Γ), compose it with the diagonal conditional expectation E_{ℓ∞Γ}:B(ℓ2Γ)→ℓ∞Γ. Since E_{ℓ∞Γ} maps K_X^{∞,1}(LΓ) into I_X, it maps S_X(LΓ) into S_{I_X}(Γ). The composed state is LΓ-central because it is Γ-invariant on group generators and normal on LΓ. This contradicts relative proper proximality of LΓ. For (2), if LΓ has a central state on S_{X_I}(LΓ), then S_I(Γ) embeds into S_{X_I}(LΓ) by diagonal multiplication operators, since [M_f,ρ_t]=M_{f−R_tf}ρ_t lies in X_I. Restricting the central state gives a Γ-invariant state on S_I(Γ). The converse follows from (1) because E_{ℓ∞Γ}(X_I)=I.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.6: coamenable subfactors inherit proper proximality
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (M,τ) be a finite factor and let N⊂M be a coamenable subfactor, meaning there is a conditional expectation from the basic construction ⟨M,e_N⟩ onto M. If M is properly proximal, then N is properly proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume N is not properly proximal. Then there is an N-central state φ on S(N) whose restriction to N is τ. Compression by e_N maps S(M) into S(N), giving an N-central state on S(M) that restricts to τ on N. Coamenability of N⊂M and Lemma 6.5 convert an N-central state on any M-system restricting normally to M into an M-central state. Applying this to S(M) contradicts proper proximality of M.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.7: free products of diffuse tracial von Neumann algebras are properly proximal
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M_1 and M_2 be diffuse tracial von Neumann algebras. Then their free product M = M_1 * M_2, with respect to the given traces, is properly proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write L2(M) as C1̂ plus the direct sum of reduced-word spaces H^0_{i_1}⊗⋯⊗H^0_{i_n}, where H^0_i=L2(M_i)⊖C1̂ and adjacent indices differ. Let H_ℓ(1) be C1̂ plus all reduced words not beginning with M_1, and let P be the projection onto H_ℓ(1)^⊥, i.e. onto words beginning with M_1. For z in M_2^0, right multiplication JzJ preserves H_ℓ(1), so [P,JzJ]=0. For z in M_1^0, right multiplication only changes the vacuum boundary, so [P,JzJ] is finite rank. By Lemma 6.1, P lies in S(M). Define Q analogously as the projection onto words beginning with M_2; again Q lies in S(M). Diffuseness supplies orthogonal trace-zero unitaries u_1,u_2 in M_2, yielding u_1*Pu_1 + u_2*Pu_2 ≤ Q, and a trace-zero unitary v in M_1 with v*Qv≤P. If φ were an M-central state on S(M), centrality would imply 2φ(P)≤φ(Q)≤φ(P), hence φ(P)=φ(Q)=0. The remaining projection 1−P−Q is the vacuum projection; centrality together with diffuseness gives φ(1−P−Q)=0 by translating the vacuum projection to infinitely many orthogonal projections of equal φ-mass. Thus φ(1)=0, a contradiction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6.8 and Corollary 6.9: obstruction from conjugation-invariant states and property Gamma
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a finite von Neumann algebra, X an M-boundary piece, and G a subgroup of U(M). Suppose there exists a state φ on B(L2(M)) such that φ restricts to the canonical traces on M and JMJ, φ∘Ad(u)=φ∘Ad(Ju*J) for every u in G, and φ vanishes on X. Then φ restricted to S_X(M) is G''-central, so the inclusion G''⊂M is not properly proximal relative to X. Consequently, if a tracial von Neumann algebra M has property Gamma, then M is not properly proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Because φ is normal on both M and JMJ, it is module-continuous in the sense B(L2(M))_J^♯. Since φ vanishes on X, Theorem 5.6 implies it vanishes on K_X^{∞,1}(M). If T lies in S_X(M) and u in G, then (Ju*J)T(JuJ)−T equals [Ju*J,T](JuJ), which belongs to K_X^{∞,1}(M). Hence φ(Ad(u)(T))=φ(Ad(Ju*J)(T))=φ(T), and the restriction is G-invariant; normality on M upgrades this to G''-centrality. For property Gamma, take trace-zero unitaries u_n asymptotically central in M and let φ be a weak-* limit of vector states T↦⟨T u_n̂,u_n̂⟩. This φ satisfies the hypotheses for X=K(L2(M)).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 6.11: properly proximal algebras exclude diffuse weakly compact regular subalgebras
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be finite and let Q⊂M be a regular von Neumann subalgebra. Let X_Q be the M-boundary piece generated by e_Q B(L2(M)) e_Q under left and right multiplication by M and JMJ. If M is properly proximal relative to X_Q and P⊂M is a weakly compact regular von Neumann subalgebra, then P embeds into Q inside M in Popa's intertwining sense, P ≺_M Q. In particular, if M is properly proximal, then M has no diffuse weakly compact regular von Neumann subalgebras.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Weak compactness of P⊂M gives a state φ on B(L2(M)) that restricts to the traces on M and JMJ, is P-central, and is invariant under Ad(u) versus Ad(Ju*J) for u in the normalizer N_M(P). If P does not intertwine into Q, Popa's criterion gives unitaries u_n in U(P) with ∥E_Q(a u_n b)∥2→0 for all a,b in M. Lemma 6.10 shows that for S,T in the dense span of X_Q, ∥S* u_n T∥∞,1→0. Averaging conjugates of positive generators of X_Q by carefully chosen u_n forces φ to vanish on X_Q. Theorem 6.8 then says N_M(P)''⊂M is not properly proximal relative to X_Q. Since P is regular, N_M(P)''=M, contradicting the hypothesis.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 7.1: subalgebras of biexact crossed products
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a biexact group acting trace-preservingly on an abelian tracial von Neumann algebra B. For every von Neumann subalgebra P⊂B⋊Γ, either the inclusion P⊂B⋊Γ is properly proximal relative to the boundary piece X_B associated with B⊂B⋊Γ, or P has an amenable direct summand.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume P⊂B⋊Γ is not properly proximal relative to X_B. Theorem 6.2 gives, after cutting by a central projection p in P, a p(B⋊Γ)p-bimodular u.c.p. map from S_{X_B}(B⋊Γ) into the basic construction ⟨p(B⋊Γ)p,e_{Pp}⟩. The diagonal embedding ℓ∞Γ⊂B(ℓ2Γ)⊂B(L2B)⊗B(ℓ2Γ) sends S(Γ) Γ-equivariantly into B'∩S_{X_B}(B⋊Γ), so one obtains a u.c.p. map from (B⊗min S(Γ))⋊_f Γ into the basic construction. Biexactness means Γ acts topologically amenably on S(Γ); since B is abelian, (B⊗min S(Γ))⋊_f Γ equals the reduced crossed product and is nuclear. A GNS/normal-support argument then embeds B⋊Γ into an injective von Neumann algebra in a way that yields a Pp-central state on ⟨B⋊Γ,e_B⟩. By Ozawa-Popa's relative amenability criterion, Pp is amenable relative to B; because B is abelian, Pp is amenable.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 8.5 and 8.6: normal-bidual criterion for proper proximal actions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ act trace-preservingly on a separable finite von Neumann algebra M, let X be a Γ-invariant M-boundary piece, and assume Γ acts ergodically on Z(M). Let (B(L2(M))_J^♯)^* be the normal bidual coming from functionals continuous for both the M-M and JMJ-JMJ module topologies, and define eS_X(M) = {T in (B(L2(M))_J^♯)^* : [T,a] belongs to (K_X(M)_J^♯)^* for every a in JMJ}. Then Γ↷M is properly proximal relative to X if and only if there is no Γ-invariant M-central state φ on eS_X(M) with φ|_M=τ. Equivalently, Γ↷M is properly proximal relative to X if and only if the action is not weakly compact and there is no Γ-invariant M-central state φ on eS_X(M) with φ|_M=τ and φ vanishing on (K_X(M)_J^♯)^*.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: One direction is obtained by restricting a state on eS_X(M) along the canonical M-bimodular and JMJ-bimodular map B(L2(M))→(B(L2(M))_J^♯)^*. If the state does not vanish on the K_X part, restriction to B(L2(M)) gives weak compactness; if it does vanish, restriction to S_X(M) is exactly an obstruction to proper proximality. The converse is a technical separation argument. If no such state on the bidual exists, Lemma 8.4 produces finitely many elements T_g in eS_X(M) and group elements g with ∥1+T_0+Σ_g(T_g−α_g(T_g))∥<1/4. Goldstein approximation replaces T_g by bounded operators T_g^i in B(L2(M)). Since commutators [T_g,JxJ] lie in the bidual K_X part, the approximants can be cut down by projections p_n and adjusted using a quasi-central approximate unit F_n in X so that sums Σ E_n T_g^{i(n)} E_n actually lie in S_X(M). If a state on S_X(M) as in the action definition existed, applying it to the resulting strict inequality gives a contradiction after choosing projections of large trace. This upgrades absence of ordinary states to absence of bidual states.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 8.8 and Remark 8.9: tensor-product permanence
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M_1 and M_2 be separable finite von Neumann algebras equipped with trace-preserving actions of a group Γ. Then the diagonal action Γ↷M_1⊗M_2 is properly proximal if and only if each action Γ↷M_i is properly proximal. In particular, for separable II1 factors, M_1⊗M_2 is properly proximal if and only if both M_1 and M_2 are properly proximal. Also, if M is a properly proximal separable II1 factor, then every amplification M^t is properly proximal; and if N⊂M is a finite-index subfactor of separable II1 factors and N is properly proximal, then M is properly proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof reduces to the case where Γ acts ergodically on the centers, since diffuse fixed centers or central summands are handled directly. Suppose the product action is not properly proximal. Lemma 8.6 gives an M_1⊗M_2-central Γ-invariant state φ on the bidual eS(M_1⊗M_2) that vanishes on the compact part. Let A be the weak-* limit in the bidual of an increasing quasi-central approximate unit A_i⊗1 with A_i in K(L2(M_1)). The completely positive map T↦A^{1/2}(1⊗T)A^{1/2} sends S(M_2) into eS(M_1⊗M_2). If φ(A)≠0, composing gives an obstruction to proper proximality of Γ↷M_2. The symmetric construction with 1⊗B_j gives the same for M_1 if φ(B)≠0. If both masses vanish, the complementary map T↦(1−A)^{1/2}(T⊗1)(1−A)^{1/2} yields an obstruction for M_1. Conversely, if Γ↷M_1 is not properly proximal, compress B(L2(M_1⊗M_2)) to L2(M_1) using the projection e_1. Lemma 8.7 gives e_1 S(M_1⊗M_2)e_1⊂S(M_1) and e_1 K^{∞,1}(M_1⊗M_2)e_1⊂K^{∞,1}(M_1). Composing an obstructing state for M_1 with this compression yields an obstructing state for the tensor product; centrality over M_2 follows because commutators with J M_2 J compress into K^{∞,1}(M_1). Amplification and finite-index statements follow because M⊗M ≅ M^t⊗M^{1/t} and the basic construction of a finite-index inclusion is an amplification.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 8.11 and Theorem 8.12: crossed products and properly proximal actions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ act trace-preservingly on a finite von Neumann algebra M. If the crossed product M⋊Γ is properly proximal, then the action Γ↷M is properly proximal. Conversely, if Γ is a countable properly proximal group and Γ↷(M,τ) is a properly proximal trace-preserving action on a separable finite von Neumann algebra, then M⋊Γ is properly proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Proposition 8.11, assume Γ↷M is not properly proximal and take a Γ-invariant M-central state φ on S(M) that is normal on M and vanishes on K^{∞,1}(M). Compress B(L2(M⋊Γ)) to L2(M) by e_M. Lemma 8.7 gives e_M S(M⋊Γ)e_M⊂S(M) and e_M K^{∞,1}(M⋊Γ)e_M⊂K^{∞,1}(M). The state ψ=φ∘Ad(e_M) is M-central and normal on M. For group unitaries u_t, the identity e_M u_t J u_t J = α_t^0 e_M and the Γ-invariance of φ show ψ(u_t T)=ψ(Tu_t) modulo K^{∞,1}; hence ψ is M⋊Γ-central, contradicting proper proximality of M⋊Γ. For Theorem 8.12, assume M⋊Γ is not properly proximal. After reducing to ergodic center, Lemma 8.6 gives a central Γ-invariant bidual state φ vanishing on the compact part. Let A be the weak-* limit of 1⊗A_i, where A_i is an almost Γ-invariant approximate unit in c0(Γ)⊂B(ℓ2Γ). If φ(A)≠0, the map T↦A^{1/2}(T⊗1)A^{1/2} sends S(M) into S(M⋊Γ) and yields an obstruction to proper proximality of the action. If φ(A)=0, the complementary map from S(Γ) into S(M⋊Γ) yields a Γ-invariant state on S(Γ), contradicting proper proximality of Γ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 9.1: malleable-deformation criterion for proper proximal actions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ↷M be a trace-preserving action on a separable finite von Neumann algebra, preserving an M-boundary piece X, and assume Γ↷Z(M) is ergodic. Let Γ↷M̃ be another trace-preserving action and let M⊂M̃ be a trace-preserving Γ-equivariant inclusion. Suppose α_n are trace-preserving Γ-equivariant automorphisms of M̃ with ∥α_n(x)−x∥2→0 for every x in M. If E_M∘α_n belongs to K_X^L(M,L2(M)) for every n, and if Γ↷M is not properly proximal relative to X, then there exists a Γ-invariant M-central state on (JMJ)' ∩ B(L2(M̃)⊖L2(M)) whose restriction to M is τ. Equivalently, if no such state exists, the deformation forces Γ↷M to be properly proximal relative to X.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Define isometries V_n:L2(M)→L2(M̃) by V_n(x̂)=α_n(x)^ for x in M, and u.c.p. maps φ_n:B(L2(M̃))→B(L2(M)) by φ_n(T)=V_n* T V_n. The convergence α_n→id on M gives approximate M-bimodularity estimates in ∥·∥∞,1 for φ_n, and similarly for JMJ. Passing to a point ultraweak limit φ into the normal bidual (B(L2(M))_J^♯)^* gives a map that is exactly M-bimodular and JMJ-bimodular. The hypothesis E_M∘α_n∈K_X^L implies φ(e_M) lies in the bidual compact part (K_X(M)_J^♯)^*. If Γ↷M is not properly proximal, Lemma 8.6 provides a Γ-invariant M-central state on eS_X(M) vanishing on the compact part. Composing this state with φ restricted to (JMJ)'∩B(L2(M̃)) and then cutting by e_M^⊥ gives a Γ-invariant M-central state on the complement L2(M̃)⊖L2(M).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 9.4 and Lemma 9.5: closable derivations with compact resolvent imply proper proximality
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a finite factor, H an M-M correspondence with a real structure, and δ:L2(M)→H a closable real derivation whose domain intersects M in an ultraweakly dense *-algebra. Let A be the C*-algebra generated by D(δ)∩M. Put Δ=δ*δ and ζ_α=(α/(α+Δ))^{1/2}. Suppose (M^op)'∩B(H) has no A-central state ψ with ψ|_A=τ. If X is an M-boundary piece and ζ_α belongs to K_X^L(M,L2(M)) for every α>0, then M is properly proximal relative to X. Moreover, if H is weakly contained in the coarse M-M correspondence and such an A-central state on (M^op)'∩B(H) exists, then M is amenable.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Proposition 9.4, define V_α:L2(M)→L2(M)⊕H by V_α(x̂)=ζ_α(x)⊕α^{-1/2}δζ_α(x). This is an isometry because ζ_α and δ satisfy the resolvent identity. The standard deformation estimates for closable derivations, refined in Lemma 9.3 for elements of D(δ)∩M, show V_α is asymptotically M-bimodular over A and asymptotically JMJ-bimodular. The maps φ_α(T)=V_α*T V_α have a point ultraweak cluster point φ into (B(L2(M))_J^♯)^*. Since the L2(M)-corner of V_α is ζ_α and ζ_α∈K_X^L, φ sends the projection onto L2(M) into the bidual compact part. If M were not properly proximal relative to X, a central bidual state vanishing on that compact part would compose with φ and the projection onto H to give an A-central state on (M^op)'∩B(H), contradicting the hypothesis. Lemma 9.5 uses Day's trick: an A-central state on (M^op)'∩B(H) gives almost A-central vectors in H⊗_M H. If H is weakly contained in the coarse correspondence, these vectors imply Connes' norm criterion ∥Σ u_k⊗Ju_kJ∥≥n for arbitrary unitaries u_k in M after approximating them by ζ_α(u_k) in A, hence M is amenable.

proof_techniques.name: Operator-system compactification S(M) and central-state obstruction
proof_techniques.description: The paper replaces the group compactification S(Γ) by the operator system S(M) = {T∈B(L2(M)) : [T,JxJ]∈K^{∞,1}(M) for all x∈M}. Proper proximality is exactly the absence of an M-central state on this operator system whose restriction to M is normal. The relative version S_X(M), built from a boundary piece X, permits localization to specific large-scale parts of L2(M).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A proof for graph products can aim to construct enough projections or operators in S(M), indexed by reduced graph-product words, so that any hypothetical M-central state satisfies contradictory inequalities. The trace-zero unitary hypothesis is naturally suited to producing equal-mass orthogonal translates under centrality.

proof_techniques.name: Checking commutators on generators via strong bimodule closure
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 6.1 shows that for a fixed operator or vector ξ, the set of x∈M for which [x,ξ] is relatively compact/mixing is automatically a von Neumann subalgebra. Thus, to prove T∈S_X(M), it suffices to verify [T,JxJ]∈K_X^{∞,1} for x in any ultraweakly generating *-subalgebra. This depends crucially on K_X^{∞,1} being a strong M-M and JMJ-JMJ bimodule.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Graph product von Neumann algebras are generated by the vertex algebras M_v. One can try to define projections from the reduced-word decomposition and verify their right-commutators only for x in each vertex algebra; Lemma 6.1 then upgrades the verification to all of M.

proof_techniques.name: Reduced-word paradoxical projection argument
proof_techniques.description: In Proposition 6.7, the free product L2-space is decomposed into reduced-word subspaces, and P,Q are projections onto words beginning with the first or second free factor. These projections lie in S(M) because right multiplication by letters either preserves the relevant word sector or changes only a finite-dimensional vacuum boundary. Diffuse unitaries in the opposite factor produce orthogonal translates with inequalities such as 2φ(P)≤φ(Q)≤φ(P), contradicting any central state.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Graph products also have a reduced-word Hilbert-space decomposition, with word sectors governed by commutation relations in the graph. The same strategy suggests defining sector projections associated to subsets of vertices, proving they lie in S(M), and using irreducibility plus trace-zero vertex unitaries to force paradoxical inequalities.

proof_techniques.name: Boundary-piece localization and monotonicity
proof_techniques.description: The paper systematically works relative to hereditary C*-subalgebras X⊂B(L2(M)) that encode boundary pieces. Proper proximality relative to a smaller boundary piece implies proper proximality relative to a larger one, and proper proximality descends to intermediate subalgebras after compressing the boundary piece. This lets one prove stronger relative statements and then pass to absolute proper proximality.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For graph products, a useful boundary piece may come from subgraphs, links, stars, or word sectors. One can first prove relative proper proximality against a graph-theoretic boundary piece, then use monotonicity or compression to obtain the absolute statement.

proof_techniques.name: Normal-bidual approximate-unit splitting
proof_techniques.description: For actions, tensor products, and crossed products, the paper passes from B(L2(M)) to the normal bidual (B(L2(M))_J^♯)^*. Approximate units in compact ideals or c0(Γ) have weak-* limits A in this bidual. A central state φ is then split according to φ(A) and φ(1−A), and each case yields an obstruction for one component. This method is the engine behind tensor-product and crossed-product permanence.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a graph product can be decomposed along a vertex, star, link, or join into a tensor/free/crossed-product-like structure, an approximate-unit split may isolate the part supported near a subgraph from the complementary word boundary.

proof_techniques.name: Malleable deformation criterion
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 9.1 turns a malleable deformation α_n of an extension M⊂M̃ into proper proximality when the compressed deformation E_M∘α_n lands in a relative compact ideal K_X^L and when the complement L2(M̃)⊖L2(M) admits no Γ-invariant M-central state. The proof uses the isometries V_n(x̂)=α_n(x)^, whose associated u.c.p. maps become bimodular in the bidual limit, while the compactness of E_M∘α_n kills the original L2(M) corner.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Graph product von Neumann algebras often admit word-length or free-product-type deformations in related literature. If such a deformation has compactness relative to a graph boundary and the complementary correspondence is nonamenable/mixing, Proposition 9.1 gives a direct route to proper proximality.

proof_techniques.name: Closely controlled derivation/resolvent deformations
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 9.4 uses a closable real derivation δ into an M-M correspondence H. The resolvents ζ_α=(α/(α+δ*δ))^{1/2} act like compact approximations; if they belong to K_X^L and the correspondence has no central state on (M^op)'∩B(H), then M is properly proximal. Lemma 9.5 supplies a common way to rule out such central states when H is weakly contained in the coarse correspondence and M is nonamenable.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A graph product may have a natural length derivation or cocycle into a correspondence built from adding/removing letters. Showing compactness of its resolvent and nonamenability of the associated correspondence would allow this criterion to bypass a direct central-state contradiction.

proof_techniques.name: Weak compactness and intertwining obstruction
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 6.11 combines proper proximality with Ozawa-Popa weak compactness. A weakly compact regular subalgebra produces a state on B(L2(M)) with strong invariance properties; if it fails to intertwine into a chosen subalgebra Q, unitaries make that state vanish on the boundary piece X_Q. Theorem 6.8 then converts this state into an obstruction to proper proximality.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: After proving proper proximality of graph products, this gives structural consequences. Conversely, in an inductive proof, one might use the contrapositive to identify where any hypothetical central state would have to concentrate relative to subgraph algebras.

proof_techniques.name: Tensor-product criterion for join decompositions
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 8.8 proves that proper proximality of tensor products of separable II1 factors is exactly componentwise proper proximality. The proof handles central-state obstructions by cutting with approximate units in one tensor factor and examining where the obstructing state places mass.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Graph products over joins decompose as tensor products of graph products over the join factors. The irreducibility hypothesis in the original problem is precisely a no-join condition, and Theorem 8.8 is the natural result for handling or excluding reducible join cases in an induction.

other_useful_info: The paper itself does not contain a theorem on graph product von Neumann algebras. Its free-product result, Proposition 6.7, assumes both free factors are diffuse; the original graph-product problem assumes only that each vertex algebra has a trace-zero unitary, so a graph-product proof needs an additional reduced-word or graph-boundary argument beyond this proposition. The paper notes that proper proximality of groups is equivalent to proper proximality of their group von Neumann algebras, and cites BIP21, IPR19, HHL20, DE21 for group-level sources of proper proximality; these may be relevant if some graph-product von Neumann algebras can be compared to group graph products. The irreducibility condition in the original problem matches the usual obstruction to a graph product decomposing as a tensor product over a join, and the tensor-product theorem here is the key citable tool for such decompositions.
why_relevant: This is the foundational paper defining proper proximality for finite von Neumann algebras via the operator-system compactification S(M). It also proves key examples and criteria, including free-product, crossed-product, relative-boundary, tensor-product, and malleable-deformation tools that are directly relevant to graph-product von Neumann algebras.

title: Structure of relatively bi-exact group von Neumann algebras
overall_summary: The paper develops a von Neumann algebraic bidual method for upgrading proper proximality relative to a boundary piece into a direct-sum decomposition whose summands are either fully properly proximal or amenable relative to the subalgebras generating the boundary piece. The core technical input is a computation inside the normal bidual of B(L^2 M), together with a construction of u.c.p. maps from basic constructions ⟨M,e_{M_i}⟩ into the relative small-at-infinity bidual when the inclusions M_i ⊂ M are mixing and admit bounded Pimsner-Popa bases. Its main upgrading theorem, Theorem 1.1, is directly relevant to any proof strategy that first establishes proper proximality relative to link/star/parabolic subalgebras and then uses irreducibility or malnormality-type properties to rule out relative amenability to those subalgebras. The paper also proves that relatively bi-exact groups with almost malnormal peripheral subgroups have von Neumann algebras whose subalgebras satisfy such a decomposition, and applies the mechanism to free products.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 2.1 and group example: bounded Pimsner-Popa bases
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let N ⊂ M be an inclusion of separable finite von Neumann algebras. A Pimsner-Popa basis for M over N is a family {m_j}_{j∈J} ⊂ M such that E_N(m_j^* m_k)=δ_{j,k} p_j for projections p_j∈N, and L^2(M)=⊕_{j∈J} m_j L^2(N), equivalently every x∈M has a unique expansion x=Σ_j m_j E_N(m_j^* x). The inclusion N ⊂ M is said to admit a bounded Pimsner-Popa basis if such a basis can be chosen with sup_j ||m_j||<∞. In the group case N=LΛ ⊂ LΓ=M for a subgroup Λ<Γ, choosing left coset representatives Γ=⊔_j t_jΛ gives a bounded basis m_j=λ_{t_j}, consisting of unitaries.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The definition is recorded for later use. In the group case, ℓ^2(Γ) decomposes as the orthogonal direct sum of the subspaces λ_{t_j}ℓ^2(Λ). The conditional expectation E_{LΛ} kills group elements outside Λ, so E_{LΛ}(λ_{t_j}^*λ_{t_k})=0 for j≠k and equals 1 for j=k. Thus the coset representatives give the required orthogonal right LΛ-module decomposition and the basis is uniformly bounded because each element is unitary.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.2: Popa intertwining-by-bimodules criterion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (M,τ) be a tracial von Neumann algebra, let P ⊂ pMp and Q ⊂ M be von Neumann subalgebras, with p a projection in M. The following are equivalent. First, there exist projections p_0∈P and q_0∈Q, a ∗-homomorphism θ:p_0Pp_0→q_0Qq_0, and a nonzero partial isometry v∈q_0Mp_0 such that θ(x)v=vx for every x∈p_0Pp_0. Second, there is no sequence of unitaries u_n∈U(P) such that ||E_Q(x^* u_n y)||_2→0 for all x,y∈pM. When these equivalent conditions hold, the notation P≺_M Q is used, meaning that a corner of P embeds into Q inside M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites this standard theorem of Popa and does not reprove it. Its operational use is the second criterion: to prove P does not intertwine into Q, one constructs unitaries in P whose Q-expectations vanish after multiplication on the left and right by arbitrary elements of M. Conversely, absence of such a vanishing sequence produces a finite P-Q subbimodule and hence the partial-isometry implementation in the first condition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2.3 / [OP10, Theorem 2.1]: state criterion for relative amenability
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let P,Q ⊂ M be von Neumann subalgebras of a finite von Neumann algebra (M,τ), and let ⟨M,e_Q⟩ be the basic construction generated by M and the Jones projection e_Q:L^2(M)→L^2(Q). The paper recalls that P is amenable relative to Q inside M iff there exists a sequence ξ_n∈L^2(⟨M,e_Q⟩) such that ⟨xξ_n,ξ_n⟩→τ(x) for every x∈M and ||yξ_n−ξ_n y||_2→0 for every y∈P. Equivalently, P is amenable relative to Q inside M iff there exists a P-central state Ω on ⟨M,e_Q⟩ whose restriction to M is normal and which is faithful on Z(P′∩M).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This criterion is cited from Ozawa-Popa and is not proved in the paper. The paper repeatedly uses the state formulation in the following way: once a P-central state on a basic construction is constructed, its support on Z(P′∩M) gives a central summand of P that is amenable relative to Q. In Theorem 1.1, the constructed state need not be faithful on all of Z(P′∩M), so the support projection of its restriction to the relative commutant center is taken to isolate the relative amenability summand.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 2.3: compactness consequence of mixing inclusions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a diffuse finite von Neumann algebra and N ⊂ M a diffuse von Neumann subalgebra. The inclusion N ⊂ M is mixing if L^2(M⊖N) is mixing as an N-N bimodule, equivalently if for any sequence of unitaries u_n∈U(N) converging weakly to 0 one has ||E_N(x u_n y)||_2→0 for all x,y∈M⊖N. When M and N are diffuse, the paper notes that one may replace such sequences of unitaries by arbitrary weakly-null sequences in N. If N ⊂ M is mixing, then for x,y∈M with at least one of x,y lying in M⊖N, the operator e_N x J y J e_N is compact as a map from the normed space M to L^2(M). Equivalently, for arbitrary x,y∈M, the difference e_N x J y J e_N − e_N E_N(x) J E_N(y) J e_N is compact in this sense. The paper records examples: M_1 and M_2 are mixing subalgebras of the tracial free product M_1∗M_2 when the factors are diffuse, and LΛ ⊂ LΓ is mixing when Λ<Γ is almost malnormal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The compactness assertion follows from the mixing condition: a bounded sequence in N that is weakly null is sent by the coefficient map a↦E_N(xay) to a norm-null sequence whenever x or y has zero N-expectation. This is the usual compactness criterion for such bimodule coefficient maps in the diffuse setting. The examples are cited from Jolissaint for free products and Boutonnet-Carderi for almost malnormal subgroup inclusions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.2: boundary piece generated by a join of commuting Jones projections
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a finite von Neumann algebra and let M_i ⊂ M, i=1,...,n, be von Neumann subalgebras such that the Jones projections e_{M_i}:L^2(M)→L^2(M_i) pairwise commute. Let e_0=∨_{i=1}^n e_{M_i}. Let X be the hereditary C∗-subalgebra of B(L^2 M) generated by operators x J y J e_0 with x,y∈M. Let Y be the hereditary C∗-subalgebra of B(L^2 M) generated by all x J y J e_{M_i}, with x,y∈M and i=1,...,n. Then X=Y.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Since 0≤e_{M_i}≤e_0, each e_{M_i} lies in X, hence Y⊂X. Conversely, because the projections e_{M_i} commute, finite joins can be written using sums and meets: e_i∨e_j=e_i+e_j−e_i∧e_j, and e_i∧e_j≤e_i. Since hereditary C∗-subalgebras contain positive suboperators dominated by their positive elements, this shows e_0=∨_i e_{M_i} lies in Y. Because M and JMJ lie in the multiplier algebra of Y, xJyJ e_0 also lies in the closed left ideal generated by Y for all x,y∈M, giving X⊂Y.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3: approximate unit for a subalgebra-generated boundary piece
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the setting of Lemma 3.2, let e_0=∨_{i=1}^n e_{M_i} and let X be the M-boundary piece generated by x J y J e_0, x,y∈M. Let F range over finite subsets of U(M), ordered by inclusion, and define e_F=∨_{u,v∈F} u J v J e_0 J v^* J u^*. Then {e_F}_F is an approximate unit for X.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Each e_F is in X because it is dominated by the finite sum of the conjugates uJvJ e_0 Jv^*Ju^*. If 1∈F then e_0 e_F=e_0, and if u_0,v_0∈F then e_F(u_0Jv_0J e_0)=u_0Jv_0J e_0; similarly right multiplication by e_F fixes the corresponding generators. Since every element of M is a linear combination of unitaries, this verifies that the net acts as a two-sided approximate unit on the generators of the hereditary C∗-algebra and hence on all of X.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Remark 3.4: induced boundary pieces and permanence observations
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M and Q be finite von Neumann algebras, let X be an M-boundary piece, and let N ⊂ pMp be a von Neumann subalgebra for a nonzero projection p∈M. Define E_N=Ad(e_N)∘Ad(pJpJ):B(L^2 M)→B(L^2 N). The paper recalls that E_N(K_X(M))⊂B(L^2 N) forms an N-boundary piece, called the induced N-boundary piece and denoted X_N. It also records: if N is properly proximal relative to X inside M, then zN is properly proximal relative to X inside M for every nonzero central projection z of N; if N is properly proximal relative to X inside M, then N has no amenable direct summand; and proper proximality is stable under direct sums, so every finite von Neumann algebra Q has a maximal properly proximal direct summand Qf for some central projection f∈Z(Q).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The induced boundary-piece assertion is cited from [DKEP22, Remark 6.3]. For central summands, a forbidden central state for zN can be composed with compression by z to produce one for N. For the no-amenable-direct-summand assertion, if qN were amenable for a nonzero central q, one could take a qN-central state on B(L^2(qN)) and compose with Ad(q)∘Ad(e_N) to obtain an N-central state on the relevant small-at-infinity operator system, normal on M, contradicting relative proper proximality. Stability under direct sums gives the maximal properly proximal summand by taking the supremum of central projections supporting properly proximal direct summands.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.5: bidual formulation of relative proper proximality
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra and X an M-boundary piece. For a C∗-subalgebra A⊂B(L^2 M) with M and JMJ in its multiplier algebra, let A_J^♯ be the subspace of A^∗ consisting of functionals whose left and right M-actions and JMJ-actions are separately normal; then (A_J^♯)^∗ is a von Neumann algebra. Define the bidual relative small-at-infinity operator system by ~S_X(M)={T∈(B(L^2M)_J^♯)^∗ : [T,a]∈(K_X(M)_J^♯)^∗ for every a∈JMJ}. Then M is properly proximal relative to X iff there is no M-central state φ on ~S_X(M) whose restriction to M is normal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The result is cited from [DKEP22, Lemma 8.5]. The point is that the usual operator system S_X(M) can be replaced by a normal bidual model without changing the existence of central states normal on M. One direction restricts a central state on the bidual to the original operator system. The other direction passes from a central state on S_X(M) to a weak∗ cluster/extension in the normal bidual, using separability and the normal-part construction so that commutators with JMJ land in the bidual of K_X(M).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.7: approximate units identify the identity in the normal bidual
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a finite von Neumann algebra and X an M-boundary piece. Let X_0⊂K_X(M) be a C∗-subalgebra with approximate unit {e_n}_{n∈I}. Assume X_0 is dense in K_X^{∞,1}(M) for the ||·||_{∞,1}-norm and that the canonical image ι(e_n)∈B(L^2M)^{∗∗} commutes with the normal support projection p_nor for every n. Then any weak∗ limit of ι_nor(e_n)=p_nor ι(e_n) p_nor is the identity of the von Neumann algebra (K_X(M)_J^♯)^∗.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The image ι_nor(K_X(M)) is weak∗ dense in (K_X(M)_J^♯)^∗, and functionals in K_X(M)_J^♯ are continuous for the ||·||_{∞,1}-topology; hence ι_nor(X_0) is weak∗ dense. If e is a weak∗ limit of ι_nor(e_n), then for T∈X_0 one has e ι_nor(T)=lim_n p_nor ι(e_n T)p_nor=ι_nor(T), and similarly ι_nor(T)e=ι_nor(T). By weak∗ density, e acts as the identity on all of (K_X(M)_J^♯)^∗.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.8: Jones projections commute with the normal support projection
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let N ⊂ M be an inclusion of finite von Neumann algebras, and let e_N∈B(L^2M) be the orthogonal projection onto L^2(N). Let p_nor∈B(L^2M)^{∗∗} be the central support projection defining the normal part for the M and JMJ actions. Then ι(e_N) commutes with p_nor.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Represent B(L^2M)^{∗∗} on a Hilbert space H. A vector ξ lies in the range of p_nor exactly when the vector states x↦⟨ι(x)ξ,ξ⟩ are normal on both M and JMJ. If ξ is in this range, then the vector state associated with ι(e_N)ξ is x↦⟨ι(E_N(x))ξ,ξ⟩ on M, hence normal; the same argument works for JMJ. Thus ι(e_N)p_nor has range in p_norH, giving ι(e_N)p_nor=p_norι(e_N)p_nor. Taking adjoints yields commutation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.9: mixing and a bounded basis decompose q_K^⊥ q_X
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let N ⊂ M be a mixing inclusion of finite von Neumann algebras admitting a Pimsner-Popa basis {m_k}_{k≥0} with m_k∈M. Let X_N be the M-boundary piece associated with N, i.e. generated by xJyJ e_N for x,y∈M. Let q_K be the identity of (K(L^2M)_J^♯)^∗ and q_{X_N} the identity of (K_{X_N}(M)_J^♯)^∗. With the notation of the paper, define p_{k,l}=q_K^⊥ ι_nor(m_k Jm_l^*J e_N Jm_lJ m_k^*) in the normal bidual. Then the p_{k,l} are pairwise orthogonal projections and their orthogonal sum satisfies Σ_{k,l} p_{k,l}=q_K^⊥ q_{X_N}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof first uses mixing to obtain the compactness relation e_N xJyJ e_N − e_N E_N(x)J E_N(y)J e_N ∈ K(M) in the sense of compact maps from M to L^2(M). After multiplying by q_K^⊥, compact terms vanish. Combining this with the Pimsner-Popa orthogonality E_N(m_k^*m_{k′})=δ_{k,k′}q_k shows that the p_{k,l} multiply as p_{k,l}p_{k′,l′}=0 unless k=k′ and l=l′, and are projections. Next, let A be the C∗-algebra generated by {m_k a : a∈N,k≥0}, and let X_0 be the hereditary C∗-subalgebra generated by xJyJ e_N with x,y∈A. The basis expansion makes A weakly dense in M, and the paper checks that X_0 is ||·||_{∞,1}-dense in K_{X_N}^{∞,1}(M), hence ι_nor(X_0) is weak∗ dense in the bidual. Finally, on dense generators such as m_k Jm_lJ aJbJ e_N and their adjoints, the sum Σ_{k,l}p_{k,l} acts as the identity after q_K^⊥; therefore it equals q_K^⊥ q_{X_N}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.10: bidual identity for a boundary piece generated by finitely many commuting subalgebras
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a finite von Neumann algebra and M_i ⊂ M, i=1,...,n, von Neumann subalgebras with pairwise commuting Jones projections e_{M_i}. Let X be the M-boundary piece associated with the family {M_i}_{i=1}^n, and let X_i be the boundary piece associated with M_i alone. Let q_X be the identity of (K_X(M)_J^♯)^∗ and q_i the identity of (K_{X_i}(M)_J^♯)^∗. Then q_X=∨_{i=1}^n q_i.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The inequality q_X≥q_i is immediate because each X_i is contained in X. For the converse, choose an increasing sequence of finite sets F_n⊂U(M) whose union generates M as a von Neumann algebra, and put e_n=∨_{u,v∈F_n}uJvJ(∨_i e_{M_i})Jv^*Ju^*. By Lemma 3.3 these form an approximate unit for X, and by Lemma 3.7 their images ι_nor(e_n) converge weak∗ to q_X. But for each n, ι_nor(e_n)≤∨_i q_i because e_n is the join of finite conjugates lying in the individual boundary pieces. Hence q_X≤∨_i q_i.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.11: compression to a subalgebra on the bidual small-at-infinity systems
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a finite von Neumann algebra, let N ⊂ pMp be a von Neumann subalgebra for a nonzero projection p∈M, and define E=Ad(e_N)∘Ad(pJpJ):B(L^2M)→B(L^2N). The paper proves that E sends the absolute small-at-infinity operator system S(M) into S(N). Moreover, there is a u.c.p. map ~E:~S(M)→~S(N) between the corresponding bidual small-at-infinity systems such that ~E restricted to M agrees with the conditional expectation from M onto N.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a∈N, the relation pJpJ e_N J_NaJ_N=JaJ pJpJ e_N gives [E(T),J_NaJ_N]=E([T,JaJ]). If T∈S(M), then [T,JaJ] lies in K^{∞,1}(M), and E is ||·||_{∞,1}-continuous, so E([T,JaJ]) lies in K^{∞,1}(N); hence E(T)∈S(N). On preduals, E^∗ sends functionals separately normal for N and JNJ to functionals separately normal for M and JMJ, and similarly for the compact ideals. Taking adjoints gives the u.c.p. map on the bidual systems. On M, the formula is exactly compression by e_N, i.e. the conditional expectation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.1: upgrading relative proper proximality to proper proximality plus relative amenability summands
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a diffuse finite von Neumann algebra. Let M_i ⊂ M, i=1,...,n, be diffuse von Neumann subalgebras such that the Jones projections e_{M_i} pairwise commute; assume each inclusion M_i ⊂ M admits a bounded Pimsner-Popa basis and is mixing. Let p∈M be a projection and let A ⊂ pMp be a von Neumann subalgebra. Let X be the M-boundary piece associated with the family {M_i}_{i=1}^n. If A is properly proximal relative to X inside M, then there exist projections f_0∈Z(A) and f_i∈Z(A′∩pMp), i=1,...,n, such that Af_0 is properly proximal, Af_i is amenable relative to M_i inside M for every i=1,...,n, and Σ_{i=0}^n f_i=p.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let f∈Z(A) be chosen so that Af^⊥ is the maximal properly proximal direct summand of A. If f=0, take f_0=p and there is nothing to prove. Otherwise Af is still properly proximal relative to X, has no amenable direct summand, and has no properly proximal direct summand. By the bidual characterization, there is an Af-central state μ on ~S(Af) normal on Af; by a maximality argument μ may be chosen faithful on Z(Af). Use Lemma 3.11 to get ~E:~S(M)→~S(Af), and set φ=μ∘~E. Then φ is Af-central and φ|_{fMf} is faithful normal. Let q_K be the identity of the compact bidual and q_X the identity of the X-boundary bidual. If φ(q_K)>0, restricting to the compact bidual gives an Af-central state on B(L^2M), hence an amenable direct summand of Af, contradiction. If φ(q_X^⊥)>0, normalizing φ∘Ad(q_X^⊥) gives an Af-central state on ~S_X(M) normal on fMf, contradicting relative proper proximality. Thus φ(q_X q_K^⊥)=1. By Lemma 3.10, q_X=∨_i q_i, where q_i is the identity for the boundary piece generated by M_i; hence some q_i has positive φ-mass after q_K^⊥. For such an i, the key claim constructs a u.c.p. map Φ_i:⟨M,e_{M_i}⟩→q_K^⊥ q_i ~S(M) q_i satisfying Φ_i(x)=q_K^⊥q_i x for all x∈M. To prove the claim, take a bounded Pimsner-Popa basis {m_k} for M over M_i. Define finite-rank cutdowns ψ_n on ⟨M,e_{M_i}⟩ by ψ_n(T)=(Σ_{k≤n}m_k e_{M_i}m_k^*)T(Σ_{l≤n}m_l e_{M_i}m_l^*). On the algebra A_0=span{m_k a e_{M_i}m_l^*:a∈M_i}, define a map φ_0 using the bidual projection e_i^b=Σ_k ι_nor(Jm_kJ e_{M_i} Jm_k^*J) by φ_0(m_r a e_{M_i}m_l^*)=q_K^⊥ι_nor(m_r a)e_i^b ι_nor(m_l^*). Mixing gives the crucial identity q_K^⊥ e_i^b ι_nor(x)e_i^b=q_K^⊥ι_nor(E_{M_i}(x))e_i^b, because all off-expectation terms are compact and disappear after q_K^⊥; this makes φ_0 a ∗-homomorphism. Boundedness of the basis is used in the estimate |⟨φ_0(Σ_r m_{k_r}a_r e_{M_i}m_{l_r}^*)ξ,η⟩|≤(sup_k||m_k||^2)||Σ_r m_{k_r}a_r e_{M_i}m_{l_r}^*||, obtained using pairwise orthogonal projections coming from mixing, so φ_0 extends by norm continuity. A further computation with unitaries in M_i shows φ_0(A_0) commutes with JMJ modulo the compact bidual, hence lies in q_K^⊥~S(M). Taking weak∗ cluster points of φ_0∘ψ_n gives Φ_i, and Lemma 3.9 gives Φ_i(x)=q_K^⊥q_i x on M. Now ν_i=φ∘Φ_i is an Af-central state on ⟨M,e_{M_i}⟩, normal on fMf after normalization when φ(q_iq_K^⊥)>0. Let f_i be the support projection of ν_i restricted to Z((Af)′∩fMf); by the relative amenability criterion, Af_i is amenable relative to M_i. Repeating for all i with positive mass gives projections f_i. Finally, φ(q_i f_i^⊥)=φ(Φ_i(f_i^⊥))=ν_i(f_i^⊥)=0, so φ(∨_i f_i)≥φ(∨_i q_i f_i)=φ(∨_i q_i)=1. Since φ|_{fMf} is faithful, ∨_i f_i=f. Orthogonalizing these central relative-commutant projections and setting f_0=f^⊥ gives the desired sum decomposition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.12: relatively bi-exact groups give relative proper proximality for subalgebras without amenable summands
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a nonamenable countable group that is bi-exact relative to a finite family of subgroups {Λ_i}_{i∈I}. Let M=LΓ, and let X be the M-boundary piece associated with the family {LΛ_i}_{i∈I}. If A ⊂ pMp is a von Neumann subalgebra, for a nonzero projection p∈M, and A has no amenable direct summand, then A is properly proximal relative to the induced A-boundary piece X_A obtained from X by compression to A.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Embed ℓ^∞(Γ) diagonally into B(ℓ^2Γ). Under this embedding, the relative ideal c_0(Γ,{Λ_i}) is sent into the boundary piece X, and the group-level relative small-at-infinity algebra S_X(Γ) embeds Γ-equivariantly into S_X(M). Therefore there is a ∗-homomorphism S_X(Γ)⋊_rΓ→B(ℓ^2Γ) whose image lies in S_X(M). If A were not properly proximal relative to X_A, the paper uses the boundary-piece formalism to obtain a nonzero central projection p_0∈Z(A) and an Ap_0-bimodular u.c.p. map from S_{X_A}(A) to Ap_0. Composing the crossed-product map with compression to A and then with this u.c.p. map gives a u.c.p. map S_X(Γ)⋊_rΓ→Ap_0, and hence a state from the vector state of p_0. In the GNS representation of this state, a Boutonnet-Carderi argument produces a projection q of full state mass and a normal unital ∗-homomorphism LΓ→qπ(S_X(Γ)⋊_rΓ)″q extending the regular representation. Relative bi-exactness means Γ acts amenably on the relative compactification, so S_X(Γ)⋊_rΓ is nuclear. Nuclearity extends the above representation to a u.c.p. map from B(ℓ^2Γ). Composing with the GNS state yields an Ap_0-central state on B(ℓ^2Γ), showing Ap_0 is amenable, contradicting the assumption that A has no amenable direct summand.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2: subalgebra decomposition for relatively bi-exact groups with almost malnormal subgroups
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a countable group with a finite family of almost malnormal subgroups {Λ_i}_{i=1}^n, meaning each Λ_i satisfies gΛ_i g^{-1}∩Λ_i finite for all g∈Γ\Λ_i. Suppose Γ is bi-exact relative to {Λ_i}_{i=1}^n. Then for every von Neumann subalgebra A⊂LΓ there exist a projection p∈Z(A) and projections p_i∈Z((Ap)′∩pLΓp), i=1,...,n, such that ∨_{i=1}^n p_i=p, each Ap_i is amenable relative to LΛ_i inside LΓ, and Ap^⊥ is properly proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For subgroup inclusions LΛ_i⊂LΓ, bounded Pimsner-Popa bases are given by unitary coset representatives. The Jones projections e_{LΛ_i} commute on ℓ^2Γ. Almost malnormality of Λ_i implies the inclusion LΛ_i⊂LΓ is mixing. Theorem 3.12 supplies relative proper proximality, with respect to the boundary piece generated by the LΛ_i, on the nonamenable/nontrivial part of any subalgebra. Applying Theorem 1.1 to that family of mixing subalgebras yields a decomposition into a properly proximal summand and summands amenable relative to the LΛ_i, which is exactly the stated conclusion.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.13: free product deformation gives relative proper proximality with respect to word length
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M_1 and M_2 be finite von Neumann algebras and let M=M_1∗M_2 be their tracial free product. Consider the free product malleable deformation on ~M=M∗LF_2; its associated maps satisfy E_M∘α_t=P_0+Σ_{n≥1}(sin(πt)/(πt))^{2n}P_n, where P_n is the orthogonal projection onto the subspace of L^2(M) spanned by reduced words of length n. Let X_F be the hereditary C∗-algebra generated by the projections {P_n}_{n≥0}. If A⊂M is a nonamenable von Neumann subalgebra, then there exists a central projection p∈Z(A) such that Ap is amenable and Ap^⊥ is properly proximal relative to X_F.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper invokes [DKEP22, Proposition 9.1], which gives an M-bimodular u.c.p. map from (M^{op})′∩B(L^2~M⊖L^2M) into ~S_{X_F}(M). Since L^2~M⊖L^2M is isomorphic to L^2M⊗K as an M-M bimodule for a right M-module K, this map may be restricted to B(L^2M)⊗id_K. Let p∈Z(A) be maximal with Ap amenable. If Ap^⊥ were not properly proximal relative to X_F, an Ap^⊥-central state on ~S_{X_F}(M), normal on Ap^⊥, could be composed with the above u.c.p. map to produce an A-central state on B(L^2M) supported on a nonzero central projection q≤p^⊥. That would make Aq amenable, contradicting maximality of p.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.1: structural decomposition for free products of exact group von Neumann algebras
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M_i=LΓ_i for countable exact groups Γ_i, i=1,2, and let M=M_1∗M_2 be the tracial free product. For every von Neumann subalgebra A⊂M there exist projections p_1,p_2,p_3∈Z(A′∩M) such that Ap_i≺_M M_i for i=1,2, Ap_3 is amenable, and A(∨_{i=1}^3 p_i)^⊥ is properly proximal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The free product group Γ_1∗Γ_2 is bi-exact relative to {Γ_1,Γ_2}, and the Jones projections e_{M_1},e_{M_2} commute. Thus Theorem 3.12 and Theorem 1.1 give central relative-commutant projections f_1,f_2 such that Af_i is amenable relative to M_i, plus a properly proximal remainder. For each i, take p_i to be the nonamenable part of Af_i after removing its maximal amenable central summand p_i′. Ioana's lemma for free products says that if a subalgebra is amenable relative to M_i, then either it embeds into M_i inside M or it is amenable. Hence the nonamenable parts satisfy Ap_i≺_M M_i, while the removed parts combine into the amenable projection p_3=p_1′+p_2′.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 1.3: proper-proximal absorption in free products
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M_i=LΓ_i where Γ_i are countable exact groups, and let M=M_1∗M_2 be the tracial free product. Let A⊂M be a von Neumann subalgebra such that A∩M_1 is diffuse. If A has no properly proximal direct summand, then A⊂M_1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Since A has no properly proximal direct summand, Theorem 3.12 and Theorem 1.1 provide projections f_1,f_2∈Z(A′∩M) with f_1+f_2=1 such that Af_i is amenable relative to M_i. If Af_2 were nonamenable, Ioana's lemma would give Af_2≺_M M_2. But because A∩M_1 is diffuse, choose trace-zero unitaries u_n∈A∩M_1 converging weakly to 0. A free product normal-form computation gives ||E_{M_2}(x u_n f_2 y)||_2→0 for all x,y∈M, contradicting Popa's intertwining criterion for Af_2≺_M M_2. Thus A is amenable relative to M_1. The Boutonnet-Houdayer absorption theorem then applies: if A∩M_1 is diffuse and A is amenable relative to M_1 inside M_1∗M_2, then A⊂M_1.

proof_techniques.name: Boundary pieces generated by finitely many subalgebras
proof_techniques.description: The paper packages a finite family of subalgebras M_i⊂M into a single M-boundary piece X, the hereditary C∗-subalgebra generated by xJyJ e_{M_i}. When the Jones projections e_{M_i} commute, this boundary piece is equivalently generated by the single projection ∨_i e_{M_i}; it has explicit approximate units built from finite unitary conjugates uJvJ(∨_i e_{M_i})Jv^*Ju^*. In the normal bidual, the identity q_X of the boundary ideal decomposes as the join q_X=∨_i q_i of the identities for the individual boundary pieces.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For graph products, natural candidates for M_i are parabolic, link, or star subalgebras. If their conditional expectations/Jones projections commute, Lemmas 3.2, 3.3, and 3.10 provide a way to combine several relative boundary pieces and later force any obstructing central state to concentrate on one of them.

proof_techniques.name: Bidual localization of a non-proper-proximal central state
proof_techniques.description: The upgrade proof works in ~S(M), the normal bidual small-at-infinity system. Given a central state φ witnessing failure of full proper proximality on the non-properly-proximal summand, the proof analyzes its mass on two bidual projections: q_K, the identity of the compact ideal, and q_X, the identity of the relative boundary ideal. Mass on q_K would produce an amenable direct summand; mass on q_X^⊥ would contradict relative proper proximality. Hence φ is supported on q_X q_K^⊥, and q_X=∨q_i forces positive mass on at least one individual boundary component q_i.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If one proves that a graph product von Neumann algebra is properly proximal relative to a family of parabolic/link subalgebras, this localization converts any failure of full proper proximality into a state supported on one parabolic component. The remaining task is then to turn that state into relative amenability and rule it out using irreducibility or normal-form arguments.

proof_techniques.name: Constructing basic-construction maps from mixing plus a bounded Pimsner-Popa basis
proof_techniques.description: The central technical claim in Theorem 1.1 constructs, for a mixing inclusion N⊂M with bounded Pimsner-Popa basis {m_k}, a u.c.p. map Φ:⟨M,e_N⟩→q_K^⊥ q_N ~S(M) q_N satisfying Φ(x)=q_K^⊥q_N x for all x∈M. The construction first defines a ∗-homomorphism on the algebraic span of m_k a e_N m_l^* using the bidual projection Σ_k ι_nor(Jm_kJ e_N Jm_k^*J). Mixing kills off-expectation terms modulo q_K, and boundedness of the basis gives the norm estimate needed to extend the map. Weak∗ limits of finite basis cutdowns then yield the desired u.c.p. map on the full basic construction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is the key mechanism for upgrading relative proper proximality. To use it for graph products, one should identify subalgebras N, likely parabolic/link/star subalgebras, for which N⊂M is mixing or has an analogous compactness property, and construct bounded Pimsner-Popa bases from reduced graph-product words.

proof_techniques.name: Central-support decomposition via relative amenability states
proof_techniques.description: After a central state on ~S(M) is pushed through the u.c.p. map from a basic construction, the result is an A-central state on ⟨M,e_{M_i}⟩. Its restriction to Z(A′∩M) may not be faithful, so the paper takes its support projection f_i in the center of the relative commutant. The Ozawa-Popa criterion then gives that Af_i is amenable relative to M_i. Faithfulness of the original state on the center of the non-properly-proximal summand is used to prove that the union of these support projections covers that whole summand.
