proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For proving the whole graph product algebra M is properly proximal, apply the decomposition to A=M. If all possible relative amenability summands M f_i relative to the chosen proper parabolic subalgebras can be excluded, the decomposition leaves only the properly proximal summand.

proof_techniques.name: Relative bi-exactness implies relative proper proximality through nuclear crossed products
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 3.12 adapts the group-level relative bi-exact boundary to the von Neumann algebra boundary piece generated by subgroup von Neumann algebras. The diagonal embedding of ℓ^∞Γ into B(ℓ^2Γ) sends the group relative ideal into the von Neumann boundary piece, yielding a map from the reduced crossed product S_X(Γ)⋊_rΓ into S_X(LΓ). If a subalgebra without amenable summands were not relatively properly proximal, an equivariant u.c.p. map and a Boutonnet-Carderi GNS argument would produce a representation of LΓ inside a corner of the crossed product bidual. Nuclearity of the crossed product, coming from relative bi-exactness, extends this to B(ℓ^2Γ), yielding an invariant state and hence amenability, contradiction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For graph products of groups, or for graph-product von Neumann algebras with an available analogue of relative bi-exactness, this gives a template for establishing the initial relative proper proximality before invoking the upgrade theorem.

proof_techniques.name: Free-product deformation as a source of relative boundary pieces
proof_techniques.description: For M=M_1∗M_2, the paper recalls the free-product malleable deformation whose compression to M is diagonal on reduced word length subspaces, with coefficients (sin(πt)/(πt))^{2n}. The hereditary C∗-algebra generated by the word-length projections P_n is used as a boundary piece X_F. An M-bimodular u.c.p. map from the complement bimodule of the deformation into ~S_{X_F}(M) then implies that every nonamenable subalgebra has a direct summand properly proximal relative to X_F, up to an amenable summand.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Graph products have reduced-word length structures and can often be decomposed using free-product-like normal forms. A comparable word-length boundary or deformation could provide the initial relative proper proximality needed before the bidual upgrade.

proof_techniques.name: Using weakly-null unitaries to rule out embedding into the wrong free-product factor
proof_techniques.description: In Corollary 1.3, once a summand is amenable relative to M_2, Ioana's dichotomy would force it to intertwine into M_2 unless it is amenable. The paper rules out this intertwining when A∩M_1 is diffuse by choosing weakly-null trace-zero unitaries u_n∈A∩M_1 and using free-product normal forms to show E_{M_2}(x u_n y)→0 for all x,y∈M, contradicting Popa's intertwining criterion.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original graph-product hypotheses include trace-zero unitaries in vertex algebras. Such unitaries may be usable, via graph-product normal forms, to build expectation-vanishing sequences that rule out relative amenability or intertwining into certain proper parabolic subalgebras.

other_useful_info: The paper explicitly notes several reusable hypotheses: subgroup inclusions LΛ⊂LΓ always have bounded Pimsner-Popa bases from coset representatives; almost malnormality of Λ<Γ implies the inclusion LΛ⊂LΓ is mixing; and free-product factors M_i⊂M_1∗M_2 are mixing when the factors are diffuse. The free product applications rely on the known fact that Γ_1∗Γ_2 is bi-exact relative to {Γ_1,Γ_2} when Γ_i are exact. The authors state that for general tracial free products M_1∗M_2 it should be true that subalgebras without amenable direct summands are properly proximal relative to the boundary piece generated by M_1 and M_2, but in this paper they only obtain the needed result under the extra assumption M_i=LΓ_i with Γ_i exact. They also mention the folklore conjecture, stated by Oyakawa, that exact relatively hyperbolic groups are bi-exact relative to their peripheral subgroups; peripheral subgroups are almost malnormal, so Theorem 1.2 would apply if that conjecture holds.
why_relevant: Develops von Neumann algebra-level techniques for upgrading relative proper proximality to full proper proximality using bidual computations. This is directly relevant if the graph-product proof first establishes proper proximality relative to link/star subalgebras and then uses irreducibility to upgrade.

title: Biexact von Neumann Algebras
overall_summary: The paper introduces a von Neumann algebraic notion of biexactness by replacing the small-at-infinity compactification of a group with an operator-system boundary S(M) inside B(L2M), and requiring the inclusion M ⊂ S(M) to be M-nuclear in Magajna's M-topology. It proves permanence results for this notion, including inheritance by expected subalgebras, stability under amplifications and commutants, tensor and free product relative versions, and equivalence with group biexactness for group von Neumann algebras. It also proves structural consequences: in particular, a biexact von Neumann algebra has a central decomposition into an amenable summand and a properly proximal summand, and every expected subalgebra of a biexact algebra satisfies the same alternative. For the original graph-product problem, the most directly useful output is Corollary 6.11: once the graph product von Neumann algebra is shown to be biexact, or biexact relative to a boundary strong enough to rule out amenable summands, proper proximality follows. The paper also supplies technical tools for proving such biexactness from dense C*-level approximations, free product decompositions, relative boundary pieces built from Jones projections, malleable deformations, or closable derivations.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 3.2 and Definition 6.1: boundary pieces, small-at-infinity systems, and relative biexactness
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a von Neumann algebra in standard form on L2M. An M-boundary piece is a hereditary C*-subalgebra X ⊂ B(L2M) such that M ∩ Mult(X) is weakly dense in M and M′ ∩ Mult(X) is weakly dense in M′. Define K_X^L(M) as the closure of B(L2M)X in both the C-M and C-M′ Magajna module topologies, define K_X(M) = K_X^L(M)* ∩ K_X^L(M), and define K_X^{∞,1}(M) as the closure of K_X(M) in both the M-M and M′-M′ topologies. The small-at-infinity operator system relative to X is S_X(M) = {T ∈ B(L2M) : [T,a] ∈ K_X^{∞,1}(M) for every a ∈ M′}. When X = K(L2M), the notation is K^L(M), K(M), K^{∞,1}(M), and S(M). The von Neumann algebra M is biexact relative to X if the inclusion M ⊂ S_X(M) is M-nuclear, meaning that there are u.c.p. maps M → M_{n(i)}(C) → S_X(M) whose compositions converge pointwise to the inclusion in the M-topology. M is biexact if it is biexact relative to K(L2M).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: These are definitions, but the paper records two structural facts used repeatedly. First, for T ∈ B(L2M), the set P_T = {a ∈ M′ : [T,a] ∈ K_X^{∞,1}(M)} is a von Neumann subalgebra of M′. It is visibly a unital C*-algebra; if a_i ∈ P_T converges ultrastrong-* to a, then for every normal positive functional ω the seminorm r_ω satisfies d_{r_ω}([T,a],K_X^{∞,1}(M)) ≤ r_ω(T(a-a_i)) + r_ω((a-a_i)T), which tends to 0. Thus to show T ∈ S_X(M), it suffices to check commutators against any von Neumann generating set of M′. Second, the positive cone of K_X^{∞,1}(M) agrees with that of K_X(M): if T ≥ 0 lies in K_X^{∞,1}(M), a noncommutative Egorov cut-down produces positive contractions z_i ∈ M tending strongly to 1 with d(z_iTz_i,X) → 0 in norm; taking square roots and polar decompositions gives T^{1/2} ∈ K_X^L(M), hence T ∈ K_X(M).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.4: noncommutative Egorov cut-down for the M-topology
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a von Neumann algebra, M0 ⊂ M an ultraweakly dense C*-subalgebra, and X an operator M0-bimodule. Suppose that for k = 1,...,n, nets x_i^k ∈ X converge to 0 in the (M0 ⊂ M)-topology. For every finite family F = {φ_1,...,φ_m} of positive normal functionals on M restricted to M0, there is a net of positive contractions 1 − z_i ∈ M0 such that ||z_i x_i^k z_i|| → 0 for every k and φ_j(1 − z_i) → 0 for every j. If M0 = M, the z_i may be chosen to be projections.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: It suffices to treat x_i^k lying in M0 X M0. By convergence in the defining seminorms, for each i,j,k one writes x_i^k = a_{i,j,k}^* y_{i,j,k} b_{i,j,k} with a_{i,j,k}, b_{i,j,k} ∈ M0, y_{i,j,k} ∈ X, and φ_j(a_{i,j,k}^*a_{i,j,k})^{1/2} ||y_{i,j,k}|| φ_j(b_{i,j,k}^*b_{i,j,k})^{1/2} tending to 0; the proof normalizes so each factor tends to 0. Fix N and choose a continuous cutoff f_N on [0,∞) with f_N = 1 on [0,1/N] and f_N = 0 on [2/N,∞). Put z_i = f_N(sum_{j,k}(a_{i,j,k}^*a_{i,j,k}+b_{i,j,k}^*b_{i,j,k})). Then ||z_i a_{i,j,k}^*|| and ||b_{i,j,k} z_i|| are bounded by (2/N)^{1/2}, so ||z_i x_i^k z_i|| becomes uniformly small for large i. Also 1 − z_i is dominated by N times the same sum, hence all φ_j(1 − z_i) tend to 0. When M0 = M, the continuous cutoff is replaced by the spectral projection 1_{[0,1/N]} of the same positive operator.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.7 and Corollary 3.8: continuity of completely positive maps in module topologies
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M and N be von Neumann algebras, let E be an operator M-system, and let F be an operator N-system. If φ : E → F is completely positive and its restriction to M is a normal map M → N, then φ is continuous from the weak M-topology on E to the weak N-topology on F. Consequently, if N ⊂ M is an expected von Neumann subalgebra and e_N : L2M → L2N is the Jones projection, then Ad(e_N) : B(L2M) → B(L2N), T ↦ e_N T e_N, is continuous from the weak M-topology to the weak N-topology, and also from the weak M′-topology to the weak N′-topology.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: To prove continuity it is enough to test against functionals η in the N-normal predual of F. By polarization one reduces to η a state. Since η|_N is normal and φ|_M is normal, the positive functional η ∘ φ has normal restriction to M. For a positive functional on an operator M-system, normality of its restriction to M is equivalent, by the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality, to M-normality of the functional on the whole system. Hence η ∘ φ lies in the M-normal predual of E. The Jones projection map is completely positive, and on M it is the normal conditional expectation onto N; similarly, on commutants it implements the corresponding normal map, so the lemma applies.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.6, Proposition 4.8, and Corollary 4.9: upgrading dense C*-nuclearity to von Neumann M-nuclearity
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a separable von Neumann algebra, M0 ⊂ M a unital ultraweakly dense C*-subalgebra, and E a normal M0-system. The inclusion M0 ⊂ E is (M0 ⊂ M)-nuclear if and only if there exist an amenable von Neumann algebra R and normal u.c.p. maps Φ : M → R and Ψ : R → (E^{M0♯M0})* such that ΨΦ(x) = x for every x ∈ M0, where E^{M0♯M0} is the space of M0-normal functionals and the target is its normal bidual. More generally, if M0 ⊂ M1 ⊂ M are ultraweakly dense C*-subalgebras and E is a normal M1-system, then (M0 ⊂ M)-nuclearity of M0 ⊂ E implies (M1 ⊂ M)-nuclearity of M1 ⊂ E; if M0 is locally reflexive, the corresponding partial converse holds. In particular, if E is a normal M-system and M0 ⊂ M is ultraweakly dense, then (M0 ⊂ M)-nuclearity of M0 ⊂ E implies M-nuclearity of M ⊂ E.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the separable theorem, choose increasing finite-dimensional operator systems E_n ⊂ M0 whose union is ultraweakly dense. From (M0 ⊂ M)-nuclearity choose u.c.p. maps θ_n = ψ_n φ_n through matrix algebras so that θ_n approximates the identity in M-topology on E_n and, inductively, θ_n also makes the previous errors small after further compositions. The latter condition is obtained using Lemma 4.5, which gives approximate bimodularity of u.c.p. maps that are close to the identity on M0. The matrix algebras with connecting maps form an operator-system inductive limit S; its bidual S** is an amenable von Neumann algebra because S is nuclear. A point-weak* limit of the maps φ_n gives Φ into S**, and a point-weak* limit of the maps ψ_n gives Ψ out of S**. The inductive summability estimates telescope to show ΨΦ(a) = a on the dense algebra M0, and normality extends this to the von Neumann algebra context. Proposition 4.8 uses this amenable factorization locally, including σ-finite corner reductions in the nonseparable case, to pass from M0 to larger dense C*-algebras. Corollary 4.9 is the special case M1 = M.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.1 and Corollary 5.6: weak exactness as M-nuclearity of the concrete inclusion
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M ⊂ B(H) be a von Neumann algebra and M0 ⊂ M an ultraweakly dense C*-subalgebra. Then M0 is weakly exact in M in Isono's sense if and only if there are nets of completely contractive maps φ_i : M0 → M_{n(i)}(C) and ψ_i : M_{n(i)}(C) → B(H) such that ψ_iφ_i(a) → a for every a ∈ M0 in the (M0 ⊂ M)-topology. Equivalently, the maps may be taken completely positive and contractive, and in the unital case u.c.p.; i.e. the inclusion M0 ⊂ B(H) is (M0 ⊂ M)-nuclear. In particular, a von Neumann algebra M ⊂ B(H) is weakly exact if and only if the inclusion M ⊂ B(H) is M-nuclear.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The implication from approximating maps to weak exactness uses Isono's criterion: the restriction of the weak (M0 ⊂ M)-topology to M0 is the ultraweak topology, so such finite-dimensional approximations imply the required min-continuity after quotienting by ideals. For the converse, one adapts Brown-Ozawa's exactness argument. Given a representation π : M0 ⊗ B → B(K) killing M0 ⊗ J, weak exactness is reformulated as ker q ⊂ ker π for the quotient q : M0 ⊗ B → M0 ⊗ B/J. Finite-dimensional factorizations are built by considering diagrams M0 ⊗ B → M_n ⊗ B → B(H) ⊗ B and using exactness of matrix algebras. Approximate units in J and Lemma 3.4 cut down errors so that vector states see them as small. To pass from completely contractive to u.c.p. maps, Lemma 4.1 replaces maps by compressions to finite-dimensional subspaces and Lemma 3.6 perturbs almost-unital completely contractive maps to u.c.p. maps in the M-topology.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.10: expected subalgebras inherit relative biexactness
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a von Neumann algebra, let N ⊂ M be a von Neumann subalgebra with a normal faithful conditional expectation E : M → N, and let e_N : L2M → L2N be the Jones projection. If X ⊂ B(L2M) is an M-boundary piece, set X_N to be the norm closure of e_N K_X(M) e_N inside B(L2N). If M is biexact relative to X, then N is biexact relative to X_N. In particular, if M is biexact, then every expected von Neumann subalgebra N ⊂ M is biexact.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Corollary 3.8, Ad(e_N) is continuous from the weak M-topology to the weak N-topology and also from the weak M′-topology to the weak N′-topology. Therefore it sends K_X^{∞,1}(M) into K_{X_N}^{∞,1}(N). Since e_N commutes with J_N N J_N in the standard inclusion, if T ∈ S_X(M), then e_N T e_N has commutators with N′ lying in K_{X_N}^{∞,1}(N), so Ad(e_N)(S_X(M)) ⊂ S_{X_N}(N). Composing the finite-dimensional u.c.p. approximations M → M_n(C) → S_X(M) implementing relative biexactness of M with restriction to N and Ad(e_N) gives u.c.p. approximations N → M_n(C) → S_{X_N}(N) converging in the N-topology. For X = K(L2M), e_N K(L2M)e_N = K(L2N), yielding ordinary biexactness of N.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 6.11: biexact von Neumann algebras are properly proximal modulo amenable summands
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a biexact von Neumann algebra. Then every von Neumann subalgebra of M is either properly proximal or has a nonzero amenable direct summand. Here proper proximality is the DKEP22 notion: a von Neumann algebra P is properly proximal if for every nonzero central projection p ∈ Z(P), there is no conditional expectation S(pP) → pP from the small-at-infinity operator system of pP onto pP.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Proposition 6.10, any expected subalgebra of a biexact algebra is biexact; the corollary is stated for arbitrary subalgebras using the paper's inherited-biexactness framework together with the central-summand definition. For a biexact algebra P, if a nonzero central summand pP is not properly proximal, then by definition there is a conditional expectation E : S(pP) → pP. Since pP is biexact, the inclusion pP ⊂ S(pP) is pP-nuclear. Composing the finite-dimensional approximating maps pP → M_n(C) → S(pP) with E gives finite-dimensional u.c.p. approximations of the identity inclusion pP ⊂ pP in the pP-topology; this is semidiscreteness/injectivity, hence amenability. Thus failure of proper proximality on a nonzero central summand forces an amenable summand; if no such summand exists, the algebra is properly proximal.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.6: criterion for unitaries to converge to zero over a boundary piece
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a σ-finite von Neumann algebra with normal faithful state ω, and let X ⊂ B(L2(M,ω)) be an M-boundary piece. Suppose u_i ∈ U(M) is a net such that the weak* limit of ω ∘ Ad(u_i) exists and belongs to the predual M_* ⊂ M*. If u_i T u_i^* → 0 ultraweakly for every T ∈ X, then u_i converges to 0 over X, meaning that u_i T u_i^* → 0 ultraweakly for every T ∈ K_X^{∞,1}(M).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For decomposable elements T = a^* S b with a,b ∈ M and S ∈ B(L2M), the vector-state estimate |⟨u_i^* a^* S b u_i xω^{1/2}, yω^{1/2}⟩| ≤ ||S|| ||x|| ||y|| ω(u_i^*a^*a u_i)^{1/2} ω(u_i^*b^*b u_i)^{1/2} holds. Taking infima over all such decompositions gives a bound by the M-topology seminorm associated with the normal limit of ω ∘ Ad(u_i). Hence if the conjugates vanish ultraweakly on X, they also vanish on the M-M closure of X. Since conjugation by u_i is M′-bimodular, the vanishing extends to the subsequent M′-M′ closure, which is K_X^{∞,1}(M).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.12 and Proposition 6.13: relative biexactness gives a Popa-intertwining/amenability alternative
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a σ-finite von Neumann algebra and let F = {(B_j,E_j)}_{j∈J} be a family of expected von Neumann subalgebras B_j ⊂ M with normal faithful conditional expectations. Let e_{B_j} be the Jones projections and let X_F be the hereditary C*-subalgebra of B(L2M) generated by {x J y J e_{B_j} : x,y ∈ M, j ∈ J}. If N ⊂ M is a finite von Neumann subalgebra with expectation, then either there is a net u_i ∈ U(N) converging to 0 over X_F, or N embeds with expectation into some B_j inside M in Popa's sense, written N ⪯_M B_j. Consequently, if M is biexact relative to X_F, then for every finite expected N ⊂ M, either N′ ∩ M is amenable or N ⪯_M B_j for some j.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the lemma, choose a normal faithful state on M whose restriction to N is tracial. If N does not intertwine into any B_j, Popa's intertwining criterion as extended by Houdayer-Isono gives a net u_i ∈ U(N) with E_j(a u_i b) → 0 strongly for all a,b ∈ M and all j. Matrix coefficient estimates on generators x J y J e_{B_j} of X_F show that Ad(u_i) sends every element of X_F ultraweakly to 0. The chosen state is tracial on N, so the hypotheses of Lemma 6.6 are satisfied and the net converges to 0 over X_F. For the proposition, assume no intertwining and use such a net. A point-ultraweak limit θ of Ad(u_i) annihilates K_{X_F}^{∞,1}(M), sends S_{X_F}(M) into M, and fixes N′ ∩ M because the u_i lie in N. Composing θ with the u.c.p. finite-dimensional approximations M → M_n(C) → S_{X_F}(M) yields weak nuclearity of the inclusion N′ ∩ M ⊂ M. Since N is finite and expected, N′ ∩ M has an expectation in M; weak nuclearity of this inclusion forces N′ ∩ M to be amenable.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.14: tensor product relative permanence
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For i = 1,2, let M_i be von Neumann algebras and X_i ⊂ B(L2M_i) M_i-boundary pieces. If each M_i is biexact relative to X_i, then M = M_1 tensor M_2 is biexact relative to the hereditary C*-subalgebra X ⊂ B(L2M_1 tensor L2M_2) generated by X_1 tensor B(L2M_2) and B(L2M_1) tensor X_2. In particular, if each M_i is biexact, then M_1 tensor M_2 is biexact relative to the boundary piece associated with the expected tensor factors M_1 and M_2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The key inclusion is S_{X_1}(M_1) tensor S_{X_2}(M_2) ⊂ S_X(M_1 tensor M_2). If T ∈ S_{X_1}(M_1), S ∈ S_{X_2}(M_2), a ∈ M_1, and b ∈ M_2, then [T tensor S, J(a tensor 1)J] = [T,JaJ] tensor S lies in K_X^{∞,1}(M), and [T tensor S, J(1 tensor b)J] = T tensor [S,JbJ] also lies in K_X^{∞,1}(M). Since M′ is generated by these two kinds of elements, the commutator criterion for S_X applies. Tensoring the u.c.p. finite-dimensional approximations implementing relative biexactness of M_1 and M_2 gives approximations of the inclusion of the algebraic tensor product into S_X(M). Corollary 4.9 upgrades the dense C*-level convergence to M-nuclearity for the von Neumann tensor product.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.16: free products preserve relative biexactness
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (M_1,ω_1) and (M_2,ω_2) be von Neumann algebras with normal faithful states, and let X_i ⊂ B(L2(M_i,ω_i)) be M_i-boundary pieces. If M_i is biexact relative to X_i for i = 1,2, then the free product von Neumann algebra M = (M_1,ω_1) * (M_2,ω_2) is biexact relative to X_1 ∨ X_2, the boundary piece generated in B(L2M) by e_1 X_1 e_1 and e_2 X_2 e_2, where e_i is the projection L2M → L2M_i. In particular, if each M_i is weakly exact, then their free product is biexact relative to the boundary piece generated by the two free-product legs; if each M_i is biexact, then their free product is biexact.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let M0 = (M_1,ω_1) *_r (M_2,ω_2) be the reduced free product C*-algebra. Theorem 5.10 gives (M0 ⊂ M)-nuclearity of the inclusion M0 ⊂ (S_{X_1}(M_1),ω_1) *_r (S_{X_2}(M_2),ω_2). Thus it remains to show this reduced free product operator system is contained in S_{X_1∨X_2}(M). Using the left and right free-product representations λ and ρ, for T ∈ S_{X_1}(M_1), a ∈ M_1′, and b ∈ M_2′ one has [λ(T),ρ(b)] = 0 and [λ(T),ρ(a)] = e_1 λ([T,a]) e_1 ∈ K_{X_1∨X_2}^{∞,1}(M); similarly for S_{X_2}(M_2). For reduced words in alternating centered elements from S_{X_1}(M_1) and S_{X_2}(M_2), one expands the commutator as a sum of terms of these forms. A technical approximation uses cyclicity of the vacuum vector to replace factors followed by e_i by actual elements of M_i. Since M′ is generated by ρ(M_1′) and ρ(M_2′), the commutator criterion gives the required inclusion. Corollary 4.9 then upgrades the dense reduced-free-product nuclearity to von Neumann relative biexactness.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 6.18 and 6.20: relative biexactness with respect to the amenable boundary piece
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a tracial von Neumann algebra M, the amenable boundary piece X_amen ⊂ B(L2M) is the hereditary C*-subalgebra generated by operators T_ξ associated with left- and right-bounded vectors ξ in Hilbert M-bimodules weakly contained in the coarse bimodule L2M tensor L2M. If M is tracial and biexact relative to X_amen, then every von Neumann subalgebra N ⊂ M with no amenable direct summand has amenable relative commutant N′ ∩ M. Moreover, if M is tracial and biexact relative to its amenable boundary piece, then every von Neumann subalgebra N ⊂ M is also biexact relative to its own amenable N-boundary piece.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The first result is a direct consequence of DKEP22 Lemma 6.13 and the proof of Proposition 6.13. The key input from DKEP22 is that, for a subalgebra N ⊂ M, the Jones projection e_N lies in the amenable boundary piece exactly in the amenable situation. Thus the relative-boundary convergence/intertwining alternative detects amenable direct summands; if no such summand exists, the conjugation-limit argument used in Proposition 6.13 yields amenability of N′ ∩ M. For the inheritance result, Lemma 6.19 shows e_N X_amen^M e_N ⊂ X_amen^N and e_N K_{X_amen^M}(M)e_N ⊂ K_{X_amen^N}(N). Proposition 6.10 then transfers relative biexactness from M to N.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 7.3: approximation and lifting characterizations of relative biexactness
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a separable von Neumann algebra with normal faithful state μ, let J be the modular conjugation on L2(M,μ), and let X ⊂ B(L2M) be an M-boundary piece. The following are equivalent, among other equivalent formulations in the theorem: (1) M is biexact relative to X. (2) M is weakly exact and for every ε > 0 and finite-dimensional operator systems E ⊂ F ⊂ B(L2M) with E ⊂ M, there exists a u.c.p. map φ : B(L2M) → B(L2M) such that d_{r_μ}(x − φ(x), K_X^{∞,1}(M)) < ε and d_{r_μ}([JxJ, φ(T)], K_X^{∞,1}(M)) < ε for all x ∈ E and T ∈ F, where r_μ is the seminorm combining the M- and M′-topologies. Equivalently, one may work with a separable ultraweakly dense C*-subalgebra M0 ⊂ M and separable C*-subalgebra B ⊂ B(L2M) containing M0 and 1, requiring M0 ⊂ B to be (M0 ⊂ M)-nuclear and the existence of a u.c.p. map B → S_X(M) that fixes M0 modulo K_X(M).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The implication from relative biexactness to the approximation condition is immediate by composing the finite-dimensional approximating maps with the inclusion S_X(M) ⊂ B(L2M); relative biexactness also implies weak exactness because S_X(M) ⊂ B(L2M). Conversely, starting from the approximate u.c.p. maps, choose dense sequences in the relevant separable C*-algebras and diagonalize. For each n, pick φ_n whose errors on the first n elements are r_μ-small modulo K_X^{∞,1}(M). Write these errors as finite decompositions with coefficients in M and M′. Let I be the ideal K_X(M) ∩ B~ in a separable C*-algebra B~ generated by all data, and choose a quasicentral approximate unit e_n in I with summable commutator and compression errors. Define a patched u.c.p. map φ(T) = e_1 T e_1 + sum_n (e_{n+1} − e_n)^{1/2} φ_n(T) (e_{n+1} − e_n)^{1/2}. The summability and r_μ estimates imply φ fixes M0 modulo K_X^{∞,1}(M) and has commutators with JM0J in K_X^{∞,1}(M); Lemma 7.2 upgrades fixing modulo K_X^{∞,1}(M) to fixing modulo K_X(M). For the final step, Lemma 7.1 gives an M-bimodular u.c.p. map B(L2M) → K_X(M)^{♯*}. If p is the support projection of K_X(M)^{♯*} inside S_X(M)^{♯*}, then combining this map on p with the constructed φ on p⊥ gives an M0-bimodular u.c.p. map into S_X(M)^{♯*}. The (M0 ⊂ M)-nuclear inclusion M0 ⊂ B, Corollary 4.9, and Lemma 4.4 yield M-nuclearity of M ⊂ S_X(M).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 7.5 and Theorem 7.6: combining relative boundary pieces via Jones-projection orthogonality
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Lemma 7.5 says the following. Let M be a von Neumann algebra and B_i ⊂ M expected subalgebras with Jones projections e_{B_i}. For an alternating product x_1 Jy_1J e_{B_{i_1}} x_2 Jy_2J e_{B_{i_2}} ... x_n Jy_nJ e_{B_{i_n}}, the expression is continuous in each variable x_k or y_k from the unit ball of M with the ultrastrong-* topology to B(L2M) for the one-sided r_ω-topologies. Theorem 7.6 uses this as follows. Let M be separable, let X,Y ⊂ B(L2M) be boundary pieces, and let F = {(B_i,E_i)} be a family of expected subalgebras with Jones projections e_{B_i}. Let X_F be the boundary piece generated by {xJyJ e_{B_i} : x,y ∈ M, i ∈ I}. Suppose there is an ultraweakly dense C*-subalgebra M0 ⊂ M such that B(L2M) X A ⊂ K_Y^L(M), where A is the C*-algebra generated by {xJyJ e_{B_i} : x,y ∈ M0, i ∈ I}. If M is biexact relative to both X and X_F, then M is biexact relative to Y.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 7.5, the proof is an induction on the position of the varying variable. The basic estimate is that one-sided seminorms of a product are controlled by r_ω(xx*)^{1/2} times the norm of the remaining factor, using Proposition 3.9 and Corollary 3.10. After moving the varying variable into an expression like x x*, a conditional expectation E_{B_i}(x_k x_k*) appears, and ultrastrong-* convergence makes the relevant seminorm tend to 0. For Theorem 7.6, Lemma 7.5 upgrades the hypothesis from coefficients in M0 to coefficients in M. Let A1 be the C*-algebra generated by MJMJ X and A2 the C*-algebra generated by MJMJ e_{B_i}. The hypothesis gives A2 A1 A2 ⊂ K_Y(M). Lemma 7.4 then applies: if boundary pieces X1 and X2 are generated by C*-algebras A1 and A2 satisfying A1 A2 A1 ⊂ Y, and M is biexact relative to X1 and X2, then M is biexact relative to Y. The proof of Lemma 7.4 chooses quasicentral approximate units e_i and f_j for A1 and A2 and patches u.c.p. maps by ψ(T) = e_i^⊥ φ_1(T)e_i^⊥ + e_i f_j^⊥ φ_2(T) f_j^⊥ e_i + e_i f_j T f_j e_i. The last term lies in K_Y, while the first two inherit the commutator estimates from relative biexactness for X1 and X2. The approximation criterion of Theorem 7.3 then gives relative biexactness for Y.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 7.8: malleable deformation criterion for relative biexactness
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a separable weakly exact von Neumann algebra with normal faithful state φ, and let X ⊂ B(L2M) be an M-boundary piece. Suppose M ⊂ M~ is an inclusion with normal faithful conditional expectation E_M : M~ → M, and write φ also for φ ∘ E_M. Assume the Hilbert M-bimodule L2(M~,φ) ⊖ L2(M,φ) is weakly contained in the coarse M-bimodule. Let α_n ∈ Aut(M~,φ) be state-preserving automorphisms such that α_n(x) → x ultraweakly for every x ∈ M. Let V_n be the implementing unitary on L2(M~,φ) defined by V_n(xφ^{1/2}) = α_n^{-1}(x)φ^{1/2}, and let e_M be the projection L2(M~) → L2(M). If e_M V_n e_M ∈ K_X^L(M,L2(M~)) for every n, then M is biexact relative to X.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Define u.c.p. maps φ_n : B(L2M~) → B(L2M) by φ_n(T) = e_M V_n^* T V_n e_M. Since α_n tends to the identity on M, the commutators [V_n,x]e_M become small in the C-M topology for x ∈ M; similarly for right multiplication by JMJ. Thus r_φ(x φ_n(T)y − φ_n(xTy)) → 0 for x,y ∈ M ∪ JMJ. Weak containment of L2(M~) ⊖ L2M in the coarse bimodule gives an M-bimodular u.c.p. map ψ : B(L2M) → M^{op′} ∩ B(L2(M~) ⊖ L2M). Extend it to ψ~ : B(L2M) → B(L2M~) by ψ~(T) = e_M T e_M + ψ(T). The compactness hypothesis e_M V_n e_M ∈ K_X^L(M,L2M~) implies that φ_nψ~ fixes elements of M modulo K_X^{∞,1}(M) and has commutators with JMJ modulo K_X^{∞,1}(M). The approximation characterization in Theorem 7.3 then yields relative biexactness.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 7.11 and Proposition 7.13: closable derivations and compact resolvents give relative biexactness or proper proximality
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a tracial von Neumann algebra, let H be a normal Hilbert M-M correspondence with a real structure, and let δ : L2M → H be a closable real derivation with ultraweakly dense *-algebra domain D(δ) ∩ M. Put Δ = δ*δ, ρ_α = α/(α+Δ), ζ_α = ρ_α^{1/2}, and δ~_α = α^{-1/2} δ ζ_α. If M is weakly exact, H is weakly contained in the coarse correspondence L2M tensor L2M, and X ⊂ B(L2M) is a boundary piece such that ρ_α ∈ K_X(M) for every α > 0, then M is biexact relative to X. Moreover, if the algebra (M^{op})′ ∩ B(H) admits no M-central state whose restriction to M is normal, and ρ_α lies in X for every α > 0, then M is properly proximal relative to X.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses the standard resolvent deformation of a closable derivation. For x ∈ D(δ) ∩ M and a ∈ M, the paper records the estimates ||x δ~_α(a) − δ~_α(xa)|| ≤ α^{-1/4}(2||δ(x)||^{1/2}+6||x||^{1/2})||δ(x)||^{1/2}||a|| and the analogous right-module estimate. Let δ = V Δ^{1/2} be the polar decomposition. Since 1 − (1 − ρ_α)^{1/2} ∈ K_X(M), these estimates imply xV − Vx and JxJ V − V JxJ lie in K_X^L(M,H) for x in the derivation domain. Thus Ψ(T) = V* T V maps (JMJ)′ ∩ B(H) into S_X(M) and satisfies Ψ(x) − x ∈ K_X(M) on the dense C*-algebra generated by D(δ) ∩ M. Weak containment of H in the coarse correspondence gives an M-bimodular u.c.p. map B(L2M) → (JMJ)′ ∩ B(H). Resolvent maps ζ_α land in the dense derivation algebra and converge ultraweakly to the identity, so Lemma 7.10 upgrades the dense bimodularity to an M-bimodular u.c.p. map into S_X(M)^{♯*}. Weak exactness, Corollary 5.6, and Lemma 4.4 then give relative biexactness. For Proposition 7.13, the same construction transfers an assumed failure of relative proper proximality to an M-central normal-on-M state on (M^{op})′ ∩ B(H), contradicting the stated hypothesis.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 7.17: condition (AO)+ or strong condition (AO) implies biexactness under weak exactness
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a weakly exact von Neumann algebra. If M has property (AO)+, then M is biexact. If M satisfies strong condition (AO), then M is biexact. Property (AO)+ means that there are ultraweakly dense unital C*-subalgebras B ⊂ M and C ⊂ JMJ with B locally reflexive such that the multiplication map B ⊙ C → B(L2M)/K(L2M) extends to B tensor_min C and has a u.c.p. lift. Strong condition (AO) means that there are an ultraweakly dense unital C*-subalgebra A ⊂ M and a nuclear C*-algebra C ⊂ B(L2M) containing A such that [c,JaJ] ∈ K(L2M) for all c ∈ C and a ∈ A.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume (AO)+ and let θ be the u.c.p. lift of the multiplication map B tensor C → B(L2M)/K. Extend θ to B(L2M) tensor C and set Ψ(T)=θ(T tensor 1). Since C is in the multiplicative domain of the quotient-level product map, [Ψ(T),c] is compact for every c ∈ C; density of C in JMJ gives Ψ(B(L2M)) ⊂ S(M). Let p be the support projection of K(L2M)^{♯*} inside S(M)^{♯*}; defining Ψ′(T)=pT+p⊥Ψ(T)p⊥ gives a u.c.p. map fixing B in S(M)^{♯*}. Lemma 7.10 supplies an M-bimodular u.c.p. map B(L2M) → S(M)^{♯*}. Since M is weakly exact, Corollary 5.6 gives M-nuclearity of M ⊂ B(L2M), and composing with the bimodular map plus Lemma 4.4 yields M-nuclearity of M ⊂ S(M). Under strong (AO), the nuclear C*-algebra C is contained in S(M) because its commutators with JAJ are compact. The inclusion A ⊂ S(M) is nuclear, and Corollary 4.9 upgrades it to M-nuclearity of M ⊂ S(M).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 7.19: relative biexactness implies a W*-AO multiplication continuity property
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a von Neumann algebra and X ⊂ B(L2M) an M-boundary piece. If M is biexact relative to X, then the multiplication map v : M ⊙ M^{op} → C*(M,M^{op},K_X(M))/K_X(M), v(sum_i a_i tensor x_i) = sum_i a_i x_i + K_X(M), is continuous for the minimal tensor norm. In particular, when X = K(L2M), every biexact von Neumann algebra satisfies condition (W*AO).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let ι : B(L2M) → B(L2M)^{♯*}_J be the canonical embedding into the normal bidual relative to both M and JMJ, and let q_X be the support projection of K_X(M)^{♯*}_J. Compressing by q_X^⊥ gives a quotient in which ι(S_X(M)) commutes with ι(JMJ). If φ_n : M → M_{k(n)}(C) and ψ_n : M_{k(n)}(C) → S_X(M) implement relative biexactness, then the maps M tensor JMJ → q_X^⊥B(L2M)^{♯*}_J q_X^⊥ obtained by φ_n tensor id followed by multiplication of πψ_n and π(JMJ) are u.c.p. and factor through M_{k(n)}(C) tensor JMJ. A point-weak* limit sends a tensor x to the compressed product q_X^⊥ι(a)ι(x). The compressed C*-algebra q_X^⊥ C*(ι(M),ι(JMJ)) identifies with C*(M,JMJ)/(C*(M,JMJ) ∩ K_X(M)); the key kernel computation uses K_X^{∞,1}(M)_+ = K_X(M)_+. This gives a u.c.p. extension of the product map from the minimal tensor product, hence min-continuity.

proof_techniques.name: Use M-topology nuclearity instead of norm nuclearity
proof_techniques.description: The paper replaces norm approximation by approximation in Magajna's M-topology, whose seminorms measure an operator only after left and right multiplication by elements controlled by normal functionals on M. This makes it possible to formulate nuclear embeddings M ⊂ S_X(M) even when S_X(M) is only an operator M-system rather than a C*-algebra. The main upgrade mechanism is: prove finite-dimensional u.c.p. approximations on an ultraweakly dense C*-subalgebra M0 in the (M0 ⊂ M)-topology, then use Theorem 4.6/Proposition 4.8/Corollary 4.9 to obtain genuine M-nuclearity for M.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a graph product von Neumann algebra, one can try to build approximations on the algebraic or reduced C*-graph product, where word combinatorics are available, and then invoke Corollary 4.9 to pass to the von Neumann completion.

proof_techniques.name: Noncommutative Egorov cut-downs
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 3.4 converts convergence in the M-topology into norm convergence after cutting down by large positive contractions or projections in M. The cut-down z_i is a functional-calculus cutoff of the sum of the coefficient squares appearing in seminorm decompositions of the error. This technique is used to perturb completely contractive maps to u.c.p. maps, construct patched maps using approximate units, and show that positive elements in K_X^{∞,1}(M) actually lie in K_X(M).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If graph-product word estimates only give smallness in trace or M-topology, this cut-down method can turn them into norm-small compact-boundary errors, which is often the missing step in Ozawa-style boundary arguments.

proof_techniques.name: Check S_X(M) membership on generators of the commutant
proof_techniques.description: For a fixed operator T, the set of commutant elements a ∈ M′ such that [T,a] lies in K_X^{∞,1}(M) is a von Neumann subalgebra. Hence to prove T ∈ S_X(M), it is enough to verify the commutator condition on any ultraweak generating set of M′. This is heavily used in tensor and free product arguments, where M′ has an explicit generating family from the component algebras.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Graph products have natural generators from vertex algebras and their commutants or right actions. A proof of relative biexactness can focus on commutators with vertex-level generators rather than all of M′.

proof_techniques.name: Boundary convergence of unitary nets and Popa intertwining
proof_techniques.description: The paper links Popa's intertwining criterion to convergence over boundary pieces generated by Jones projections. If a finite expected subalgebra N does not embed into any member of a family F = {B_j}, Popa's criterion gives unitaries u_i ∈ U(N) whose conditional expectations onto B_j vanish. These unitaries then conjugate the Jones-projection boundary X_F to zero. Relative biexactness turns the resulting conjugation-limit map into weak nuclearity of N′ ∩ M ⊂ M, hence amenability of the relative commutant.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In graph products, subalgebras associated to links, stars, or full subgraphs often appear with conditional expectations. Trace-zero unitaries in vertex algebras may help construct the vanishing nets needed to rule out intertwining into certain subgraph algebras.

proof_techniques.name: Relative boundary pieces from Jones projections
proof_techniques.description: Given expected subalgebras B_j ⊂ M, the boundary piece X_F generated by xJyJ e_{B_j} encodes compactness relative to these subalgebras. This relative boundary supports a robust alternative: if M is biexact relative to X_F, finite expected subalgebras either intertwine into some B_j or have amenable relative commutant. It also behaves well under expected inclusions by compressing with Jones projections.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A graph product can often be decomposed using expected subalgebras for full subgraphs. Relative boundaries associated to these subgraphs may let one prove enough relative biexactness to force proper proximality.

proof_techniques.name: Free-product commutator computation
proof_techniques.description: In Proposition 6.16 the paper proves free product permanence by embedding the reduced free product of the small-at-infinity systems into the small-at-infinity system of the free product. The core identities are [λ(T),ρ(b)] = 0 for operators from opposite free-product legs and [λ(T),ρ(a)] = e_i λ([T,a]) e_i for operators from the same leg. Reduced-word commutators are then sums of compact-boundary terms.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Graph products interpolate between free and tensor products. Similar reduced-word commutator calculations, adapted to graph-product normal forms and links, may prove membership in a graph-product small-at-infinity boundary.

proof_techniques.name: Boundary-combination via orthogonality of Jones-projection pieces
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 7.6 combines two relative biexactness statements into a third when products of the corresponding boundary generators land in a smaller boundary K_Y. The proof patches approximating u.c.p. maps using quasicentral approximate units for the two boundary C*-algebras. Lemma 7.5 supplies continuity of alternating Jones-projection products, allowing hypotheses verified on a dense C*-subalgebra to extend to the von Neumann algebra.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Irreducibility of a graph, i.e. non-decomposition as a join, is often used to force orthogonality or compactness between different link/star pieces. Theorem 7.6 gives a template for turning such orthogonality into absolute or stronger relative biexactness.

proof_techniques.name: Malleable deformation criterion
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 7.8 shows that a deformation α_n of a larger algebra M~ proves relative biexactness of M if the complement L2(M~)⊖L2(M) is weakly coarse and the compressed implementing operators e_M V_n e_M lie in the left compact ideal associated to X. The proof builds u.c.p. maps by conjugating through V_n and uses weak containment of the complement to insert an M-bimodular u.c.p. map from B(L2M).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the graph product admits a dilation or length deformation analogous to free-product malleable deformations, the compactness of its compression could be checked by graph-word length and used to prove relative biexactness.

proof_techniques.name: Closable derivation/resolvent compactness
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 7.11 proves relative biexactness from a closable real derivation δ into a coarse correspondence when the resolvents ρ_α = α/(α+δ*δ) lie in the boundary piece. The approximate bimodularity estimates for δ~_α show that the polar part V of δ commutes with M and JMJ modulo the boundary compact operators, producing a u.c.p. map into S_X(M).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Graph products have natural word-length or Markov semigroup structures in many settings. If such a structure yields a closable derivation with compact resolvent relative to a graph boundary, this theorem can convert it into biexactness.

proof_techniques.name: Proper proximality via absence of conditional expectations from S(M)
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses the DKEP22 definition of proper proximality: no nonzero central summand pM admits a conditional expectation S(pM) → pM. Biexactness gives finite-dimensional approximations into S(pM). If such an expectation existed, composing with it would make pM amenable. Thus nonamenability of every central summand forces proper proximality.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the target graph-product algebra, after proving biexactness it remains to rule out amenable direct summands. The trace-zero unitary and irreducibility hypotheses are likely designed to ensure this nonamenability/no-amenable-summand condition.

proof_techniques.name: AO-style lifting to biexactness
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 7.17 shows how an Ozawa-type property (AO)+ or strong (AO), together with weak exactness, yields biexactness. In the (AO)+ case a u.c.p. lift of the multiplication map modulo compacts gives a u.c.p. map B(L2M) → S(M) fixing a dense C*-subalgebra modulo compacts; weak exactness then supplies the M-nuclearity. In the strong (AO) case, a nuclear C*-algebra inside S(M) contains a dense subalgebra of M directly.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a graph-product C*-algebra is known or can be shown to satisfy an AO-type condition, this theorem gives a direct route to biexactness and then to proper proximality via Corollary 6.11.

other_useful_info: The paper itself does not discuss graph product von Neumann algebras, but its introduction lists broad sources of biexact von Neumann algebras: amenable algebras, group von Neumann algebras of biexact groups, von Neumann algebras satisfying condition (AO)+ or strong (AO), free products of biexact algebras, expected subalgebras of biexact algebras, amplifications, and commutants. For groups, Theorem 6.2 shows that relative biexactness of a discrete group is equivalent to relative biexactness of its group von Neumann algebra for the corresponding boundary piece, and Corollary 6.3 says group biexactness is W*-invariant. Proposition 6.16 may be useful as a base case or inductive comparison because graph products reduce to free products when there are no edges and to tensor products over joins; Proposition 6.14 describes the tensor-product relative boundary behavior. The paper repeatedly cites DKEP22 for the definition and boundary technology of properly proximal von Neumann algebras; in particular, the key practical consequence used here is that biexactness plus nonamenability of central summands implies proper proximality.
why_relevant: Introduces biexactness for general von Neumann algebras and develops the associated small-at-infinity and M-nuclearity technology. Even if the target theorem is about proper proximality rather than biexactness, these boundary and compactness techniques are part of the same operator-algebraic toolbox.

title: On the structure of graph product von Neumann algebras
overall_summary: The paper develops a structural theory for graph products of von Neumann algebras equipped with faithful normal states, with special attention to subalgebras coming from induced subgraphs. Its central technical tool is a relative reduced-word calculus, which yields an explicit decomposition of L2(M) as an M_V1–M_V2 bimodule and explicit fusion rules for these bimodules. As applications it classifies relative amenability between induced-subgraph subalgebras, non-intertwining in the tracial case, amenability, diffuseness, factoriality, and fullness of graph products. For the original problem, the most immediately relevant consequence is that a join-irreducible graph with at least three vertices and vertex algebras having trace-zero unitaries gives a diffuse full factor; the relative amenability and bimodule decompositions are also likely useful for ruling out boundary-central states in approaches to proper proximality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 1.1 and CF17 structural facts used in the paper
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G=(V,E) be a finite simple graph, and let {(M_v,φ_v): v∈V} be statial von Neumann algebras, meaning von Neumann algebras equipped with faithful normal states. The graph product (M,φ)=graph product over G of (M_v,φ_v) is the unique statial von Neumann algebra generated by state-preserving copies of the M_v such that centered elements indexed by every G-reduced word have φ-expectation zero. For U⊆V, M_U denotes the graph product over the induced subgraph G|_U. The paper uses the facts that each inclusion M_U⊆M has a unique φ-preserving faithful normal conditional expectation E_{M_U}:M→M_U; for V1,V2⊆V the square M_{V1∩V2}⊆M_{V1}, M_{V2}⊆M is a commuting square; and M_{V1}∩M_{V2}=M_{V1∩V2}. A word v1...vn in V is G-reduced if, whenever i<k and v_i=v_k, there is i<j<k such that v_i is not adjacent to v_j.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Existence and uniqueness of graph products and the Fock-space description are cited from Młotkowski and Caspers–Fima. In the Fock picture, L2(M,φ) is spanned by the vacuum vector and products of centered vectors indexed by G-reduced words. The conditional expectation E_{M_U} is the orthogonal projection onto the closed span of the vacuum and reduced words whose letters all lie in U; consequently it kills reduced words with a letter outside U. Uniqueness of φ-preserving expectations forces E_{M_{V1}}E_{M_{V2}}=E_{M_{V1∩V2}}, giving the commuting-square property. The intersection identity is invoked from CF17, Proposition 2.25, and is also compatible with the reduced-word/Fock-space description.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G=(V,E) be a graph and let {(M_v,φ_v): v∈V} be statial von Neumann algebras. If G decomposes as a graph join G=G_1+...+G_n, where G_j has vertex set V_j and the graph join adds all edges between distinct V_i and V_j, then the graph product over G decomposes as the spatial tensor product of the graph products over the G_j: graph product_G(M_v,φ_v) = tensor product over j of graph product_{G_j}(M_v,φ_v), with the tensor product state.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is immediate from the defining independence and commutation relations of the graph product. Vertices in distinct join components are connected by edges, so the corresponding vertex algebras commute and sit in tensor-product position. Within each component the original graph-product relations remain unchanged. Therefore the universal state-preserving von Neumann algebra generated by the vertex algebras is exactly the tensor product of the component graph products.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G=(V,E) be a join-irreducible graph, meaning G is nonempty and cannot be written as a graph join of two nonempty graphs. Then either G is disconnected, or for every vertex v0∈V there exist v1,v2∈V∖{v0} such that v0 is adjacent to v1, v0 is not adjacent to v2, and v1 is not adjacent to v2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is by contrapositive. Suppose G is connected and there is a vertex v0 for which no such pair v1,v2 exists. Then every neighbor of v0 is adjacent to every non-neighbor of v0 distinct from v0. Taking one join side to be the neighbors of v0 and the other to be its complement, including v0, gives two nonempty induced subgraphs with all cross-edges present; connectedness ensures the neighbor side is nonempty. Thus G decomposes nontrivially as a graph join, contradicting join-irreducibility.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 2.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G=(V,E) be a join-irreducible graph. Let {(M_v,φ_v): v∈V} be statial von Neumann algebras, and let (M,φ) be their graph product over G. Assume that the centralizer M_v^{φ_v} contains a unitary u_v with φ_v(u_v)=0 for every v∈V. Then: (1) if |V|=2, say V={v,w}, and dim(M_v)=dim(M_w)=2, then M is diffuse but not a factor; (2) if |V|=2 and max(dim(M_v),dim(M_w))≥3, then M is a diffuse full factor; (3) if |V|≥3, then M is a diffuse full factor.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For |V|=2, join-irreducibility means the two vertices are not adjacent, so M=(M_v,φ_v)*(M_w,φ_w). If both algebras have dimension 2, the state-zero-unitary assumption forces equal weights on C⊕C, and Dykema’s computation gives a diffuse non-factor. If at least one dimension is at least 3, Ueda’s results on free product von Neumann algebras give a diffuse full factor. For |V|≥3, diffuseness follows because M contains, with expectation, a diffuse free-product subalgebra produced by two non-adjacent vertices or by a disconnected decomposition. For fullness and factoriality, the proof shows M′∩M^ω=C for every cofinal ultrafilter ω. If G is disconnected, choose a component V0 and two vertices outside it; then M=M_{V0}*M_{V∖V0}. State-zero unitaries in the two free factors satisfy the hypotheses of Lemma B.1 with B=C, forcing M′∩M^ω⊆C. If G is connected, Lemma 2.2 supplies, for each v0, vertices v1,v2 with v0∼v1, v0 not∼v2, and v1 not∼v2. Caspers–Fima’s amalgamated free product decomposition gives M=M_{B(v0)} *_{M_{S(v0)}} M_{V∖{v0}}, where S(v0) is the sphere and B(v0)=S(v0)∪{v0}. Choosing state-zero unitaries u0∈M_{v0}, u1∈M_{v1}, u2∈M_{v2}, the unitaries u0 in the first amalgamated free product factor and u2, u1^*u2u1 in the second have zero M_{S(v0)}-expectations, including the mixed expectation required by Lemma B.1. Hence M′∩M^ω⊆M_{S(v0)}^ω for every v0. Intersecting over all v0 and using the commuting-square/intersection result gives M′∩M^ω⊆M_{∩_{v0}S(v0)}^ω=C, since v0∉S(v0) for each v0. Thus M is full; the center embeds as constant central sequences, so fullness here also gives factoriality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G=(V,E) be a finite graph with graph-join decomposition into join-irreducible components. A vertex v forms a one-vertex component in the graph-join decomposition if and only if v is adjacent to every other vertex of G. A pair of distinct vertices {v,w} forms a two-vertex component in the graph-join decomposition if and only if v and w are not adjacent to each other but both are adjacent to every vertex in V∖{v,w}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If a singleton or two-point non-edge is a join component, the definition of graph join gives all cross-edges from that component to the rest of the graph, yielding the stated adjacency conditions. Conversely, if the adjacency condition holds, the indicated singleton or pair splits off as a join factor: all vertices in it are adjacent to all vertices outside it, and in the two-point case the induced graph on the pair is join-irreducible because the two vertices are not adjacent.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem E
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G=(V,E) be a graph, let {(M_v,φ_v): v∈V} be statial von Neumann algebras, and let (M,φ) be their graph product. Assume M_v^{φ_v} contains a unitary u_v with φ_v(u_v)=0 for every v∈V. Then: (1) M is diffuse if and only if either some vertex algebra M_v is diffuse or G is not a complete graph. (2) M is a factor if and only if both conditions hold: whenever a vertex v is adjacent to all other vertices of G, M_v is a factor; and whenever v,w are non-adjacent vertices that are adjacent to all vertices in V∖{v,w}, one has max(dim M_v, dim M_w)≥3. (3) M is full if and only if both conditions hold: whenever a vertex v is adjacent to all other vertices of G, M_v is full; and whenever v,w are non-adjacent vertices that are adjacent to all vertices in V∖{v,w}, one has max(dim M_v, dim M_w)≥3.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Write G=G_1+...+G_n as its unique graph-join decomposition, and let N_j be the graph product over G_j. Proposition 2.1 gives M=N_1 tensor ... tensor N_n. A tensor product is diffuse exactly when one tensor factor is diffuse; by Theorem 2.4, every join-irreducible component with at least two vertices gives a diffuse N_j, so failure of diffuseness occurs exactly when every component is a singleton, i.e. G is complete, and no vertex algebra is diffuse. A tensor product is a factor exactly when every tensor factor is a factor; Theorem 2.4 makes all components with at least three vertices automatic full factors, and the two-vertex case is a factor exactly outside the dim 2–dim 2 exception. Lemma 2.6 identifies precisely which one- and two-vertex join components occur in terms of the adjacency conditions in the theorem. Fullness of tensor products is characterized by fullness of the tensor factors using Connes and Houdayer–Marrakchi–Verraedt, and Theorem 2.4 gives the same small-component obstruction as for factoriality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 3.11–3.13
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a graph G=(V,E) and subsets V1,V2⊆V. A word w is G-reduced relative to V1 on the left if v w is G-reduced for every v∈V1, and relative to V2 on the right if w v is G-reduced for every v∈V2; it is relative to (V1,V2) if both hold. Words are considered equivalent if related by admissible swaps of adjacent commuting letters, splittings, and merges. The lemmas prove: (a) if w=w^(1)w^(2)w^(3), where w^(1) is G-reduced in the alphabet V1, w^(2) is G-reduced relative to (V1,V2), and w^(3) is a word in V2 that is G-reduced relative to U on the left, with U={v∈V1∩V2: v is adjacent to every letter of w^(2)}, then w is G-reduced; (b) every word is equivalent to one of this form; and (c) if two such decompositions w^(1)w^(2)w^(3) and ŵ^(1)ŵ^(2)ŵ^(3) are equivalent, then the corresponding three factors are equivalent separately.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (a), one checks the G-reduced condition by considering two equal letters in the total word. If both lie in the same factor, reducedness of that factor applies. If one lies in w^(1) and one in w^(2), relative reducedness on the left forces a non-adjacent intervening letter; the case w^(2) versus w^(3) is analogous. If one lies in w^(1) and the other in w^(3), then either the common vertex is not in U, so some letter of w^(2) is non-adjacent to it, or it is in U, so reducedness of w^(3) relative to U supplies an intervening non-adjacent letter. For (b), reduce the word first, then among equivalent reduced words maximize the initial segment lying in V1 and, subject to that, minimize the terminal boundary before the V2-tail. These extremal choices force the middle piece to be relatively reduced and force the tail to be reduced relative to the corresponding U. For (c), use the characterization of equivalent reduced words by a unique monotone matching/permutation respecting non-commuting order. Induct from the left to show the permutation sends the middle-plus-right part of one decomposition to the middle-plus-right part of the other, and then induct from the right to show it sends the middle part to the middle part; the remaining factors then match as well.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.17
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G=(V,E) be a graph, let {(M_v,φ_v): v∈V} be statial von Neumann algebras, let (M,φ) be their graph product, and let V1,V2⊆V. Let w=w1...wm and ŵ=ŵ1...ŵn be G-reduced words relative to (V1,V2). Let x_j∈M_{w_j}∩ker φ and x̃_j∈M_{ŵ_j}∩ker φ, and set x=x1...xm and x̃=x̃1...x̃n, with the convention x=1 or x̃=1 for the empty word. Let U={v∈V1∩V2: v is adjacent to every letter of w}, with U=V1∩V2 if w is empty. Then for every y∈M_{V1}, E_{M_{V2}}(x̃^* y x)=φ(x̃^*x) E_{M_U}(y). In particular, if w and ŵ are not equivalent, then E_{M_{V2}}(x̃^* y x)=0 for every y∈M_{V1}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: It suffices to test the claimed equality against arbitrary z∈M_{V2} and prove φ(x̃^* y x z)=φ(x̃^*x)φ(E_{M_U}(y)z). By the reduced-word spanning lemma, z may be taken as a product of centered letters along a reduced word in V2. Lemma 3.12 lets one decompose this word into a U-part and a part reduced relative to U; the U-part can be absorbed into y, so one reduces to z represented by a word reduced relative to U. Similarly y is reduced to a centered product along a reduced word in V1. Lemma 3.11 implies that the concatenated word indexing y x z is reduced, and Lemma 3.13 implies it can be equivalent to the word indexing x̃ only in the trivial case where the extra y- and z-words are empty and w is equivalent to ŵ. Orthogonality of graph-product reduced words then gives zero in all nontrivial cases; in the trivial case it gives the scalar φ(x̃^*x). This yields the displayed conditional-expectation identity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G=(V,E) be a graph, and for each v∈V let (M_v,τ_v) be a tracial von Neumann algebra containing a trace-zero unitary. Let (M,τ) be their graph product. For V1,V2⊆V, the following are equivalent: (i) M_{V1} does not intertwine into M_{V2} inside M, equivalently M_{V1} is diffuse relative to M_{V2} in M; (ii) M_{V1} does not intertwine into M_{V1∩V2} inside M; (iii) either there are v∈V1∖V2 and v′∈V1∩V2 with v not adjacent to v′, or M_{V1∖V2} is diffuse; (iv) either there are v∈V1∖V2 and v′∈V1 with v≠v′ and v not adjacent to v′, or there is v∈V1∖V2 such that M_v is diffuse.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The implication (i)⇒(ii) follows from Popa’s intertwining criterion and the identity E_A=E_A∘E_B for A⊆B. For (ii)⇒(iii), the contrapositive is used: if every vertex in V1∖V2 is adjacent to every vertex in V1∩V2 and M_{V1∖V2} has a finite-dimensional direct summand, then M_{V1}=M_{V1∖V2} tensor M_{V1∩V2} on that part, and a matrix-unit calculation shows M_{V1} intertwines into M_{V1∩V2}. For (iii)⇒(iv), again contrapositively, if there is no non-edge from V1∖V2 to V1 and no diffuse vertex algebra in V1∖V2, then V1∖V2 is a complete graph of non-diffuse algebras and hence M_{V1∖V2} is not diffuse, while the non-edge condition in (iii) also fails. For (iv)⇒(i), Popa’s criterion is verified by constructing unitaries in M_{V1} whose M_{V2}-expectations vanish asymptotically after multiplication by arbitrary fixed x,y∈M. In the non-edge case choose trace-zero unitaries u0∈M_{v′} and u1∈M_v and use powers (u0u1)^k; relative reduced-word decompositions of x and y reduce E_{M_{V2}}(x(u0u1)^k y) to an expectation in the two-vertex free product, which is zero for large k. In the diffuse-vertex case choose a Haar unitary u∈M_v and use powers u^k; after the same relative reduction the remaining scalar coefficient tends to zero weakly.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G=(V,E), {(M_v,φ_v)}, (M,φ), and V1,V2⊆V be as above. Let w=w1...wn be G-reduced relative to (V1,V2), and let ξ=ξ1...ξn where ξ_j∈L2(M_{w_j},φ_{w_j})⊖C1 has norm 1; for the empty word take ξ=1. Let H_ξ be the M_{V1}–M_{V2} sub-bimodule of L2(M,φ) generated by ξ. Let U={v∈V1∩V2: v is adjacent to every letter of w}. Then H_ξ is unitarily isomorphic, as an M_{V1}–M_{V2} bimodule, to L2(M_{V1},φ_{V1}) ⊗_{M_U} L2(M_{V2},φ_{V2}), with ξ corresponding to 1⊗1. If ŵ is another G-reduced word relative to (V1,V2) and η is a corresponding product vector, then H_ξ is orthogonal to H_η unless w and ŵ are equivalent and the product-vector inner product ⟨ξ,η⟩ is nonzero.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First reduce by density to product vectors coming from centered algebra elements x_j∈M_{w_j}. For analytic b∈M_{V2} and a∈M_{V1}, compare the inner product of a·ξ·b with ξ to the inner product of a·(1⊗1)·b with 1⊗1 in the relative tensor product. The latter is φ(E_{M_U}(a)σ_{-i/2}(b)), while the former is φ(E_{M_{V2}}(x^*ax)σ_{-i/2}(b)); Lemma 3.17 identifies these quantities. This proves the cyclic bimodule isometry and hence the unitary equivalence. The orthogonality assertion is proved similarly with two words w and ŵ: Lemma 3.17 makes the mixed coefficient vanish unless the words are equivalent, and in the equivalent case it equals the scalar product of the product vectors times the same M_U coefficient.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.4 and Proposition 5.5, stated as Theorem D in the introduction
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G=(V,E), {(M_v,φ_v):v∈V}, and (M,φ) be a graph product of statial von Neumann algebras. For V1,V2⊆V and U⊆V1∩V2, let W_G(V1,V2,U) be the set of G-reduced words w=w1...wℓ that are reduced relative to (V1,V2) and satisfy U={v∈V1∩V2: v is adjacent to w_j for every j=1,...,ℓ}. Define k_G(V1,V2,U)=sum over w∈W_G(V1,V2,U) of product over letters w_j of (dim L2(M_{w_j},φ_{w_j})−1), interpreted as the appropriate Hilbert-space multiplicity. Then M_{V1}L2(M,φ)_{M_{V2}} is isomorphic to the direct sum over U⊆V1∩V2 of (L2(M_{V1},φ_{V1}) ⊗_{M_U} L2(M_{V2},φ_{V2})) with multiplicity k_G(V1,V2,U). Moreover, if H_U(V1,V2)=L2(M_{V1})⊗_{M_U}L2(M_{V2}) and G2 is the induced subgraph on V2, then for U1⊆V1∩V2 and U2⊆V2∩V3 one has the fusion rule H_{U1}(V1,V2) ⊗_{M_{V2}} H_{U2}(V2,V3) ≅ direct sum over W⊆U1∩U2 of H_W(V1,V3) with multiplicity k_{G2}(U1,U2,W).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For each vertex v choose an orthonormal basis B_v of L2(M_v,φ_v)⊖C1. For every relatively reduced word w=w1...wℓ and every choice ξ_j∈B_{w_j}, Lemma 5.3 identifies the cyclic bimodule generated by ξ1...ξℓ with L2(M_{V1})⊗_{M_U}L2(M_{V2}), where U is the common-neighbor set of w inside V1∩V2. Lemma 5.3 also gives mutual orthogonality except for identical equivalence classes, and the multiplicity is exactly the number of basis choices, giving the product of dimensions in k_G. Density follows from the graph-product reduced-word spanning theorem plus Lemma 3.12, which factors every reduced word into a V1-left part, a relatively reduced middle part, and a V2-right part; hence the cyclic bimodules span L2(M). For the fusion rule, first rewrite H_{U1}(V1,V2)⊗_{M_{V2}}H_{U2}(V2,V3) as L2(M_{V1})⊗_{M_{U1}}L2(M_{V2})⊗_{M_{U2}}L2(M_{V3}). Then apply the same bimodule decomposition to the middle M_{U1}–M_{U2} bimodule L2(M_{V2}), using the induced graph G2, and absorb the outside tensor factors to obtain the stated direct sum of H_W(V1,V3).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G=(V,E), {(M_v,φ_v):v∈V}, and (M,φ) be a graph product of statial von Neumann algebras, and assume dim(M_v)≥2 for all v∈V. For V0⊆V, the M_{V0}–M_{V0} bimodule L2(M,φ)⊖L2(M_{V0},φ_{V0}) is weakly coarse if and only if M_{B(v)∩V0} is amenable for every vertex v∉V0, where B(v)=S(v)∪{v}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Using Theorem 5.4 with V1=V2=V0, the complement of L2(M_{V0}) decomposes as a direct sum over nonempty relatively reduced words. For such a word w=w1...wℓ, relative reducedness forces w1∉V0, and its associated amalgamating set U is contained in B(w1)∩V0. If M_{B(w1)∩V0} is amenable, then its subalgebra M_U is amenable; by the standard amenability criterion for correspondences, L2(M_U) is weakly contained in the coarse M_U-bimodule. Connes fusion with L2(M_{V0}) on the left and right makes the corresponding summand weakly coarse over M_{V0}; summing gives the forward implication. Conversely, if M_{B(v)∩V0} is non-amenable for some v∉V0, choose a centered unit vector x∈M_v. The M_{V0}–M_{V0} bimodule generated by x contains, upon restriction to M_{B(v)∩V0}, the identity bimodule L2(M_{B(v)∩V0}), which is not weakly coarse when that algebra is non-amenable. Hence the full complement cannot be weakly coarse.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 6.1 and Lemma 6.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Lemma 6.1: If M is a factor with faithful normal state φ and H1,...,Hn are M–M bimodules such that L2(M,φ) is weakly contained in H1⊕...⊕Hn, then L2(M,φ) is weakly contained in H_i for some i. Lemma 6.2: Let G=(V,E), {(M_v,φ_v)}, and (M,φ) be a graph product, and let V1,V2⊆V. Suppose the M_{V1}–M_{V1} bimodule L2(M,φ) is weakly contained in L2(M,φ)⊗_{M_{V2}}L2(M,φ). Then: (i) M_{V0} is amenable for every V0⊆V1∖V2; and (ii) if V0⊆V1 and M_{V0} is a factor, then there exists U⊆V0∩V2 such that M_{V0} is amenable relative to M_U.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 6.1, represent M⊗_{max}M^{op} on L2(M,φ) by left and right multiplication. Since M is a factor, this representation is irreducible, so the vector state of the standard bimodule is an extreme point of the state space. A Fell-type argument then says that if this representation is weakly contained in a finite direct sum, one summand already weakly contains it. For Lemma 6.2, restrict the assumed weak containment from M_{V1} to M_{V0}. Theorem 5.4 decomposes L2(M) as an M_{V0}–M_{V2} bimodule, and Proposition 5.5 fuses the two copies over M_{V2}; the result is that L2(M) over M_{V0} is weakly contained in a finite direct sum of H_U(V0,V0)=L2(M_{V0})⊗_{M_U}L2(M_{V0}) with U⊆V0∩V2. If V0∩V2=∅, only U=∅ occurs, giving weak containment in the coarse bimodule, hence amenability of M_{V0} by the Bannon–Marrakchi–Ozawa criterion. If M_{V0} is a factor, Lemma 6.1 selects one U from the finite sum, and the same criterion identifies weak containment in L2(M_{V0})⊗_{M_U}L2(M_{V0}) with amenability of M_{V0} relative to M_U.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem C and Theorem A
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Theorem C, statial version: Let G=(V,E) be a graph, let {(M_v,φ_v):v∈V} be statial von Neumann algebras, let (M,φ) be their graph product, and assume M_v^{φ_v} has a state-zero unitary for every v. For V1,V2⊆V, M_{V1} is amenable relative to M_{V2} inside M if and only if: (1) M_v is amenable for every v∈V1∖V2; and (2) for each v∈V1∖V2 and w∈V1 with v≠w, either v is adjacent to w, or both of the following hold: (a) M_{ {v,w} }=M_v*M_w is amenable if w∉V2, while M_v*M_w is amenable relative to M_w if w∈V2; and (b) v and w are adjacent to every vertex in V1∖{v,w}. Theorem A, tracial specialization: If all φ_v=τ_v are traces and each M_v has a trace-zero unitary, then the same relative amenability holds if and only if: (1) M_v is amenable for every v∈V1∖V2; and (2) for every v∈V1∖V2 and w∈V1 with v≠w, either v∼w or dim(M_v)=dim(M_w)=2 and v,w are adjacent to all vertices in V1∖{v,w}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For sufficiency in Theorem C, list the non-adjacent pairs P_j={v,w} with v∈V1∖V2 and w∈V1. Condition (2b) forces these pairs to be mutually disjoint and adjacent to all remaining vertices. The remaining vertices split into Q1⊆V1∖V2 and Q2⊆V1∩V2, and the graph-product algebra decomposes as a tensor product of the M_{P_j}, M_{Q1}, and M_{Q2}; similarly M_{V1∩V2} decomposes using P_j∩V2 and Q2. By (1), M_{Q1} is amenable, and by (2a), each M_{P_j} is amenable relative to M_{P_j∩V2}. Lemma C.3 tensors these relative amenability statements, and Lemma C.1 passes from relative amenability to M_{V1∩V2} inside M_{V1} to relative amenability to M_{V2} inside M. For necessity, relative amenability implies the weak containment L2(M)≺L2(M)⊗_{M_{V2}}L2(M) as M_{V1}–M_{V1} bimodules. Lemma 6.2 applied to singleton V0={v} gives amenability of every M_v with v∈V1∖V2. For a non-adjacent pair {v,w}, if the two-dimensional exceptional case does not hold, Ueda’s theorem makes M_v*M_w a factor, so Lemma 6.2 applied to V0={v,w} gives the relative amenability alternative in (2a). To prove (2b), take a third vertex u∈V1∖{v,w}; if u is not adjacent to one of v,w, the subgraph on {v,w,u} is join-irreducible, so Theorem 2.4 makes M_{ {v,w,u} } a factor. Lemma 6.2 would force it to be amenable relative to a subalgebra contained in M_{ {w,u} }. But M_{ {v,w,u} } splits as an amalgamated free product M_{ {v,u} } *_{M_u} M_{ {w,u} }, and the state-zero unitaries satisfy Lemma C.2, contradicting such relative amenability. Hence both v and w must be adjacent to u. The tracial Theorem A follows from Theorem C by using Ching’s theorem and Ueda’s theorem: for tracial M_1,M_2 with trace-zero unitaries, dim(M_1)=dim(M_2)=2 is equivalent to amenability of M_1*M_2; and if M_1*M_2 is amenable relative to one free factor, Proposition 4.2 plus Ioana’s result implies M_1*M_2 is amenable.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G=(V,E) be a graph, let {(M_v,φ_v):v∈V} be statial von Neumann algebras with dim(M_v)≥2, and let (M,φ) be their graph product. Without assuming state-zero unitaries, M is amenable if and only if: (1) each M_v is amenable; and (2) whenever v and w are not adjacent in G, one has dim(M_v)=dim(M_w)=2 and both v and w are adjacent to every vertex in V∖{v,w}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If the two conditions hold, decompose G as a graph join. Each join-irreducible component must be either a singleton or a two-vertex non-edge: if a component contains a vertex not adjacent to another vertex, condition (2) forces that non-edge pair to be adjacent to all vertices outside the pair, hence to be the whole component; otherwise a universal vertex splits off as a singleton. The graph product is therefore a tensor product of amenable vertex algebras and free products of two two-dimensional algebras, and Ching’s theorem gives amenability of the two-dimensional free products. Conversely, if M is amenable, then every expected subalgebra M_U is amenable, so each M_v is amenable. For a non-edge {v,w}, M_{ {v,w} }=M_v*M_w is amenable, and Ching’s theorem forces both dimensions to be 2. If a third vertex u is not adjacent to one of v,w, then the subalgebra on {u,v,w} is a free product of an algebra of dimension at least 3 with an algebra of dimension at least 2, contradicting Ching’s non-amenability criterion. Thus the non-edge vertices are adjacent to all others.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary A.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (M,φ) be a statial von Neumann algebra. If φ is a trace and M has a nonzero minimal projection p with φ(p)>1/2, then p is central and φ(u)≠0 for every unitary u∈M. If φ is a trace, then there exists a unitary u∈M with φ(u)=0 if and only if φ(p)≤1/2 for every minimal projection p∈M. In general, there exists a unitary u∈M^φ in the centralizer with φ(u)=0 if and only if φ(p)≤1/2 for every minimal projection p∈M^φ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For tracial M, first decompose M into atomic and diffuse central summands. Lemma A.1 shows the set of trace values on unitaries is compact enough that the minimum of |τ(u)| is attained. Lemma A.2 analyzes direct sums A=A_1⊕...⊕A_n with weights α_i and shows the possible absolute trace values on unitaries form the interval [(α_1(1+s)−1)∨0,1], where s is the minimum absolute trace value on the largest summand. If a minimal projection has trace >1/2, finite-factor considerations force its central support to be itself, so it is a central summand too large to cancel by unitaries on the complement. Conversely, if no trace-zero unitary exists, the direct-sum interval formula forces a one-dimensional central summand of weight >1/2, hence a minimal projection with trace >1/2. The general statial case is obtained by applying the tracial statement to the centralizer M^φ, on which φ restricts to a trace.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma B.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (B,φ) be statial, and let B⊆M_i be inclusions with faithful normal expectations E_i:M_i→B for i=1,2. Put φ_i=φ∘E_i and let (M,E_B)=(M_1,E_1)*_B(M_2,E_2) be the amalgamated free product. Suppose there are unitaries u1∈(M_1)^{φ_1} and u2,u3∈(M_2)^{φ_2} such that E_B(u1)=E_B(u2)=E_B(u3)=E_B(u2^*u3)=0. Then for every cofinal ultrafilter ω, M′∩M^ω⊆B^ω.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the free-product Hilbert-space decomposition L2(M)=L2(B)⊕H1⊕H2, where H_i is the sum of reduced tensors whose first letter lies in M_i⊖B. Let P_i be the projection onto H_i. Since the u_j lie in centralizers, right multiplication by u_j is isometric on L2(M). The expectation-zero hypotheses imply the same orthogonality relations as in Ioana’s free product lemma: u1H2u1^*⊆H1; u2H1u2^*⊆H2; u3H1u3^*⊆H2; and u2H1u2^* is orthogonal to H1+u3H1u3^*. Thus for a uniformly bounded centralizing net (x_i) representing an element of M′∩M^ω, the estimates lim_ω ||P2(x_i)||_2 ≤ lim_ω ||P1(x_i)||_2 ≤ 2^{-1/2} lim_ω ||P2(x_i)||_2 force both limits to vanish. Hence x_i−E_B(x_i) tends to zero strongly-* along ω, so the ultrapower element lies in B^ω.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Appendix C: Lemmas C.1, C.2, and C.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Lemma C.1: If (M,φ) is statial and M1,M2⊆M have φ-preserving normal conditional expectations forming a commuting square over M1∩M2, then amenability of M1 relative to M1∩M2 inside M1 implies amenability of M1 relative to M2 inside M. Lemma C.2: In an amalgamated free product (M,E_B)=(M_1,E_1)*_B(M_2,E_2) over (B,φ), if there are unitaries u0∈(M_1)^{φ_1}, u1∈M_1, and u2∈(M_2)^{φ_2} such that E_B(u0)=E_B(u1)=E_B(u0^*u1)=E_B(u2)=0, then M is not amenable relative to either M_1 or M_2. Lemma C.3: If N_i⊆M_i are inclusions with faithful normal conditional expectations and M_i is amenable relative to N_i for i=1,2, then M_1 tensor M_2 is amenable relative to N_1 tensor N_2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For C.1, identify L2(M1) as a subspace of L2(M) and compress the basic construction ⟨M,e_{M1∩M2}⟩ by the Jones projection e_{M1}. The compression map sends it onto ⟨M1,e_{M1∩M2}⟩. Composing a conditional expectation from this latter algebra to M1 with the compression gives a conditional expectation from ⟨M,e_{M1∩M2}⟩ to M1 that is normal on M; restricting to the subalgebra ⟨M,e_{M2}⟩ proves relative amenability to M2. For C.2, decompose L2(M) into reduced-word subspaces and, as a right M1-module, write L2(M)=K⊕K^⊥ with K the words ending in M1 in the prescribed alternating form. The hypotheses imply u0K^⊥ and u1K^⊥ are orthogonal subspaces of K, while u2K⊆K^⊥. If a conditional expectation Φ:⟨M,e_{M1}⟩→M existed, applying Φ to the projection inequalities and evaluating with the state would force Φ(P_K)=Φ(P_{K^⊥})=0, contradicting Φ(1)=1; the M2 case is symmetric. For C.3, use Tomita’s commutation theorem to identify ⟨M1⊗M2,e_{N1⊗N2}⟩ with ⟨M1,e_{N1}⟩⊗⟨M2,e_{N2}⟩. Although the given expectations need not be normal, the proof establishes an extension principle: a conditional expectation S→B induces one S⊗B(H)→B⊗B(H), and it restricts over tensor subalgebras. Applying this twice gives the required conditional expectation onto M1⊗M2.

proof_techniques.name: Graph-join reduction to tensor factors
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly decomposes a graph as a graph join G=G1+...+Gn and uses Proposition 2.1 to turn the graph product into a tensor product of the graph products over join-irreducible components. Structural properties such as diffuseness, factoriality, fullness, and amenability are then reduced to the corresponding properties of the tensor factors, with Lemma 2.6 identifying the only join components that can create small exceptional cases.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The irreducibility hypothesis in the original problem is exactly the absence of a nontrivial graph-join decomposition. Thus the proof can work directly in the join-irreducible case and use Theorem 2.4 to obtain a diffuse full factor before addressing proper proximality.

proof_techniques.name: Fullness from amalgamated free product central-sequence absorption
proof_techniques.description: The proof of Theorem 2.4 uses amalgamated free product decompositions of graph products, together with Lemma B.1, to trap central sequences in smaller subalgebras. For each vertex v0 in the connected join-irreducible case, the decomposition M=M_{B(v0)}*_{M_{S(v0)}}M_{V∖{v0}} and carefully chosen state-zero unitaries imply M′∩M^ω⊆M_{S(v0)}^ω. Intersecting these containments over all vertices collapses the central sequence algebra to C.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Boundary-central states for proper proximality often behave like central sequences or relative centrality obstructions. This technique suggests trying to localize any such obstruction into M_{S(v)} for every v and then use irreducibility or empty intersection of spheres to force triviality.

proof_techniques.name: Relative reduced words as a double-coset/Pimsner–Popa basis
proof_techniques.description: The relative reduced-word calculus factors every word, up to graph-product equivalence, into a left word in V1, a middle word reduced relative to (V1,V2), and a right word in V2. The middle words index orthogonal cyclic M_{V1}–M_{V2} bimodules, much like double-coset representatives for subgroup bimodules. This gives concrete control of conditional expectations and bimodule coefficients.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If proper proximality is approached through correspondences or compactifications built from subgraph subalgebras, these relative representatives provide a way to compute which pieces of L2(M) can support central or invariant states.

proof_techniques.name: Conditional expectation computation for relatively reduced words
