proof_techniques.description: Lemma 3.17 gives an exact formula: for relatively reduced words w and ŵ and products x and x̃ along them, E_{M_{V2}}(x̃^* y x)=φ(x̃^*x)E_{M_U}(y), where U is the set of vertices in V1∩V2 adjacent to all letters of w. If the words are inequivalent, the expression vanishes. The proof combines reduced-word orthogonality with the uniqueness of the relative decomposition.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This formula can be used to show that many averaged or central states vanish on off-diagonal reduced-word pieces, reducing analysis to subalgebras M_U determined by common-neighbor sets.

proof_techniques.name: Explicit bimodule decomposition and fusion rules
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 5.4 decomposes L2(M) as an M_{V1}–M_{V2} bimodule into summands L2(M_{V1})⊗_{M_U}L2(M_{V2}), with multiplicities determined by relatively reduced words. Proposition 5.5 computes Connes fusion of these summands purely in terms of the induced graph on the middle vertex set. This converts analytic questions about correspondences into finite graph-combinatorial questions plus amenability of the subalgebras M_U.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Proper proximality criteria for von Neumann algebras are often phrased in terms of weak containment or absence of invariant states on bimodules. These fusion rules give a concrete mechanism for checking such conditions for graph products.

proof_techniques.name: Relative amenability via weak containment and finite-factor extremality
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses Bannon–Marrakchi–Ozawa’s characterization of relative amenability through weak containment of the standard bimodule in the basic construction. Lemma 6.2 combines this with the explicit fusion rules to force amenability of subgraph algebras disjoint from V2, and to force relative amenability to some M_U when a subgraph algebra is a factor. Lemma 6.1 is the finite-direct-sum selection device in the factorial case.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: To rule out boundary-central states, one may need to rule out amenability or relative amenability of M to certain subalgebras. Theorem A and Lemma 6.2 provide graph-theoretic criteria for exactly when such relative amenability can occur.

proof_techniques.name: Popa intertwining obstruction using trace-zero unitary powers
proof_techniques.description: In Proposition 4.2, non-intertwining of induced-subgraph algebras is proved by constructing nets of unitaries whose expectations vanish against arbitrary fixed left and right multipliers. If there is a non-edge, products of trace-zero unitaries such as (u0u1)^k behave like free reduced words; if a vertex algebra is diffuse, powers of a Haar unitary are used instead. Relative reduced-word decompositions reduce the general expectation calculation to these elementary vanishing facts.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Trace-zero unitaries are an explicit hypothesis in the original problem. This technique can help produce mixing sequences inside M or inside subgraph algebras, which are often used in proper proximality and rigidity arguments.

proof_techniques.name: Small-subgraph contradiction for relative amenability
proof_techniques.description: In the proof of Theorem C, missing adjacency in a triple of vertices creates a join-irreducible three-vertex subgraph whose graph product is a factor by Theorem 2.4. Lemma 6.2 would force relative amenability to a smaller subgraph, but the same three-vertex graph product is an amalgamated free product satisfying Lemma C.2, which forbids relative amenability to either factor. This local contradiction forces global adjacency conditions.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For an irreducible graph with at least three vertices, local triples with non-commutation can be used to produce non-amenability or non-relative-amenability obstructions. Such local obstructions may be the key to eliminating candidate invariant states in a proper proximality proof.

proof_techniques.name: Amenability classification by exceptional join components
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 6.3 shows that a graph product is amenable only when all vertex algebras are amenable and every non-edge is a two-dimensional exceptional pair adjacent to all other vertices. The proof uses graph-join decomposition and Ching’s theorem on amenability of free products. Thus non-amenability is detected by very small graph patterns unless the graph is built from singleton and two-dimensional non-edge join components.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In the original irreducible setting with at least three vertices, this classification strongly restricts amenable exceptional cases and helps show that the algebra cannot be one of the obvious amenable tensor/free-product exceptions.

proof_techniques.name: State-zero-unitary criterion through centralizer minimal projections
proof_techniques.description: Appendix A characterizes when a statial algebra has a state-zero unitary in its centralizer: exactly when no minimal projection in the centralizer has state greater than 1/2. In the tracial case this is a statement about minimal projections in M itself. The proof reduces to the possible trace values of unitaries in direct sums and shows how a large one-dimensional summand is the only obstruction.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem assumes trace-zero unitaries at every vertex. This criterion clarifies that the assumption excludes only large atomic obstructions and justifies using the trace-zero unitaries repeatedly in free-product and mixing constructions.

other_useful_info: The introduction notes that proper proximality for graph products has been investigated in DKE22; this paper itself supplies structural and bimodule tools rather than a direct proper-proximality theorem in the provided text. The paper emphasizes that the state-zero-unitary assumption is mild: in the tracial setting it holds for nontrivial group von Neumann algebras, diffuse algebras, and finite factors other than C. In the two-dimensional tracial case, the trace-zero-unitary assumption forces C⊕C with equal weights, which is exactly the exceptional amenable free-product case appearing throughout the classification.
why_relevant: Gives a detailed structural theory for graph-product von Neumann algebras, including bimodule decompositions for induced-subgraph subalgebras and classifications of relative amenability, fullness, diffuseness, and factoriality. These results are likely essential for using irreducibility of the graph and the trace-zero-unitary hypothesis to rule out boundary-central states.

title: Rigid Graph Products
overall_summary: The paper develops deformation-rigidity tools for von Neumann algebraic graph products, focusing on relative amenability, Popa intertwining, quasi-normalizers, primeness, free-product decompositions, strong solidity, and unique rigid graph product decompositions. The techniques repeatedly exploit the standard link decomposition M_Γ = M_Star(v) ∗_{M_Link(v)} M_{Γ\{v}}, precise conditional-expectation calculations for graph-product reduced words, and Popa-Vaes normalizer dichotomies for amalgamated free products. For the original problem, the most directly relevant part is Section 8: under finite irreducibility, at least three vertices, and trace-zero vertex unitaries, the paper proves that the graph product is a prime II_1 factor and establishes the key non-intertwining result M_Γ not≺ M_Λ for every proper subgraph Λ. The relative amenability intersection theorem and quasi-normalizer localization results are also potentially useful for proving proper proximality, because they turn amenability relative to several subgraph algebras into amenability relative to their intersection and control possible join/tensor obstructions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 2.1 and Lemma 2.2, Popa intertwining notation and transitivity
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (M,τ) be a finite von Neumann algebra, and let A⊆1_A M 1_A and B⊆1_B M 1_B be von Neumann subalgebras. The notation A≺_M B means Popa intertwining: equivalently, there exist projections p∈A and q∈B, a normal *-homomorphism θ:pAp→qBq, and a nonzero partial isometry v∈qMp such that θ(x)v=vx for all x∈pAp; equivalently, there is no net of unitaries u_i∈A such that ||E_B(x* u_i y)||_2→0 for every x,y∈1_A M 1_B; equivalently, L^2(M) contains a nonzero A-B subbimodule of finite right B-dimension. The notation A≺^s_M B means Ar≺_M B for every projection r∈A′∩M. Lemma 2.2 states: if P≺_M Q and Q≺^s_M R, then P≺_M R; and if Pz≺_M Q for every nonzero projection z in Nor_{1_P M 1_P}(P)′∩1_P M 1_P, then P≺^s_M Q. In particular, if Q′∩1_Q M 1_Q is a factor and P≺_M Q and Q≺_M R, then P≺_M R.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The equivalences defining A≺_M B are quoted from Popa's intertwining-by-bimodules theory. The transitivity statement is quoted from earlier work of Drimbe-Hoff-Ioana and Vaes. The key mechanism is that finite-index bimodules compose under relative tensor product, while the stable intertwining hypothesis prevents loss of support when passing through corners. The final factor-relative-commutant observation follows because in a factor relative commutant a nonzero intertwining corner can be upgraded to the stable form needed for transitivity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2.6, link amalgamated free product decomposition
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a simple graph, and for each vertex v∈Γ let (M_v,φ_v) be a von Neumann algebra with normal faithful state. For a subgraph Λ⊆Γ write M_Λ for the graph product over Λ. If v∈Γ, put Γ_1=Star_Γ(v)={v}∪Link_Γ(v), Λ=Link_Γ(v), and Γ_2=Γ\{v}, and assume Γ_2 is nonempty. Then the graph product von Neumann algebra decomposes as a reduced amalgamated free product M_Γ = M_{Γ_1} ∗_{M_Λ} M_{Γ_2}, with respect to the canonical trace- or state-preserving conditional expectations onto M_Λ. The analogous statement holds for reduced C^*-algebraic graph products A_Γ = A_{Γ_1} ∗_{A_Λ} A_{Γ_2}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is recalled from Caspers-Fima. The graph-product relations split the vertices into the star of v and the complement Γ\{v}; the common relations between the two sides are exactly those through Link(v). Reduced graph-product words alternating between letters outside the link on the two sides are orthogonal to M_Link(v), matching the reduced amalgamated free product Fock-space construction. The canonical conditional expectations onto subgraph algebras agree with the amalgamating expectations.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.1, iterated conditional expectations in graph products
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a simple graph with subgraphs Γ_1,Γ_2⊆Γ. Let (M_Γ,φ_Γ)=∗_{v,Γ}(M_v,φ_v) be a graph product of von Neumann algebras with normal faithful states, and write E_{M_Λ}:M_Γ→M_Λ for the canonical conditional expectation onto a subgraph Λ⊆Γ. Let W_Γ be the right-angled Coxeter group of Γ, used to index reduced word types, and for a word s∈W_Γ let Link_Γ(s) be the intersection of the links of its letters. Suppose u,v∈W_Γ admit reduced decompositions u=u_l u_c u_r and v=v_l v_c v_r with u_l,v_l∈W_{Γ_1}, u_r,v_r∈W_{Γ_2}, and with u_c,v_c not starting with letters from Γ_1 and not ending with letters from Γ_2. If a=a_l a_c a_r and b=b_l b_c b_r are reduced operators with a_l∈˚M_{u_l}, a_c∈˚M_{u_c}, a_r∈˚M_{u_r}, b_l∈˚M_{v_l}, b_c∈˚M_{v_c}, and b_r∈˚M_{v_r}, then for every x∈M_Γ one has E_{M_{Γ_2}}(a* E_{M_{Γ_1}}(x) b) = φ_Γ(a_c* b_c) a_r* E_{M_{Γ_1∩Γ_2∩Link(u_c)}}(a_l* x b_l) b_r.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: First pull out the left and right parts lying in the target subgraph algebras: since a_l,b_l∈M_{Γ_1} and a_r,b_r∈M_{Γ_2}, the expression reduces to a_r* E_{M_{Γ_2}}(a_c* E_{M_{Γ_1}}(a_l* x b_l) b_c) b_r. The remaining central calculation is exactly the graph-product conditional-expectation computation from Charlesworth-de Santiago-Hayes-Jekel-Kunnawalkam Elayavalli-Nelson: the only part of E_{M_{Γ_1}}(a_l* x b_l) surviving multiplication by a_c*,b_c and projection to M_{Γ_2} is the component supported in Γ_1∩Γ_2∩Link(u_c), and the middle reduced-word overlap contributes the scalar φ_Γ(a_c* b_c). Combining the two reductions gives the displayed formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.3, relative amenability intersects over subgraph algebras
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a simple graph with subgraphs Γ_1,Γ_2⊆Γ. For each vertex v∈Γ let (M_v,τ_v) be a von Neumann algebra with normal faithful trace, and let M_Γ=∗_{v,Γ}(M_v,τ_v). Let P⊆M_Γ be a von Neumann subalgebra. If P is amenable relative to M_{Γ_1} inside M_Γ and also amenable relative to M_{Γ_2} inside M_Γ, then P is amenable relative to M_{Γ_1∩Γ_2} inside M_Γ. Here relative amenability means that there is a P-central positive functional on the Jones basic construction 1_P⟨M_Γ,e_{M_{Γ_i}}⟩1_P restricting to the trace on 1_P M_Γ 1_P.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: After reducing to unital inclusions, set Q_i=M_{Γ_i} and Q_0=Q_1∩Q_2=M_{Γ_1∩Γ_2}. Consider the Connes relative tensor product H=L^2(⟨M_Γ,e_{Q_1}⟩)⊗_{M_Γ} L^2(⟨M_Γ,e_{Q_2}⟩). The assumptions imply, by the standard characterization of relative amenability through weak containment of correspondences, that the M_Γ-P correspondence L^2(M_Γ) is weakly contained in H. A dense subspace of H is spanned by vectors x e_{Q_1} y ⊗_{M_Γ} e_{Q_2} z, where y is a reduced word not starting in Γ_1 and not ending in Γ_2. Proposition 5.1 gives the inner product identity E_{Q_0}(y* E_{Q_0}(x* x′) y′)=E_{Q_1}(y* E_{Q_2}(x* x′) y′), which identifies this dense subspace isometrically with a subspace of a correspondence built from L^2(M_Γ)⊗_{Q_0}L^2(M_Γ). Thus H is weakly contained in the correspondence witnessing relative amenability over Q_0, and P is amenable relative to M_{Γ_1∩Γ_2}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.8, quasi-normalizer localization from minimal subgraph intertwining
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a simple graph, and for each v∈Γ let (M_v,τ_v) be a tracial von Neumann algebra. Let Λ⊆Γ be a subgraph and let {Λ_j}_{j∈J} be a nonempty family of subgraphs of Γ. Define Λ_emb = Λ ∪ ⋂_{j∈J} ⋃_{v∈Λ\Λ_j} Link_Γ(v). Let A⊆1_A M_Γ 1_A be a von Neumann subalgebra. First, if A⊆1_A M_Λ 1_A and A does not intertwine into M_{Λ_j} inside M_Γ for any j∈J, then: (a) there is a net of unitaries u_i∈A such that ||E_{M_{Λ_emb}}(a u_i b)||_2→0 for all a,b∈1_A M_Γ 1_A with E_{M_{Λ_emb}}(a)=E_{M_{Λ_emb}}(b)=0; (b) 1_A qNor_{M_Γ}(A)′′ 1_A⊆M_{Λ_emb}; and (c) if u∈M_Γ is a unitary with u* A u⊆M_{Λ_emb}, then 1_A u 1_A∈M_{Λ_emb}. Second, if P=Nor_{M_Γ}(A)′′ and r∈P∩P′ is a projection such that rA≺_{M_Γ} M_Λ but rA does not intertwine into any M_{Λ_j}, then rP≺_{M_Γ} M_{Λ_emb}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For (a), Lemma 5.5 supplies a single net of unitaries u_i∈A that is asymptotically orthogonal to every M_{Λ_j}. Take reduced words b,c orthogonal to M_{Λ_emb}, decompose their word types relative to Λ_emb and Λ, and apply Proposition 5.1. Because the central word part contains a letter outside Λ_emb, one can choose j such that Λ∩Link(central word)⊆Λ_j. The resulting norm is bounded by a scalar multiple of ||E_{M_{Λ_j}}(b_l* u_i c_l)||_2, which tends to zero. Density extends this to all a,b orthogonal to M_{Λ_emb}. For (b) and (c), the mixing net from (a) is fed into Vaes's quasi-normalizer lemma: if Ax is finitely generated as a right A-module, or if Au⊆uM_{Λ_emb}, then the corner 1_A x 1_A, respectively 1_A u 1_A, must lie in M_{Λ_emb}. For the second part, use Popa intertwining to choose a partial isometry v and homomorphism θ:pAp→qM_Λq with θ(pAp) still avoiding all M_{Λ_j}. Applying the first part to θ(pAp) localizes its quasi-normalizer in M_{Λ_emb}. Cutting by central supports and conjugating normalizers of A through v yields an intertwining of rP into M_{Λ_emb}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.9 and Remark 5.10, diffuse vertex subalgebras cannot intertwine into subgraphs omitting the vertex
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a simple graph, and for each v∈Γ let (M_v,τ_v) be a tracial von Neumann algebra. Fix a vertex v∈Γ, let N⊆M_v be a diffuse von Neumann subalgebra, and let Λ⊆Γ be a subgraph. If N≺_{M_Γ} M_Λ, then v∈Λ. Consequently, when all vertex algebras M_v are II_1 factors and Λ⊆Γ is finite, qNor_{M_Γ}(M_Λ)′′ = Nor_{M_Γ}(M_Λ)′′ = M_{Λ∪Link_Γ(Λ)}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If v∉Λ, choose a net of unitaries u_k∈N with τ_v(u_k)=0 and u_k→0 weakly. For reduced words x,y∈M_Γ, write x=x′a and y=by′ with a,b∈M_v and with x′ not ending in v and y′ not starting in v. Split a u_k b into its scalar part τ_v(a u_k b) and its trace-zero part. The scalar coefficient tends to zero by weak convergence. The trace-zero part remains a reduced word containing the vertex v after commuting past link letters, hence its conditional expectation onto M_Λ is zero because v∉Λ. Thus ||E_{M_Λ}(x u_k y)||_2→0 for all reduced x,y and hence for all x,y by density, so Popa's criterion gives N not≺_{M_Γ}M_Λ. For the normalizer formula, M_{Λ∪Link(Λ)} plainly normalizes M_Λ; the reverse inclusion follows from Proposition 5.8 applied to all proper subgraphs of Λ, using Proposition 5.9 to see that M_Λ does not intertwine into any proper subgraph algebra.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.11, unitary conjugacy into the localization subgraph
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a simple graph, and for each v∈Γ let (M_v,τ_v) be a tracial II_1 factor. Let Q⊆M_Γ be a subfactor such that Q′∩M_Γ is also a factor. Let Λ⊆Γ be a subgraph and let {Λ_j}_{j∈J} be a nonempty collection of subgraphs of Λ. Define Λ_emb = Λ ∪ ⋂_{j∈J} ⋃_{v∈Λ\Λ_j} Link_Γ(v). If Q≺_{M_Γ}M_Λ and Q does not intertwine into M_{Λ_j} for any j∈J, then there is a unitary u∈M_Γ such that u* Q u⊆M_{Λ_emb}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Start from an intertwining Q≺M_Λ that is minimal relative to the Λ_j. Lemma 5.4 gives projections q∈Q and e∈M_Λ, a normal homomorphism θ:qQq→eM_Λe, and a nonzero partial isometry v∈eM_Γq with θ(x)v=vx, while θ(qQq) still avoids all M_{Λ_j}. Cut q to a subprojection q_0 of rational trace 1/m so that v_0=vq_0 remains nonzero. Proposition 5.8 localizes θ(q_0Qq_0)'s normalizer in M_{Λ_emb}. Since Q is a factor, choose partial isometries in Q whose initial projections are q_0 and whose final projections sum to 1; these produce a nonzero projection q′∈Q′∩M_Γ from v_0* v_0. Because Q′∩M_Γ is a factor, cut q′ to rational trace 1/n and choose partial isometries in Q′∩M_Γ summing to 1. The resulting smaller intertwiner v_{00} has range projection v_{00}v_{00}* lying in M_{Λ_emb}. Since M_{Λ_emb} is a factor, choose partial isometries w_{j,k}∈M_{Λ_emb} with common range v_{00}v_{00}* and initial projections summing to 1. The explicit sum u=Σ_{j,k} u_j u′_k v_{00}* w_{j,k} is unitary, and a matrix-unit calculation gives u*Q u⊆M_{Λ_emb}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 7.3, Vaes normalizer dichotomy for amalgamated free products
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (N_1,τ_1) and (N_2,τ_2) be tracial von Neumann algebras with a common von Neumann subalgebra B⊆N_i such that τ_1|_B=τ_2|_B, and let N=N_1∗_B N_2 be their tracial amalgamated free product. Let A⊆1_A N 1_A be a von Neumann subalgebra that is amenable relative to N_1 or to N_2 inside N. Put P=Nor_{1_A N 1_A}(A)′′. Then at least one of the following holds: A≺_N B; P≺_N N_i for some i∈{1,2}; or P is amenable relative to B inside N.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This theorem is quoted from Vaes and is not proved in the paper. In the paper it is applied to the link decomposition M_Γ=M_Star(v)∗_{M_Link(v)}M_{Γ\{v}}. The trichotomy says that an amenable subalgebra of an amalgamated free product must either intertwine into the amalgam, have its normalizer intertwine into one free-product side, or have its normalizer remain amenable relative to the amalgam.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 7.8, amenability classification for graph products
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a simple graph, and for each v∈Γ let (M_v,φ_v) be a von Neumann algebra not equal to C with normal faithful state. Let M_Γ=∗_{v,Γ}(M_v,φ_v). Then M_Γ is amenable if and only if both conditions hold: every vertex algebra M_v is amenable; and whenever distinct vertices v,w∈Γ do not share an edge, one has dim M_v=dim M_w=2 and Link_Γ({v,w})=Γ\{v,w}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this as Proposition 6.3 of Charlesworth-de Santiago-Hayes-Jekel-Kunnawalkam Elayavalli-Nelson and does not reprove it. The quoted classification isolates the only way non-edge free-product behavior can remain amenable: both endpoint algebras must be two-dimensional and all remaining vertices must commute with both endpoints.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 7.9, diffuseness criterion under trace-zero vertex unitaries
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (M_Γ,τ_Γ)=∗_{v,Γ}(M_v,τ_v) be a graph product of tracial von Neumann algebras over a simple graph Γ. Assume that for every vertex v∈Γ there is a unitary u_v∈M_v with τ_v(u_v)=0. Then M_Γ is diffuse if either at least one vertex algebra M_v is diffuse, or Γ is not a complete graph.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper quotes this from Theorem E of Charlesworth-de Santiago-Hayes-Jekel-Kunnawalkam Elayavalli-Nelson. The underlying mechanism is that trace-zero unitaries supply nontrivial reduced words; if the graph is not complete, alternating reduced words generate diffuse behavior even if all vertex algebras are finite-dimensional.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 8.2, in irreducible graphs normalizer non-intertwining is equivalent to algebra non-intertwining
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a finite irreducible simple graph. For each v∈Γ let M_v≠C be a von Neumann algebra with normal faithful trace, and let M_Γ be the tracial graph product. Let N⊆M_Γ be a diffuse von Neumann subalgebra. Then the following are equivalent: N does not intertwine into M_{Γ_0} for any strict subgraph Γ_0⊊Γ; and Nor_{M_Γ}(N)′′ does not intertwine into M_{Γ_0} for any strict subgraph Γ_0⊊Γ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: One direction is immediate because N⊆Nor_{M_Γ}(N)′′. Conversely, choose a subgraph Λ⊆Γ minimal with N≺M_Λ and N not≺M_{λ} for every strict subgraph λ⊊Λ. Since N is diffuse, Λ is nonempty. Apply Proposition 5.8 to the family of all strict subgraphs of Λ; it gives Nor_{M_Γ}(N)′′≺M_{Λ∪Link(Λ)}. If the normalizer does not intertwine into a strict subgraph of Γ, then Λ∪Link(Λ)=Γ. Irreducibility of Γ rules out a nontrivial decomposition Γ=Λ∪Link(Λ) with both parts nonempty, so Link(Λ) must be empty and hence Λ=Γ. Thus N itself cannot intertwine into any proper subgraph.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 8.3, prime-or-amenable dichotomy for irreducible graph products
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a finite irreducible graph with |Γ|≥2. For each v∈Γ let M_v≠C be a von Neumann algebra with normal faithful trace, and let M_Γ be the tracial graph product. Suppose M_Γ is a II_1 factor and M_Γ does not intertwine into M_{Γ_0} for any strict subgraph Γ_0⊊Γ. Then M_Γ is either prime or amenable.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Assume M_Γ is not prime, so M_Γ=N_1⊗N_2 with diffuse factors N_1,N_2. Since Nor(N_1)′′=M_Γ and M_Γ avoids all proper subgraph algebras, Lemma 8.2 implies N_1 avoids all proper subgraph algebras. Choose a diffuse amenable von Neumann subalgebra A⊆N_2. Then Nor(A)′′ contains N_1, so Nor(A)′′ also avoids every proper subgraph algebra, and Lemma 8.2 implies A itself avoids every proper subgraph algebra. For each vertex v, use the amalgamated free product M_Γ=M_Star(v)∗_{M_Link(v)}M_{Γ\{v}} and apply Vaes's trichotomy to A. The alternatives A≺M_Link(v) and Nor(A)′′≺M_Star(v) or M_{Γ\{v}} are impossible because those are proper subgraph algebras. Hence Nor(A)′′ is amenable relative to M_Link(v) for every v. Repeatedly applying Theorem 5.3 over all vertices gives amenability relative to ⋂_v M_Link(v)=C, so Nor(A)′′ is amenable. Thus N_1 is amenable; by symmetry N_2 is amenable; hence M_Γ is amenable.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 8.8, reduced-word conditional expectation vanishing
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a simple graph, let Λ⊆Γ be a subgraph, and for each vertex v let (M_v,τ_v) be tracial. Let u∈W_Γ\W_Λ, where W_Γ is the right-angled Coxeter group indexing graph-product word types. Let v,v′∈W_Γ be such that every letter at the start of v, respectively v′, does not commute with any letter at the end of u^{-1}, respectively u. Let w,w′∈W_Γ satisfy |w|≤|v| and |w′|≤|v′|. Then for all a∈˚M_w, x∈˚M_{v^{-1} u v′}, and b∈˚M_{w′}, one has E_{M_Λ}(a x b)=0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Define H(u)=⊕_{s∈W(u)}˚H_s and M(u)=⊕_{s∈W(u)}˚M_s, where W(u) consists of words that remain reduced after left multiplication by u. If y_1∈M(u^{-1}), y_2∈˚M_u, and y_3∈M(u), then every reduced component of y_1* y_2 y_3 contains u as a subword; since u∉W_Λ, its expectation onto M_Λ is zero. Write x=x_1* x_2 x_3 with x_1∈˚M_v, x_2∈˚M_u, x_3∈˚M_{v′}. Using the creation-annihilation decomposition of graph-product left multiplication, Lemma 2.6 shows that x_3 b lies in H(u) because |w′|≤|v′| and the starting-letter noncommutation prevents cancellation of u; similarly x_1 a* lies in H(u^{-1}). Therefore a x b has the form y_1* y_2 y_3 with y_1∈M(u^{-1}), y_2∈˚M_u, y_3∈M(u), and its M_Λ-expectation is zero.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 8.10, trace-zero unitaries force M_Γ not to intertwine into any proper subgraph
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a simple graph with |Γ|≥3 and Star_Γ(v)≠Γ for every vertex v∈Γ. For each v∈Γ let (M_v,τ_v) be a tracial von Neumann algebra containing a unitary u_v with τ_v(u_v)=0. Then for every strict subgraph Λ⊊Γ, the graph product M_Γ does not intertwine into M_Λ inside M_Γ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix Λ⊊Γ and choose a vertex v∈Γ\Λ. Under |Γ|≥3 and Star(s)≠Γ for all s, the right-angled Coxeter group W_Γ is icc, so the conjugacy class of v is infinite. Choose elements v_n∈W_Γ with |v_n^{-1} v v_n|≥2n+1. By multiplying v_n on the left by initial letters commuting with v, one may assume every letter at the start of v_n does not commute with v; the conjugate v_n^{-1} v v_n remains unchanged and is reduced, and |v_n|≥n. If v_n^{-1} v v_n=s_1...s_l is reduced, define U_n=u_{s_1}...u_{s_l}∈˚M_{v_n^{-1} v v_n}; it is a unitary because the u_s are vertex unitaries and the word is reduced. For reduced coefficients a∈˚M_w and b∈˚M_{w′} with |w|,|w′|≤n, Lemma 8.8 gives E_{M_Λ}(a U_n b)=0. Approximate arbitrary x,y∈M_Γ in L^2 by finite sums of reduced words whose lengths are ≤n; for large n, the expectation E_{M_Λ}(x U_n y) is arbitrarily small in L^2. Hence ||E_{M_Λ}(x U_n y)||_2→0 for all x,y∈M_Γ, and Popa's criterion gives M_Γ not≺_{M_Γ}M_Λ.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 8.4, primeness for graph products of II_1 factors
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a finite simple graph with |Γ|≥2. For each v∈Γ let M_v be a II_1 factor with its trace. Then the tracial graph product M_Γ=∗_{v,Γ}(M_v,τ_v) is prime if and only if Γ is irreducible, meaning that Γ is not a nontrivial join Γ=Γ_1∪Γ_2 with Γ_1,Γ_2 nonempty and Link_Γ(Γ_1)=Γ_2.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If Γ is irreducible, M_Γ is a factor by Caspers-Fima. Proposition 5.9 implies M_Γ cannot intertwine into any proper subgraph algebra, because any omitted vertex algebra is diffuse. Lemma 8.3 then gives that M_Γ is prime or amenable. Irreducibility with |Γ|≥2 makes Γ non-complete, and Proposition 7.8 rules out amenability for graph products of II_1 factors over such a graph. Hence M_Γ is prime. Conversely, if Γ is reducible, write Γ=Γ_1∪Γ_2 with Γ_1,Γ_2 nonempty and Link(Γ_1)=Γ_2. Then M_Γ=M_{Γ_1}⊗M_{Γ_2}, and each tensor factor is a diffuse II_1 factor, so M_Γ is not prime.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 8.11, prime factor result under trace-zero vertex unitaries
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be an irreducible finite simple graph with |Γ|≥3. For each v∈Γ let M_v≠C be a von Neumann algebra with normal faithful trace τ_v, and assume M_v contains a unitary u_v with τ_v(u_v)=0. Then the tracial graph product M_Γ=∗_{v,Γ}(M_v,τ_v) is a prime factor.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper first invokes Theorem E of Charlesworth-de Santiago-Hayes-Jekel-Kunnawalkam Elayavalli-Nelson to conclude from the trace-zero unitary hypothesis and irreducibility that M_Γ is a II_1 factor. Since Γ is irreducible and |Γ|≥3, no vertex has Star(v)=Γ; otherwise Γ would split as {v} joined with Γ\{v}. Lemma 8.10 therefore gives M_Γ not≺M_Λ for every strict subgraph Λ⊊Γ. Lemma 8.3 implies M_Γ is prime or amenable. Finally, Proposition 7.8 rules out amenability: irreducibility with |Γ|≥3 excludes the exceptional amenable non-edge configuration Link({v,w})=Γ\{v,w}, and any nonamenable vertex would already rule out amenability. Thus M_Γ is prime.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 9.1, free-product decomposability exactly detects disconnected graphs for II_1 factor vertices
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Γ be a simple graph with |Γ|≥2, and for each v∈Γ let M_v be a II_1 factor with separable predual and trace τ_v. Let M_Γ=∗_{v,Γ}(M_v,τ_v). Then M_Γ can be decomposed as a tracial free product M_Γ=(M_1,τ_1)∗(M_2,τ_2) of II_1 factors if and only if Γ is not connected.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If Γ is disconnected, any connected component Γ_0 gives M_Γ=M_{Γ_0}∗M_{Γ\Γ_0}. Conversely, suppose Γ is connected and M_Γ=M_1∗M_2. Fix v∈Γ and choose, by Ozawa-Popa, an amenable II_1 subfactor N_0⊆M_v with N_0′∩M_Γ=M_v′∩M_Γ=M_Link(v). Apply Vaes's trichotomy to the free product over C. The alternative N_0≺C is impossible because N_0 is diffuse. If Nor(N_0)′′≺M_i, the unitary-conjugacy method of Theorem 5.11 places N_0 into M_i; then Proposition 5.8 propagates this containment through normalizers along every edge path in the connected graph, forcing every vertex algebra M_w, and hence all of M_Γ, into a conjugate of M_i, contradiction. Thus Nor(N_0)′′ must be amenable. Since M_Link(v)⊆Nor(N_0)′′, every link algebra is amenable. Proposition 7.8 then implies each Link(v) is a clique and its vertex algebras are amenable. A connected graph whose every link is a clique is complete, so M_Γ is an amenable tensor product of amenable II_1 factors. But the hyperfinite II_1 factor cannot be a nontrivial free product of II_1 factors, giving the contradiction.

proof_techniques.name: Link-based amalgamated free product decomposition
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly decomposes a graph product around a vertex v as M_Γ=M_Star(v)∗_{M_Link(v)}M_{Γ\{v}}. This turns graph-product questions into amalgamated free product questions where Vaes's normalizer dichotomy applies: an amenable subalgebra either intertwines into the link algebra, has normalizer intertwining into one side, or has normalizer amenable relative to the link.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For the original irreducible graph, Star(v) is always proper when |Γ|≥3, so applying this decomposition at all vertices can force any amenability obstruction to be relative to all link algebras simultaneously. Combined with the intersection theorem, this may collapse relative amenability to amenability over C.

proof_techniques.name: Conditional-expectation calculus for graph-product reduced words
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 5.1 gives a precise formula for E_{M_{Γ_2}}(a*E_{M_{Γ_1}}(x)b) when a and b are reduced words decomposed into parts lying in Γ_1, Γ_2, and a middle part. The formula identifies the surviving component as an expectation onto Γ_1∩Γ_2∩Link(middle word), multiplied by a scalar inner product of the middle word components.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is the main computational tool for proving vanishing of conditional expectations onto proper subgraph algebras. In the original trace-zero unitary setting, it underlies the construction of long reduced unitaries witnessing M_Γ not≺M_Λ for proper Λ.

proof_techniques.name: Long conjugate trace-zero unitaries
proof_techniques.description: In Lemma 8.10, trace-zero unitaries in the vertex algebras are multiplied along long reduced words v_n^{-1} v v_n in the right-angled Coxeter group W_Γ. These products are unitaries in M_Γ supported on reduced word spaces whose central subword contains a vertex outside a chosen proper subgraph Λ, so their conditional expectations onto M_Λ vanish against all bounded-length reduced coefficients.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This technique uses exactly the original hypothesis that every M_v has a trace-zero unitary. It supplies a concrete sequence or net of unitaries that can rule out embeddings into proper subgraph algebras, a likely ingredient in excluding join/tensor/amenability obstructions to proper proximality.

proof_techniques.name: Relative amenability intersection
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 5.3 shows that if a subalgebra P is amenable relative to two subgraph algebras M_{Γ_1} and M_{Γ_2}, then it is amenable relative to M_{Γ_1∩Γ_2}. The proof identifies the relevant Jones basic construction correspondence over the two subgraphs with a correspondence over the intersection using the conditional-expectation formula.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a proof of proper proximality produces relative amenability to multiple links Link(v), this theorem can iterate over vertices. Since ⋂_v Link(v)=∅, one can often promote relative amenability to actual amenability, contradicting nonamenability.

proof_techniques.name: Quasi-normalizer localization by minimal subgraph support
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 5.8 says that if a subalgebra A lies in a subgraph algebra M_Λ but does not intertwine into specified smaller subgraph algebras, then its quasi-normalizer is forced into the enlarged subgraph Λ_emb built from Λ and appropriate link vertices. In the common minimal case where the smaller subgraphs are all proper subgraphs of Λ, this enlargement is Λ∪Link(Λ).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Irreducibility is precisely a condition ruling out nontrivial Λ∪Link(Λ)=Γ decompositions. Thus this localization can be used to show that large normalizers cannot be supported on proper subgraphs unless a join obstruction exists.

proof_techniques.name: Irreducibility as exclusion of join/tensor obstructions
proof_techniques.description: The paper uses irreducibility in the form: if Λ⊆Γ is nonempty and Λ∪Link(Λ)=Γ, then either Λ=Γ or Γ decomposes as a nontrivial join, contradicting irreducibility. This observation is central in Lemma 8.2 and in primeness arguments.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Proper proximality is often obstructed by tensor product or join phenomena. The exact graph-theoretic irreducibility hypothesis in the original problem is designed to rule out these obstructions, and the paper provides a tested way to convert that into operator-algebraic non-intertwining.

proof_techniques.name: Normalizer propagation along graph paths
proof_techniques.description: In the proof of Theorem 9.1, once a diffuse amenable subfactor of one vertex algebra is conjugated into one side of a free product decomposition, quasi-normalizer control forces adjacent vertex algebras into the same side. Repeating along paths propagates the containment to the whole connected component.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For connected components of the original graph, this provides a way to propagate local information from one vertex or link to all vertices. If proper proximality is first shown for components or for a subgraph, this technique may help extend it across connected paths.

proof_techniques.name: Using trace-zero unitaries for diffuseness, factoriality, and nonamenability inputs
proof_techniques.description: The paper repeatedly invokes results from Charlesworth-de Santiago-Hayes-Jekel-Kunnawalkam Elayavalli-Nelson showing that trace-zero vertex unitaries give strong structural consequences for graph products, including diffuseness criteria and, in Section 8, factoriality under the relevant irreducibility assumptions. Amenability is then ruled out using the graph-product amenability classification.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original hypotheses match this trace-zero unitary framework. Before proving proper proximality, one can likely use the paper's extracted consequences to reduce to a nonamenable II_1 factor with no proper-subgraph intertwining.

other_useful_info: The paper itself does not prove proper proximality for general graph product von Neumann algebras. Its Theorem 8.11 nevertheless matches the original graph-theoretic and trace-zero-unitary hypotheses closely and proves that the corresponding graph product is a prime factor. Remark 9.5 cites Ding-Kunnawalkam Elayavalli [26, Proposition 3.7] for proper proximality of certain graph product groups, specifically examples ∗_{v,Γ_i}F_2 when Radius(Γ_i)≥3; this is only a related-work pointer in the paper, not a theorem proved there. Section 8 shows that the trace-zero unitary assumption is used concretely to build long reduced-word unitaries, not merely for abstract factoriality. Proposition 7.8 is useful for ruling out amenability: in an irreducible graph with at least three vertices, the exceptional amenable non-edge configuration Link({v,w})=Γ\{v,w} would be a join decomposition and is therefore excluded.
why_relevant: Develops rigidity results for von Neumann algebraic graph products and proves technical theorems on relative amenability and embeddings of quasi-normalizers. Those tools are relevant for controlling subgraph subalgebras and excluding tensor/join-type obstructions in irreducible graph products.

title: On Relative Biexactness of Amalgamated Free Product von Neumann Algebras
overall_summary: The paper proves relative biexactness results for amalgamated free product von Neumann algebras M = M_1 *_B M_2 and, in the body, for finitely many factors. Its main technical theorem is a Toeplitz-Pimsner factorization showing that, under weak exactness of the free components and injectivity of the amalgam, the reduced AFP embeds M-nuclearly into an AFP of basic constructions; this embedding is then shown to lie in an appropriate small-at-infinity boundary. In the injective-component case, the authors improve relative biexactness from relative to the free factors to relative to the amalgam B, and if B is mixing they upgrade to genuine biexactness. The paper also develops bidual and mixing-subalgebra machinery to decompose subalgebras of relatively biexact tracial von Neumann algebras into properly proximal, amenable, and relatively amenable pieces. For the original graph-product problem, the most transferable parts are the AFP-over-link viewpoint, the commutator localization into boundary ideals, the mixing lemmas for AFPs, and the bidual central-state/upgrading arguments for proper proximality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 2.2 construction: standard representation of AFP von Neumann algebras
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M_1 and M_2 be von Neumann algebras containing a common unital von Neumann subalgebra B with faithful normal conditional expectations E_i: M_i -> B. Let L2 M_i^o = L2 M_i ⊖ L2 B and let F = L2 B ⊕ direct sum over alternating words i_1 != ... != i_n of L2 M_{i_1}^o tensor_B ... tensor_B L2 M_{i_n}^o, with relative tensor products over B. If N_i = <M_i,e_B> is the Jones basic construction for B ⊂ M_i, then identifying F with L2 M_i tensor_B F(i), where F(i) consists of alternating tensors not starting with i, gives a representation lambda_i: N_i -> B(F). The AFP von Neumann algebra is M = M_1 *_B M_2 = (lambda_1(M_1) ∪ lambda_2(M_2))'', and the reduced AFP C*-algebra is M_r = C*(lambda_1(M_1),lambda_2(M_2)). The conditional expectation M -> B is x -> i_B^* x i_B, where i_B: L2 B -> F is the vacuum inclusion. The right action is similarly generated by representations rho_i of M_i^op using F ≅ F'(i) tensor_B L2 M_i, and M' is generated by these right actions.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a construction rather than a theorem. The key point is to use W*-correspondences and relative tensor products, avoiding any assumption that B is sigma-finite or carries a fixed faithful normal state. The left action of N_i on the first tensor leg is well-defined because L2 M_i is naturally an N_i-B correspondence. The conditional expectation onto B is the vacuum vector/state compression. The same Fock-space decomposition from the right gives the commuting right action and identifies the commutant of the AFP representation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definitions 3.4, 3.5, 3.7 and Lemma 3.6: small-at-infinity boundary, proper proximality, and biexactness
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M ⊂ B(L2 M) be a von Neumann algebra and let X be an M-boundary piece, meaning a nonzero hereditary C*-subalgebra X ⊂ B(L2 M) whose multiplier algebra meets M and M' ultraweakly densely. Define K_X(M) from X by first taking the closure of B(L2 M)X in the C-M and C-M' topologies, then forming the hereditary algebra (K_X^L(M))* K_X^L(M). Define K_X^{∞,1}(M) to be the closure of K_X(M) in the M-M and M'-M' bimodule topology. The small-at-infinity boundary relative to X is S_X(M) = {T in B(L2 M): [T,x] belongs to K_X^{∞,1}(M) for every x in M'}. M is properly proximal relative to X if there is no M-central state on S_X(M) whose restriction to M is normal. M is biexact relative to X if the inclusion M -> S_X(M) is M-nuclear. In the special case X = K(L2 M), these are called proper proximality and biexactness. Lemma 3.6 says that if M is separable and tracial, then M is properly proximal relative to X iff there is no M-central state phi on the bidual boundary tilde S_X(M) = {T in (B(L2 M)_J^#)^*: [T,a] belongs to (K_X(M)_J^#)^* for all a in JMJ} such that phi restricted to M is normal.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The definitions are those of Ding-Elayavalli-Peterson and Ding-Peterson, expressed through Magajna-type bimodule topologies. The bidual criterion in Lemma 3.6 passes from the concrete operator-system boundary S_X(M) to the compressed bidual B(L2 M)_J^#*, where both the left M-action and the right JMJ-action are normal. The key input is that, in the separable tracial case, states on the concrete boundary normal on M extend or correspond to states on the bidual boundary after compressing by the normal-support projection p_nor^#; centrality and normality are preserved. This criterion is repeatedly used later to manipulate central states by support projections q_X inside the bidual.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Example 3.8: boundary pieces generated by subalgebras
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let B_family = {(B_i,E_i)}_{i in I} be a family of von Neumann subalgebras B_i ⊂ M with faithful normal conditional expectations E_i: M -> B_i. Let e_{B_i} be the orthogonal projection L2 M -> L2 B_i. The boundary piece X_{B_family} is the hereditary C*-subalgebra of B(L2 M) generated by x J y J e_{B_i}, where x,y range over M and i ranges over I. M is called biexact relative to {B_i} if it is biexact relative to this boundary piece. For an AFP M = M_1 *_B M_2, if e_i denotes the projection L2 M -> L2 M_i, then the boundary piece generated by e_1 B(L2 M)e_1 and e_2 B(L2 M)e_2 is X_{ {M_1,M_2} }.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is a definition/example. It identifies the von Neumann algebraic analogue of relative group biexactness: for group von Neumann algebras, DP23 proves that LΓ is biexact relative to {LΛ: Λ in G} iff Γ is biexact relative to the subgroup family G. In the AFP situation, the projections e_i onto the component subalgebras give the concrete hereditary boundary used in the paper's relative biexactness proofs.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.1: weak exactness detected through an injective-amalgam basic construction
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a von Neumann algebra and A ⊂ M a unital ultraweakly dense C*-subalgebra. Let B ⊂ M be an injective von Neumann subalgebra with a normal conditional expectation E: M -> B, and let N = <M,e_B> be the Jones basic construction. Then A is weakly exact in M if and only if the inclusion A -> N is (A ⊂ M)-nuclear, i.e. pointwise approximable in the (A ⊂ M)-topology by completely positive contractions factoring through matrix algebras.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: If A is weakly exact in M, DP23 gives c.c.p. factorizations A -> M_k(C) -> B(L2 M) converging to the standard inclusion in the (A ⊂ M)-topology, with additional control by small-norm and ultrastrongly null terms. Since B is injective, the basic construction N is injective, so there is a conditional expectation B(L2 M) -> N. Composing the second leg of the approximations with this expectation gives c.c.p. factorizations into N. For functionals normal on A from both sides, the controlled error still tends to zero, proving (A ⊂ M)-nuclearity. Conversely, if A -> N is (A ⊂ M)-nuclear, then composing with the inclusion N ⊂ B(L2 M) gives (A ⊂ M)-nuclearity of A -> B(L2 M), which is equivalent to weak exactness by DP23.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3: K_X^{∞,1}(M) is the M-M and M'-M' closure of X
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X be an M-boundary piece in B(L2 M). Then K_X^{∞,1}(M), originally defined as the M-M and M'-M' closure of K_X(M), equals the closure of X itself in the same M-M and M'-M' topology: K_X^{∞,1}(M) = closure_{M-M, M'-M'}(X).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Since X ⊂ K_X(M), one inclusion is immediate. For the reverse inclusion, write a typical element of K_X(M) as S*R with S,R in K_X^L(M). Approximate S and R by nets S_n,R_n in B(L2 M)X in the C-M and C-M' topologies. The paper uses the seminorms r_omega for the M-M and M'-M' topology and rr_omega for the one-sided C-topologies. If S_n - S and R_n - R have decompositions controlled by small right coefficients in M and M', then (S_n-S)*(R_n-R) is small in r_omega. The mixed terms (S_n-S)*R and S*(R_n-R) are small because C-M and C-M' convergence is stronger than the two-sided topology after multiplication by a bounded operator. Thus S_n*R_n -> S*R in the M-M and M'-M' topology, and S_n*R_n belongs to X because X is hereditary.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.10 and Lemma 3.11: relative solidity from relative biexactness
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be sigma-finite, let {B_j}_{j in J} be a countable family of von Neumann subalgebras with expectations, and suppose M is biexact relative to X_{ {B_j} }. Let N ⊂ M be a finite von Neumann subalgebra with expectation. Then either N embeds with expectation into some B_j inside M in Popa's intertwining sense, written N ⪯_M B_j, or the relative commutant N' ∩ M is amenable. Lemma 3.11 supplies the key dynamical input: if N is finite with expectation and N ⪯̸_M B_j for every j, then there exists a net of unitaries u_i in U(N) such that Ad(u_i)(S) = u_i^* S u_i converges ultraweakly to 0 for every S in K_{X_{ {B_j} }}^{∞,1}(M).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 3.11, Popa's intertwining criterion gives a net u_i in U(N) with E_{B_j}(a u_i b) -> 0 strongly for all a,b in M and all j. One first checks on generators a_1 b_1 e_{B_{j_1}} T e_{B_{j_2}} b_2 a_2 of X_{ {B_j} } that u_i^*(.)u_i tends ultraweakly to 0, using Cauchy-Schwarz and the strong convergence of the expectations. Since u_i commutes with M' and the span of such generators is dense in X, the conclusion holds on X. Lemma 3.3 then extends it to K_X^{∞,1}(M), using estimates in the r_phi seminorms associated to a faithful normal state whose centralizer contains N. For Proposition 3.10, take a point-ultraweak cluster point theta of Ad(u_i). It annihilates K_X^{∞,1}(M) and fixes N' ∩ M, hence maps S_X(M) into M. Choosing a faithful normal state phi on M that is tracial on N and preserved by the expectation onto N makes theta|_M normal. Since M -> S_X(M) is M-nuclear and theta is weak-M continuous, the inclusion N' ∩ M -> M becomes weakly nuclear; composing with the expectation M -> N' ∩ M gives amenability.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 4.2-4.6: Toeplitz creation operator identities and compression onto an AFP of basic constructions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the two-factor AFP setting with common B, set K = K_1 ⊕ K_2, where K_i is the direct sum of all nonempty alternating relative tensor products starting with L2 M_i. Let N_i = <M_i,e_B>. There is a representation of N_1 ⊕ N_2 on K by action on the first tensor leg and an isometry T on K that prepends the opposite B-vacuum leg. Lemma 4.2 states that T^*(a_1 ⊕ a_2)T = E_2(a_2) ⊕ E_1(a_1) for a_i in N_i, where E_i: N_i -> B is the canonical compression expectation. Lemma 4.3 identifies C*(A_1 ⊕ A_2,T) with the Toeplitz-Pimsner algebra of the C*-correspondence associated to the u.c.p. map (a_1,a_2) -> (E_2(a_2),E_1(a_1)). With subspaces K_{i,j} of pK, where p = 1 - T^2(T*)^2, and natural unitaries V_{i,j}: K_{i,j} -> L2(M_1 *_B M_2), Lemma 4.5 gives formulas such as T^* V_{i,j}^* = V_{i+1,j}^* V_{i+1,j} T^* V_{i,j}^* and V_{i+1,j} T^* V_{i,j}^* equals either lambda_{i+1}(e_B)^⊥ or lambda_i(e_B), depending on whether i=j. Corollary 4.6 defines a u.c.p. map Phi: B(K) -> B(L2 M) by Phi(x) = 1/4 sum_{i,j,k,l=1}^2 V_{i,j} x V_{k,l}^*, and proves Phi(C*(N_{1,0} ⊕ N_{2,0},T)) = N_{1,0} *_B N_{2,0} whenever C*(A_i,e_B) ⊂ N_{i,0} ⊂ N_i. Also Phi(T)=1/2 and Phi(T^2(T*)^2)=0.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 4.2, one approximates the formal B-vacuum vector by finite-weight projections e_j in B and computes T^* a_i T on vectors in the dense domain of right-bounded vectors. The result is exactly compression of a_i to B on the newly created first leg. Lemma 4.3 uses the Toeplitz-Pimsner universal representation: tau(a tensor b)=aTb satisfies the correspondence relations because T^* a T is the u.c.p. map above, and gauge-invariant uniqueness identifies the generated algebra with T(H). For Lemma 4.5, the spaces K_{i,j} record first and last letters. Applying T or T^* shifts the first letter, and after transferring via V_{i,j} this becomes either a projection onto words beginning with a given component or its complement; the identities follow from direct tensor-word inspection. Corollary 4.6 reduces every word in N_1,N_2,T,T* using T^* a T = E(a), so it has a normal form c_1 T ... T c_m T a T* d_1 T* ... d_n. Moving the V_{i,j} compressions through such a word via Lemma 4.5 expresses each compressed term as an element of N_{1,0} *_B N_{2,0}. The reverse inclusion follows because Phi(n_1 ⊕ n_2)=1/2 lambda_1(n_1)+1/2 lambda_2(n_2).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.1 / Proposition 4.9: M-nuclear embedding of AFPs into AFPs of basic constructions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For i=1,...,n, let M_i be von Neumann algebras with a common von Neumann subalgebra B and faithful normal conditional expectations E_i: M_i -> B. Let A_i ⊂ M_i be ultraweakly dense unital C*-subalgebras such that E_i(A_i)=D, where D is a common C*-subalgebra of all A_i. Let N_i=<M_i,e_B>, and let N_{i,0} be C*-subalgebras satisfying C*(A_i,e_B) ⊂ N_{i,0} ⊂ N_i. If each inclusion A_i -> N_{i,0} is (A_i ⊂ M_i)-nuclear, then the inclusion A = A_1 *_D ... *_D A_n -> N_{1,0} *_B ... *_B N_{n,0} is (A ⊂ M)-nuclear, where M = M_1 *_B ... *_B M_n and the left side is the reduced AFP C*-algebra.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is first done for n=2 using the Toeplitz-Pimsner construction summarized in Lemmas 4.2-4.6. The nuclearity of A_i -> N_{i,0} implies nuclearity of A_1 ⊕ A_2 -> N_{1,0} ⊕ N_{2,0}. A lemma from Toy25 applied to the Toeplitz-Pimsner algebra gives a weakly nuclear *-homomorphism pi from T(H)=C*(A_1 ⊕ A_2,T) into the normal bidual of C*(N_{1,0} ⊕ N_{2,0},T), agreeing with the canonical inclusion on A_1 ⊕ A_2 and on the correspondence creation operators. Define p=1-T^2(T*)^2 and u=p(T+T*)p. Brown-Ozawa's AFP u.c.p. construction gives maps psi_i(a)=pap+uau from A_i into pT(H)p that agree on D and hence a u.c.p. map Psi: A_1 *_D A_2 -> pT(H)p, multiplicative on reduced words. The compression Phi from Lemmas 4.2-4.6 extends normally to the relevant bidual. The identities Phi(T)=1/2 and Phi(T^2(T*)^2)=0 imply that pi(p) and pi(u) lie in the multiplicative domain of Phi after bidual compression. Therefore Phi pi Psi(a)=lambda_i(a) for a in A_i, and the multiplicative-domain argument gives Phi pi Psi equal to the canonical inclusion on the whole AFP A. Since pi is weakly nuclear and Phi is normal, this canonical inclusion is weakly nuclear into the normal bidual, hence (A ⊂ M)-nuclear by DP23. For n>2, group the first n-1 factors, apply the two-factor case inductively, and note that C*(A_1 *_D ... *_D A_{n-1},e_B) is contained in N_{1,0} *_B ... *_B N_{n-1,0}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.10 / Theorem 1.1: weakly exact or injective inputs give concrete nuclear embeddings
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M_i, i=1,...,n, have a common von Neumann subalgebra B with faithful normal conditional expectations, and let M = M_1 *_B ... *_B M_n. If each M_i is weakly exact and B is injective, then the inclusion M_r = M_1 *_B ... *_B M_n -> N_1 *_B ... *_B N_n is (M_r ⊂ M)-nuclear, where N_i=<M_i,e_B>. If each M_i is injective, then the inclusion M_r -> C*(M_1,e_B) *_B ... *_B C*(M_n,e_B) = C*(M_i,lambda_i(e_B): i=1,...,n) is (M_r ⊂ M)-nuclear.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: In the weakly exact case, apply Lemma 3.1 to each inclusion B ⊂ M_i: because B is injective, M_i weak exact implies M_i -> N_i is M_i-nuclear. Theorem 4.1 with A_i=M_i and N_{i,0}=N_i yields the first statement. In the injective case, the identity inclusion M_i -> M_i is M_i-nuclear; since C*(M_i,e_B) contains M_i, the inclusion M_i -> C*(M_i,e_B) is M_i-nuclear, and Theorem 4.1 with N_{i,0}=C*(M_i,e_B) yields the second statement.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 4.11, Lemma 4.12, and Remark 4.13: Condition (A) and tracial approximation in the basic construction
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For an inclusion B ⊂ M with faithful normal conditional expectation E: M -> B, Condition (A) means e_B <M,e_B> is contained in the C-M closure of e_B M, where e_B is the Jones projection. Lemma 4.12 says Condition (A) holds if M is tracial with faithful normal trace tau and tau composed with E equals tau. Remark 4.13 says Condition (A) also holds if B is finite-dimensional, even without traciality of M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: In the tracial case, Magajna's result identifies the C-M topology on bounded sets with the seminorm s_tau^r. Given T in <M,e_B> with norm at most 1, view T tau^{1/2} as an affiliated operator L_{T tau^{1/2}} and let P_n be the spectral projection of L*L for [0,n]. Decompose e_B T* as e_B T* P_n plus e_B T* P_n^⊥. The first term equals e_B L_{T tau^{1/2}}^* P_n and is of the desired e_B M form after bounded truncation; the second term is small in s_tau^r because tau(P_n^⊥) -> 0. Then approximate T strongly by bounded elements T_n in M e_B M via Kaplansky density; since T_n e_B = m_n e_B for some m_n in M and m_n tau^{1/2} -> T tau^{1/2}, the seminorm estimate gives e_B m_n^* -> e_B T*. If B is finite-dimensional, e_B has finite rank over B in the relevant sense, and strong convergence e_B m_n -> e_B T improves to norm convergence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.14: AFP of basic constructions lies in the boundary relative to the free factors under Condition (A)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M = M_1 *_B ... *_B M_n, with N_i=<M_i,e_B>. If every inclusion (B ⊂ M_i,E_i) satisfies Condition (A), then N_1 *_B ... *_B N_n is contained in S_{ {M_1,...,M_n} }(M), the small-at-infinity boundary of M relative to the component subalgebras M_i.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: It suffices to show that the commutator of a reduced word lambda_{i_1}(T_1)...lambda_{i_k}(T_k), with T_j in N_{i_j}^o and adjacent i_j distinct, with each generator of M' of the form J lambda_l(M_l) J belongs to K_{ {M_i} }^{∞,1}(M). For T in N_i and a in J lambda_j(M_j)J, the commutator [lambda_i(T),a] is zero if i=j and, if i≠j, is supported by the projection e_i onto L2 M_i: [lambda_i(T),a]=e_i[lambda_i(T),a]e_i. Expanding the commutator of the reduced word gives sums with a single such supported commutator. On the neighboring tensor legs one can replace lambda_i(T)e_{next} by lambda_i(T e_B)e_{next} and similarly on the right by e_B T. Thus each term is rewritten using T_r e_B or e_B T_r around a middle e_i B(L2 M)e_i piece. Condition (A) lets one approximate each T_r e_B or e_B T_r in the relevant one-sided C-M topology by M_i e_B or e_B M_i elements. Inductively replacing the basic-construction letters by algebra letters reduces to terms in M e_i B(L2 M)e_i M, which are generators of the boundary piece X_{ {M_i} }. Lemma 3.3 and the closure properties of K^{∞,1} then give the desired inclusion.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.15 / Corollary 1.2: relative biexactness of weakly exact tracial AFPs over injective amalgams
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M_i, i=1,...,n, be weakly exact von Neumann algebras with a common injective von Neumann subalgebra B and faithful normal conditional expectations E_i: M_i -> B. If every (B ⊂ M_i,E_i) satisfies Condition (A), then M = M_1 *_B ... *_B M_n is biexact relative to {M_1,...,M_n}. In particular, this holds if each M_i has a faithful tracial state tau_i with tau_i composed with E_i equal to tau_i; it also holds if B is finite-dimensional. In the introduction this is stated for two separable tracial weakly exact algebras with a common injective trace-preserving amalgam.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Corollary 4.10 gives an (M_r ⊂ M)-nuclear inclusion M_r -> N_1 *_B ... *_B N_n, where M_r is the reduced AFP C*-algebra and N_i=<M_i,e_B>. Proposition 4.14 places the target N_1 *_B ... *_B N_n inside the small-at-infinity boundary S_{ {M_i} }(M). Hence M_r -> S_{ {M_i} }(M) is (M_r ⊂ M)-nuclear. Since M_r is ultraweakly dense in M, DP23 Corollary 4.9 upgrades this to M-nuclearity of M -> S_{ {M_i} }(M), exactly relative biexactness. Lemma 4.12 and Remark 4.13 provide the two listed sufficient conditions for Condition (A).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.16: in the injective-input case the C*(M_i,e_B)-AFP lies in the boundary relative to B
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M = M_1 *_B ... *_B M_n and set N_{i,0}=C*(M_i,e_B) inside <M_i,e_B>. Then N_{1,0} *_B ... *_B N_{n,0} = C*(M_i,lambda_i(e_B): i=1,...,n) is contained in S_B(M), the small-at-infinity boundary relative to the amalgam B.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof uses a creation-preservation-annihilation normal form. The projection lambda_i(e_B) is the projection onto L2 B plus tensor words beginning with an M_i-letter, and sum_i lambda_i(e_B)^⊥ = 1-e_B. For x in M_i^o, decompose lambda_i(x) = lambda_i^+(x)+lambda_i^0(x)+lambda_i^-(x), where lambda_i^+(x)=lambda_i(x)lambda_i(e_B), lambda_i^0(x)=lambda_i(e_B)^⊥ lambda_i(x) lambda_i(e_B)^⊥, and lambda_i^-(x)=lambda_i(e_B)lambda_i(x). Creation and annihilation pieces are further decomposed using e_B and the complements lambda_j(e_B)^⊥. The relations lambda_i^-(x)lambda_j^+(y)=delta_{ij} E(xy)lambda_i(e_B) and lambda_i^0(x)lambda_j^+(y)=delta_{ij}lambda_j^+(xy-E(xy)), together with adjoints, reduce any word to a sum of three normal forms: creation... e_B b e_B ...annihilation, creation... lambda_l^0(z) ...annihilation, or creation... lambda_i(e_B)lambda_j(e_B) ...annihilation. Commutators with right generators J lambda_j(M_j)J either vanish or reduce to M[e_B,a']M. For instance [lambda_i(e_B)^⊥,a'] = -delta_{ij}[e_B,a'], and [lambda_l(e_B)^⊥lambda_l(z)lambda_l(e_B)^⊥,a'] is a sum of terms involving [lambda_l(e_B),a'], which equals [e_B,a'] for a' in J M_l J. These all lie in X_B ⊂ K_B^{∞,1}(M), proving membership in S_B(M).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 4.17 / Theorem 1.3: AFPs of injective algebras are biexact relative to the amalgam
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M_1,...,M_n be injective von Neumann algebras admitting a common unital von Neumann subalgebra B with faithful normal conditional expectations. Then M = M_1 *_B ... *_B M_n is biexact relative to B.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By the injective case of Corollary 4.10, the reduced AFP C*-algebra M_r embeds (M_r ⊂ M)-nuclearly into C*(M_i,lambda_i(e_B): i=1,...,n). Proposition 4.16 places this C*-algebra inside S_B(M). Thus M_r -> S_B(M) is (M_r ⊂ M)-nuclear. Since M_r is ultraweakly dense in M, DP23 Corollary 4.9 extends the nuclearity to the inclusion M -> S_B(M), which is precisely biexactness relative to B.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.18: relative commutant consequence for injective AFPs
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M_1,...,M_n be sigma-finite injective von Neumann algebras with a common unital von Neumann subalgebra B and normal conditional expectations, and let M = M_1 *_B ... *_B M_n. For every finite von Neumann subalgebra N ⊂ M with expectation, either N ⪯_M B or N' ∩ M is amenable. In particular, every separable finite subfactor N ⊂ M with expectation is either McDuff or prime.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The first assertion follows immediately from Theorem 4.17 and Proposition 3.10 applied to the singleton family {B}. Since B is an expected subalgebra of injective algebras, it is injective, so if a finite subalgebra has no amenable direct summand it cannot intertwine into B. For the prime/McDuff conclusion, if a separable finite factor N decomposes as N_1 tensor N_2 with both diffuse, then if one tensor component is amenable it is the hyperfinite II_1 factor and N is McDuff. If one component is nonamenable, the first assertion makes its relative commutant in M amenable, forcing the other tensor component amenable via the conditional expectation from M to N and then onto that component. Thus any non-prime such factor is McDuff.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemmas 5.1 and 5.3: support projections of boundary pieces in the bidual
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let N ⊂ M be a von Neumann subalgebra with expectation and e_N the Jones projection. In B(L2 M)** let iota be the canonical inclusion and let p_nor^# be the support projection enforcing normality for both M and JMJ; write iota^#(T)=p_nor^# iota(T)p_nor^#. If X_N is the hereditary boundary piece generated by M JMJ e_N B(L2 M)e_N M JMJ, then the unit q_{X_N} of X_N** is q_{X_N} = join_{u,v in U(M)} iota(u JvJ e_N Jv*J u*) and, after compression, q_{X_N} = join_{u,v in U(M)} iota^#(u JvJ e_N Jv*J u*). Similarly, q_{M e_N M} = join_{u in U(M)} iota(u e_N u*) and q_{J M J e_N J M J} = join_{u in U(M)} iota(JuJ e_N Ju*J), with compressed analogues. If F={N_i}_{i in I} is a family of expected subalgebras and X_F is the boundary piece generated by the e_{N_i}, then q_{X_F} = join_i q_{X_{N_i}} = join_{u,v in U(M), i in I} iota(u JvJ e_{N_i} Jv*J u*), and similarly after compression by p_nor^#.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The dense span of X_N consists of elements uJvJ e_N T e_N Jv'J u' with u,u',v,v' in U(M). The left and right support of such an element is dominated by the projection iota(uJvJ e_N Jv*J u*) or its analogue for the right side, hence the identity q_{X_N} is bounded above by the displayed join. Conversely each displayed conjugate of e_N belongs to the hereditary algebra generated by X_N, so the join is bounded by q_{X_N}. The formulas for M e_N M and JMJ e_N JMJ are the one-sided versions. For a family, the hereditary algebra generated by all X_{N_i} has open support projection equal to the join of the individual supports; the same dense-span argument gives the explicit join. Since iota(M), iota(JMJ), and iota(e_N) commute with p_nor^#, applying the normal compression Ad(p_nor^#) gives the compressed formulas.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 5.4, Lemma 5.5, Corollaries 5.6 and 5.7: mixing subalgebras and AFP inheritance
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For an M-boundary piece Y and an expected subalgebra N ⊂ M, N is called Y-mixing if e_N x J y J e_N belongs to K_Y^{∞,1}(M) whenever x,y in M and at least one of x,y lies in M ⊖ N. For Y=K(L2 M), this is called mixing; in the tracial case it agrees with the usual sequence definition. Lemma 5.5: if B is mixing in M_2, then M_1 is mixing in M_1 *_B M_2. Corollary 5.6: if B is mixing in both M_1 and M_2, then B is mixing in M_1 *_B M_2. Corollary 5.7: if B is mixing in M_i for all i=1,...,n, then B and every M_i are mixing subalgebras of M_1 *_B ... *_B M_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Lemma 5.5 it suffices, by M_1- and JM_1J-bimodularity, to show e_{M_1}(M⊖M_1)J(M⊖M_1)Je_{M_1} ⊂ K^{∞,1}(M). Check first on alternating centered words x_1...x_n and y_1...y_m. After removing exterior M_1 letters, assume the first and last letters are in M_2. Then e_{M_1} x_1...x_n J y_1...y_m J e_{M_1} equals e_B x_1...x_n J y_1...y_m J e_B and inductively collapses to zero unless the tensor patterns match; in the matching case it factors as e_B(x_1Jy_1J)e_B ... e_B(x_nJy_nJ)e_B. Since B is mixing in M_2, the end factors lie in K^{∞,1}(M_2), hence in K^{∞,1}(M); sandwiching any bounded middle operator between two such ideal elements remains in K^{∞,1}(M) using the bidual ideal description. Density of alternating words gives the result. Corollary 5.6 uses Lemma 5.5 to know M_1 is mixing in M and then reduces e_B M^o J M^o J e_B to e_B M_1^o J M_1^o J e_B plus compact-boundary terms, which are small by mixing of B in M_1. Corollary 5.7 follows by induction.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.12 with Lemmas 5.8-5.11: a basic-construction copy inside the bidual modulo a mixing boundary
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a von Neumann algebra, let N ⊂ M be an expected subalgebra, and let Y be an M-boundary piece. Assume N is Y-mixing and that the support projection q_Y commutes with iota^#(e_N). Let q = q_{J M J e_N J M J} in the compressed bidual. Then there is a normal M**-bimodular u.c.p. map Phi: <M,e_N>** -> (iota^#(M) q_{X_N} ∨ C q) q_Y^⊥ such that Phi(x)=x q_{X_N} q_Y^⊥ for every x in M**.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Lemma 5.10 is the mixing input: for x in M, q iota^#(x) q q_Y^⊥ = q iota^#(E_N(x)) q q_Y^⊥. For x in M⊖N, approximate q strongly by elements of JMJ e_N JMJ; sandwiching x by these approximants gives elements of K_Y^{∞,1}(M), which vanish after multiplying by q_Y^⊥ in the bidual. Lemma 5.11 defines on the algebraic span M e_N M a map phi_0(x e_N y)=iota^#(x) q iota^#(y) q_Y^⊥. The previous lemma gives the multiplication relations. Norm boundedness follows from the standard matrix norm formula ||sum x_k e_N y_k|| = ||[E_N(x_i^*x_j)]^{1/2}[E_N(y_i y_j^*)]^{1/2}|| in M_n(N), and the same formula after applying q q_Y^⊥. Thus phi_0 extends normally to (M e_N M)**. Lemma 5.8 says q_{M e_N M}<M,e_N>**q_{M e_N M}=(M e_N M)**, so define Phi as this extension composed with compression by q_{M e_N M}. Finally compute phi(q_{M e_N M}) = join_{u in U(M)} iota^#(u) q iota^#(u*) q_Y^⊥ = q_{X_N} q_Y^⊥ using the support-projection formulas of Lemma 5.1. This proves the stated formula and M**-bimodularity.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.14: relative proper proximality plus finite mixing family gives a central projection decomposition
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra, let F={N_1,...,N_n} be a finite family of mixing von Neumann subalgebras of M, and let p be a projection in M. If A ⊂ pMp is properly proximal relative to the compressed boundary piece X_F^A, then there exist projections f_0 in Z(A) and f_i in Z(A' ∩ pMp) for i=1,...,n such that sum_{i=0}^n f_i=p, A f_0 is properly proximal, and A f_i is amenable relative to N_i inside M for every i=1,...,n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Let f_0 be the maximal central projection in Z(A) such that A f_0 is properly proximal. On the complement, A has no properly proximal direct summand and, because relative proper proximality excludes amenable summands, no amenable direct summand. Hence there is an A-central state mu on the bidual ordinary boundary tilde S(A) normal on A and faithful on the center. Compressing B(L2 M) to B(L2 A) by Ad(e_A)Ad(pJpJ) gives a bidual u.c.p. map tilde E that sends tilde S(M) into tilde S(A) and sends the relative boundary tilde S_{X_F}(M) into tilde S_{X_F^A}(A). Put phi=mu composed with tilde E. Then phi is A-central and normal/faithful on A. Let q_K be the compact-boundary support and q_{X_F} the relative-boundary support in the bidual. Nonamenability gives phi(q_K^⊥)=1. If phi(q_{X_F}^⊥)>0, then normalizing phi after compression by q_{X_F}^⊥ gives an A-central state on the relative boundary of A, contradicting relative proper proximality; hence phi(q_{X_F})=1. Since F is finite, q_{X_F}=join_i q_{X_{N_i}}, so some components have positive phi(q_K^⊥ q_{X_{N_i}}). For each such i, Theorem 5.12 with Y=K(L2 M) gives an M-bimodular u.c.p. map from <M,e_{N_i}> to q_K^⊥ tilde S(M) sending x to x q_{X_{N_i}} q_K^⊥. Composing and normalizing yields an A-central state on <M,e_{N_i}> normal on M, which is exactly relative amenability of a central summand. Taking support projections f_i in Z(A'∩pMp) and using phi(q_{X_{N_i}} f_i^⊥)=0 shows these f_i cover the complement of f_0; orthogonalizing gives the stated decomposition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.15: variant of the decomposition with a prescribed boundary Y
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra, p a projection, Y an M-boundary piece, and F={N_1,...,N_n} a finite family of Y-mixing subalgebras such that q_Y commutes with iota^#(e_{N_i}) for all i. Let A ⊂ pMp be properly proximal relative to X_F inside M and assume A has no amenable direct summand. If there exists an A-central state phi on tilde S_Y(M) whose restriction to pMp is faithful normal, then there are projections f_i in Z(A'∩pMp), i=1,...,n, with sum_i f_i=p and A f_i amenable relative to N_i inside M.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof is the same central-state localization argument as Theorem 5.14, but starts with a central state on the Y-boundary rather than obtaining it from non-proper-proximality of A. Because the N_i are Y-mixing and q_Y commutes with the e_{N_i} support projections, Theorem 5.12 applies with this Y. One compresses phi by the support projections q_{X_{N_i}} q_Y^⊥ and composes with the resulting u.c.p. maps from <M,e_{N_i}>. The finite join formula for q_{X_F} forces the resulting relative-amenability support projections to cover p.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.16 / Theorem 1.4: subalgebra decomposition for relatively biexact algebras with mixing boundary family
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M be a separable tracial von Neumann algebra and let F={N_1,...,N_n} be a finite family of mixing von Neumann subalgebras of M. Let p be a projection in M. If M is biexact relative to X_F, then for every von Neumann subalgebra A ⊂ pMp there exist projections f_0 and f_{n+1} in Z(A), and projections f_i in Z(A'∩pMp) for i=1,...,n, such that sum_{i=0}^{n+1} f_i=p, A f_0 is properly proximal, A f_{n+1} is amenable, and A f_i is amenable relative to N_i inside M for every i=1,...,n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Relative biexactness passes to subalgebras by DP23 Proposition 6.10: A is biexact relative to the compressed boundary X_F^A. DP23 Lemma 6.11 then gives a central projection f_{n+1} in Z(A) such that A f_{n+1} is amenable and A(1-f_{n+1}) is properly proximal relative to X_F^A. Applying Theorem 5.14 to the nonamenable relatively properly proximal complement yields a maximal properly proximal summand f_0 and relative-amenability summands f_i for the mixing subalgebras N_i. Combining these projections gives the required partition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 5.17 / Corollary 1.5: absorption for tracial weakly exact AFPs with mixing amenable amalgam
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (M_1,tau_1) and (M_2,tau_2) be separable tracial weakly exact von Neumann algebras with a common amenable subalgebra B such that tau_1 restricted to B equals tau_2 restricted to B, and let M=M_1 *_B M_2. Assume B is mixing in M_i for i=1,2. If A ⊂ M is a von Neumann subalgebra with no properly proximal direct summand and A ∩ M_1 ⪯̸_{M_1} B, then A ⊂ M_1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Corollary 1.2, M is biexact relative to {M_1,M_2}. By the mixing inheritance results, M_1 and M_2 are mixing subalgebras of M. Applying Theorem 5.16 to A and using that A has no properly proximal direct summand gives central projections f_1,f_2 in Z(A'∩M), with f_1+f_2=1 after absorbing any amenable piece, such that A f_i is amenable relative to M_i. Suppose A f_2 were not amenable relative to B. Ioana's AFP intertwining result implies A f_2 ⪯_M M_2. Since A∩M_1 ⪯̸_{M_1} B, Popa's criterion gives unitaries u_n in A∩M_1 with E_B(xu_n y)->0 in L2 for all x,y in M_1. Mixing of M_2 inside M then yields E_{M_2}(x u_n f_2 y)->0 for all x,y in M, contradicting A f_2 ⪯_M M_2. Thus A f_2 is amenable relative to B, so A is amenable relative to M_1. The main theorem of Boutonnet-Houdayer on amenable absorption in AFPs, together with A∩M_1 ⪯̸ B, gives A ⊂ M_1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 5.20, Theorem 5.21, Corollary 5.22, and Corollary 5.23: upgrading relative biexactness to genuine biexactness
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Theorem 5.21 gives a bidual criterion: for a separable von Neumann algebra M with faithful normal state and an M-boundary piece X, M is biexact relative to X iff M is weakly exact and the inclusion iota^#|_M: M -> tilde S_X(M) is weak*-nuclear; it is also equivalent to an approximation condition by u.c.p. maps on B(L2 M) modulo K_X^{∞,1}(M). Lemma 5.20 says that if N ⊂ M is a mixing subalgebra with expectation, M is biexact relative to N, and the embedding M -> <M,e_N> is M-nuclear, then iota^#|_M: M -> tilde S(M) is weak*-nuclear. Corollary 5.22 concludes that, under the same hypotheses and with M separable, M is genuinely biexact. Corollary 5.23 applies this to AFPs: if M_1,...,M_n are separable injective von Neumann algebras with a common subalgebra B with expectation, and B is mixing in each M_i, then M=M_1 *_B ... *_B M_n is biexact.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For Theorem 5.21, the implication from relative biexactness to weak exactness plus weak*-nuclearity is immediate by passing to the compressed bidual. Conversely, given weak*-nuclear approximations M -> matrices -> tilde S_X(M), use Arveson's extension theorem to extend the first maps to B(L2 M). Since iota^#(B(L2 M)) is strongly dense in the compressed bidual, approximate the second maps by u.c.p. maps into iota^#(B(L2 M)), then pull back through iota^#. Commutators with JMJ land in K_X(M)^#*; after taking convex combinations, they become small in the r_mu seminorm modulo K_X^{∞,1}(M), giving DP23's approximation criterion for relative biexactness. For Lemma 5.20, relative biexactness gives weak*-nuclearity of M into tilde S_{X_N}(M). Compressing by q_K and by q_{X_N}^⊥ gives two weak*-nuclear pieces already landing in the ordinary boundary tilde S(M). Theorem 5.12 with Y=K(L2 M) gives a normal u.c.p. map from the bidual of <M,e_N> into tilde S(M)q_K^⊥ sending x to x q_{X_N}q_K^⊥; composing this with the assumed M-nuclear embedding M -> <M,e_N> gives weak*-nuclearity of the missing component Ad(q_{X_N}q_K^⊥)iota^#. Since iota^#|_M is the sum of the q_K part, the q_{X_N}^⊥ part, and this missing part, it is weak*-nuclear into tilde S(M). Theorem 5.21 then gives genuine biexactness. For Corollary 5.23, Theorem 4.17 supplies biexactness relative to B, Corollary 5.7 supplies mixing of B in the AFP, and the required nuclearity of M -> <M,e_B> follows from weak exactness plus injectivity of the expected amalgam via Lemma 3.1.

proof_techniques.name: Toeplitz-Pimsner factorization of AFP inclusions
proof_techniques.description: The core nuclearity proof replaces an unavailable free product of B-linear u.c.p. approximations by a Toeplitz-Pimsner algebra. For two AFP factors, the authors build a correspondence over A_1 ⊕ A_2 from the u.c.p. map that swaps the conditional expectations to B, represent it by a creation operator T on a Fock-type Hilbert space K, and use the Toeplitz-Pimsner universal property to produce a weakly nuclear representation when the component embeddings A_i -> N_{i,0} are nuclear. A carefully chosen u.c.p. map Psi from the reduced AFP into the Toeplitz algebra, together with a compression Phi back to the AFP of basic constructions, recovers the canonical inclusion on generators and hence on the whole AFP.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Graph-product von Neumann algebras often decompose as AFPs over link or star subalgebras. Even if vertex algebras are not weakly exact, the Toeplitz-Pimsner model suggests a way to build or test boundary maps for such AFP decompositions without requiring literal free products of u.c.p. maps.

proof_techniques.name: Compression by first-last tensor-word sectors
proof_techniques.description: The map Phi: B(K) -> B(L2 M) is built by decomposing the auxiliary Fock space K into subspaces K_{i,j} according to the first and last tensor letters, then summing V_{i,j} x V_{k,l}^* over natural identifications V_{i,j}: K_{i,j} -> L2 M. The resulting compression has explicit values on T, T^2(T*)^2, and N_i-letters, allowing multiplicative-domain arguments to show that Phi composed with the Toeplitz representation recovers the AFP inclusion.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For graph products, reduced words have combinatorial first/last syllable data. An analogous sector compression may help construct boundary maps or central-state contradictions based on word supports.

proof_techniques.name: Commutator localization into boundary ideals
proof_techniques.description: To prove an algebra lies inside a small-at-infinity boundary S_X(M), the paper expands commutators with right-action generators and shows each term is supported on projections associated with the chosen boundary subalgebras. In Proposition 4.14, reduced words in basic construction letters have commutators supported inside e_i B(L2 M)e_i after replacing neighboring letters by T e_B or e_B T. Approximation in the C-M topology then pushes these terms into the hereditary boundary piece generated by the component subalgebras.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A graph-product proof of proper proximality may need to define a boundary from link/star subalgebras and show graph-product word operators have commutators small relative to it. This localization method is directly aligned with that goal.

proof_techniques.name: Condition (A) tracial approximation of basic construction letters
proof_techniques.description: Condition (A) is a one-sided approximation property saying e_B <M,e_B> lies in the C-M closure of e_B M. In tracial inclusions it follows by truncating the affiliated operator T tau^{1/2} with spectral projections and using Kaplansky density in M e_B M. This lets arbitrary basic-construction letters be replaced, in boundary-topology arguments, by genuine algebra letters.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Graph-product von Neumann algebras in the original problem are tracial. If link inclusions satisfy analogous trace-preserving expectations, this approximation can help convert basic-construction or projection terms into graph-product algebraic words.

proof_techniques.name: Creation-preservation-annihilation normal forms around the Jones projection
proof_techniques.description: For C*(M_i,e_B) inside an AFP, each centered letter x in M_i is decomposed relative to lambda_i(e_B) into creation, preservation, and annihilation pieces. Simple relations among these pieces reduce arbitrary words to a few normal forms where commutators with the right action visibly contain [e_B,a]. This proves containment in the boundary relative to B without Condition (A).
