proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Graph products have syllable creation/annihilation behavior relative to link subalgebras. A similar normal form may isolate the obstruction to commuting with the opposite action in terms of link projections.

proof_techniques.name: Bidual support projections for hereditary boundary pieces
proof_techniques.description: The paper systematically uses the unit q_X of X** inside the compressed bidual B(L2 M)_J^#*. For boundary pieces generated by expected subalgebras N_i, q_X is the join of all M- and JMJ-conjugates of the Jones projections e_{N_i}. This converts statements about hereditary C*-algebras and closures into projection calculus in a von Neumann algebra.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Proper proximality is often proved by excluding central states on a boundary. The q_X calculus gives a precise way to localize such a state to individual link/star boundary pieces in a finite graph.

proof_techniques.name: Mixing inheritance through AFPs
proof_techniques.description: The paper proves that if B is mixing in one AFP component, then the other component is mixing in the full AFP, and if B is mixing in all components then B and all components are mixing in the full AFP. The proof checks alternating reduced words and collapses e_{M_1} word J word e_{M_1} to products of e_B(xJyJ)e_B terms, where mixing of B supplies compact-boundary smallness.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Trace-zero unitaries in graph-product vertex algebras often provide orthogonality/mixing phenomena for link inclusions. This lemma indicates how such mixing can propagate after decomposing a graph product as an AFP.

proof_techniques.name: New basic construction modulo a mixing boundary
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 5.12 constructs a normal M**-bimodular u.c.p. map from <M,e_N>** into a bidual corner of the boundary generated by N, modulo another boundary Y, whenever N is Y-mixing. The key identity q x q = q E_N(x) q modulo q_Y comes from mixing. This turns relative amenability states on <M,e_N> into pieces of central states on boundaries and vice versa.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a graph-product boundary is built from link subalgebras and these links are mixing, this mechanism can force any hypothetical non-proper-proximal central state to concentrate on relative amenability to links, potentially contradicting irreducibility.

proof_techniques.name: Central-state partition via finite boundary joins
proof_techniques.description: Theorem 5.14 and Theorem 5.16 use the identity q_{X_F}=join_i q_{X_{N_i}} for a finite family to decompose an A-central state into finitely many relative-amenability supports. The maximal properly proximal direct summand is separated first; on the complement, a central state on the ordinary boundary is forced to have full mass on the relative boundary, and finite projection joins yield central support projections f_i.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original graph is finite. A proof by contradiction to proper proximality could try to use finite joins over links or vertex stars to derive a finite central decomposition, then use irreducibility to rule out all relative-amenable alternatives.

proof_techniques.name: Upgrading relative biexactness to genuine biexactness under mixing
proof_techniques.description: If M is biexact relative to a mixing subalgebra N and M -> <M,e_N> is M-nuclear, Lemma 5.20 splits iota^#|_M into three bidual pieces: the compact part, the part outside q_{X_N}, and the missing q_{X_N}q_K^⊥ part. The first two come from relative biexactness; the third comes from the basic-construction map of Theorem 5.12 and nuclearity into <M,e_N>. The bidual criterion of Theorem 5.21 then gives genuine biexactness.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If some graph-product decomposition gives relative biexactness or an analogous relative boundary property over a mixing link, this upgrade suggests a route to ordinary biexactness, which for nonamenable factors implies proper proximality by known results cited in the paper.

proof_techniques.name: Relative solidity/intertwining from relative biexactness
proof_techniques.description: Proposition 3.10 combines relative biexactness with Popa's intertwining criterion. If a finite subalgebra N does not intertwine into any boundary subalgebra B_j, a net of unitaries in N kills the boundary ideal; a cluster point of the conjugation maps sends the small-at-infinity boundary back into M and fixes N'∩M. Nuclearity then forces N'∩M amenable.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a template for using failure of intertwining into link/star subalgebras to produce amenability or contradiction. It may be useful if graph-product irreducibility can rule out the intertwining alternatives.

proof_techniques.name: Amenable absorption in AFPs after relative-amenability decomposition
proof_techniques.description: Corollary 5.17 shows how Theorem 5.16 can be combined with Ioana's AFP intertwining and Boutonnet-Houdayer amenable absorption. Once a subalgebra with no properly proximal summand is decomposed into pieces amenable relative to AFP components, a non-intertwining assumption and mixing force one piece to be amenable relative to the amalgam, after which absorption places the whole subalgebra inside a component.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A graph product can be peeled by AFP decompositions over links. If non-proper-proximality leads to relative amenability to components, an absorption step may force containment in a proper graph-product subalgebra, enabling induction or contradiction with irreducibility.

other_useful_info: The paper itself does not treat graph products or trace-zero unitaries directly; its conclusions on relative biexactness require weak exactness/injectivity hypotheses that are not present in the original problem, so the directly transferable content is mainly the AFP boundary technology, mixing propagation, and central-state decomposition/upgrading machinery. The introduction notes the known implication that every nonamenable biexact von Neumann factor is properly proximal; thus any independent route to biexactness plus nonamenability/factoriality would imply proper proximality. The paper cites DP23 for the von Neumann algebraic definition of relative biexactness, DEP23 for properly proximal von Neumann algebras and bidual boundary criteria, DKE24 for relative-biexact decomposition methods, BH18 for amenable absorption in AFPs, Ioa15 for AFP intertwining tools, and CFM13/Oza10 for mixing-subalgebra characterizations. The finiteness of the boundary family is used materially in Theorems 5.14 and 5.16 through the finite join formula q_{X_F}=join_i q_{X_{N_i}}.
why_relevant: Graph-product von Neumann algebras admit natural decompositions as amalgamated free products over link subalgebras. This paper supplies recent relative biexactness and Toeplitz-Pimsner techniques for amalgamated free products, which may be useful for a relative-boundary approach to the problem.
